#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to prevent empty password authentication on RHEL 8
# This script removes the 'nullok' option from PAM configuration files

set -e

# List of PAM configuration files that commonly contain nullok
PAM_FILES=(
    "/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
    "/etc/pam.d/password-auth"
)

# Function to remove nullok from a file
remove_nullok() {
    local file="$1"
    
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        # Check if nullok exists in the file
        if grep -q '\bnullok\b' "$file"; then
            # Create backup before modification
            cp -p "$file" "${file}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
            
            # Remove nullok option (handles both 'nullok' and 'nullok_secure')
            # Using sed to remove nullok while preserving the rest of the line
            sed -i 's/\s\+nullok\b//g; s/\bnullok\s\+//g; s/\bnullok$//g' "$file"
            
            echo "Removed nullok from $file"
        else
            echo "nullok not found in $file - no changes needed"
        fi
    else
        echo "Warning: $file does not exist"
    fi
}

# Process each PAM file
for pam_file in "${PAM_FILES[@]}"; do
    remove_nullok "$pam_file"
done

# Also check for authselect managed systems (RHEL 8 default)
# If authselect is in use, we need to modify the custom profile or use authselect commands

if command -v authselect &> /dev/null; then
    # Check current profile
    current_profile=$(authselect current -r 2>/dev/null || echo "")
    
    if [[ -n "$current_profile" ]]; then
        echo "System is using authselect with profile: $current_profile"
        
        # Check if without-nullok feature is available and enable it
        if authselect list-features "$current_profile" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "without-nullok"; then
            # Enable the without-nullok feature
            if ! authselect current 2>/dev/null | grep -q "without-nullok"; then
                authselect enable-feature without-nullok 2>/dev/null || true
                echo "Enabled without-nullok feature in authselect"
            else
                echo "without-nullok feature already enabled"
            fi
        fi
        
        # For custom profiles, check the custom directory
        if [[ "$current_profile" == custom/* ]]; then
            custom_name="${current_profile#custom/}"
            custom_dir="/etc/authselect/custom/$custom_name"
            
            if [[ -d "$custom_dir" ]]; then
                for template_file in "$custom_dir"/system-auth "$custom_dir"/password-auth; do
                    if [[ -f "$template_file" ]] && grep -q '\bnullok\b' "$template_file"; then
                        cp -p "$template_file" "${template_file}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
                        sed -i 's/\s\+nullok\b//g; s/\bnullok\s\+//g; s/\bnullok$//g' "$template_file"
                        echo "Removed nullok from $template_file"
                    fi
                done
                # Apply the changes
                authselect apply-changes 2>/dev/null || true
            fi
        fi
    fi
fi

# Verify the changes
echo ""
echo "Verification - checking for remaining nullok entries:"
nullok_found=false

for pam_file in "${PAM_FILES[@]}"; do
    if [[ -f "$pam_file" ]] && grep -q '\bnullok\b' "$pam_file"; then
        echo "WARNING: nullok still present in $pam_file"
        nullok_found=true
    fi
done

if [[ "$nullok_found" == "false" ]]; then
    echo "SUCCESS: No nullok entries found in PAM configuration files"
    echo "Empty password authentication has been disabled"
else
    echo "WARNING: Some nullok entries may still be present"
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

mkdir -p "${DCONF_LOCKS_DIR}"

# The profile should list system-db before user-db for system settings to take precedence

idle-activation-enabled=true

chmod 644 "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"
chmod 644 "${DCONF_LOCKS_FILE}"

dconf update

echo "  - Screen lock enabled: true"
echo "  - Lock delay: 0 seconds (immediate)"
echo "  - Idle activation enabled: true"
echo "  - Idle delay: 900 seconds (15 minutes)"
echo ""
echo "These settings are now enforced system-wide and cannot be changed by users."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to disable kexec kernel image loading on RHEL 8
# This prevents loading a replacement kernel image into memory

SYSCTL_PARAM="kernel.kexec_load_disabled"
DESIRED_VALUE="1"
SYSCTL_CONF_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-kexec-disable.conf"

# Function to check current runtime value
get_runtime_value() {
    sysctl -n "${SYSCTL_PARAM}" 2>/dev/null
}

# Function to set runtime value
set_runtime_value() {
    sysctl -w "${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${DESIRED_VALUE}" >/dev/null 2>&1
}

# Function to check if persistence is configured correctly
check_persistence() {
    if [[ -f "${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE}" ]]; then
        grep -q "^${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*${DESIRED_VALUE}$" "${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE}" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to configure persistence
configure_persistence() {
    echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}" > "${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE}"
    chmod 644 "${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE}"
}

# Main execution

# Ensure persistence configuration exists
if ! check_persistence; then
    configure_persistence
    echo "Configured ${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE} for persistence"
else
    echo "Persistence already configured in ${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE}"
fi

# Check and set runtime value if not already set
current_value=$(get_runtime_value)

if [[ "${current_value}" == "${DESIRED_VALUE}" ]]; then
    echo "Runtime value already set: ${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}"
else
    set_runtime_value
    new_value=$(get_runtime_value)
    if [[ "${new_value}" == "${DESIRED_VALUE}" ]]; then
        echo "Runtime value set: ${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not set runtime value. Current value: ${new_value}"
        exit 1
    fi
fi

echo "kexec kernel loading has been disabled"
exit 0

    exit 1
fi

if ! grep -q "Red Hat Enterprise Linux.*8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Warning: This script is designed for RHEL 8"

if ! rpm -q fapolicyd &>/dev/null; then

    echo "fapolicyd installed successfully"
else
    echo "fapolicyd is already installed"
fi

FAPOLICYD_RULES="/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.rules"
FAPOLICYD_TRUST="/etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.trust"

if [[ -f "$FAPOLICYD_CONF" && ! -f "${FAPOLICYD_CONF}.orig" ]]; then
    cp "$FAPOLICYD_CONF" "${FAPOLICYD_CONF}.orig"
    echo "Backed up original fapolicyd.conf"
fi

if [[ -f "$FAPOLICYD_RULES" && ! -f "${FAPOLICYD_RULES}.orig" ]]; then
    cp "$FAPOLICYD_RULES" "${FAPOLICYD_RULES}.orig"
    echo "Backed up original fapolicyd.rules"
fi

echo "Configuring fapolicyd for enforcing deny-all mode..."

# Configure fapolicyd.conf for enforcing mode with deny-all default
# Set permissive = 0 for enforcing mode
if grep -q "^permissive" "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"; then
    sed -i 's/^permissive.*/permissive = 0/' "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"
else
    echo "permissive = 0" >> "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"
fi

# Enable integrity checking (optional but recommended for security)
if grep -q "^integrity" "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"; then
    sed -i 's/^integrity.*/integrity = sha256/' "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"
else
    echo "integrity = sha256" >> "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"
fi

# Set trust source to include RPM database and local trust file
if grep -q "^trust" "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"; then
    sed -i 's/^trust.*/trust = rpmdb,file/' "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"
else
    echo "trust = rpmdb,file" >> "$FAPOLICYD_CONF"
fi

# Ensure rules file exists and has proper deny-all configuration
# The default rules should allow trusted executables and deny everything else
# We'll ensure the deny-all rule is at the end

allow perm=any all : ftype=application/x-sharedlib trust=1
allow perm=any all : ftype=application/x-pie-executable trust=1

allow perm=any all : ftype=text/x-python trust=1

EOF

# Allow trusted executables
allow perm=any all : ftype=application/x-executable trust=1
allow perm=any all : ftype=application/x-sharedlib trust=1
allow perm=any all : ftype=application/x-pie-executable trust=1

# Allow trusted scripts
allow perm=any all : ftype=text/x-python trust=1
allow perm=any all : ftype=text/x-shellscript trust=1
allow perm=any all : ftype=text/x-perl trust=1

# Allow ld.so to execute
allow perm=open all : path=/lib64/ld-linux-x86-64.so.2
allow perm=open all : path=/lib/ld-linux.so.2

# Deny all other executions (default deny)
deny all all
EOF
        echo "Configured fapolicyd.rules with deny-all policy"
    fi
fi

# Initialize/update the trust database
echo "Updating fapolicyd trust database..."
fapolicyd-cli --update 2>/dev/null || true

# Enable and start fapolicyd service
echo "Enabling fapolicyd service..."
systemctl enable fapolicyd

# Check if service is already running and restart if needed
if systemctl is-active --quiet fapolicyd; then
    echo "Restarting fapolicyd service to apply changes..."
    systemctl restart fapolicyd
else
    echo "Starting fapolicyd service..."
    systemctl start fapolicyd
fi

# Verify the service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet fapolicyd; then
    echo "fapolicyd service is active and running"
else
    echo "Warning: fapolicyd service failed to start"
    echo "Check logs with: journalctl -u fapolicyd"
    exit 1
fi

# Display current status
echo ""
echo "=== fapolicyd Configuration Summary ==="
echo "Service Status: $(systemctl is-active fapolicyd)"
echo "Enabled at Boot: $(systemctl is-enabled fapolicyd)"
echo ""
echo "Configuration:"
grep -E "^(permissive|integrity|trust)" "$FAPOLICYD_CONF" 2>/dev/null || true
echo ""
echo "Policy Mode: Enforcing (deny-all, permit-by-exception)"
echo ""
echo "To add trusted applications, use:"
echo "  fapolicyd-cli --file add /path/to/trusted/binary"
echo "  fapolicyd-cli --update"
echo ""
echo "To check if a file would be allowed:"
echo "  fapolicyd-cli --check-path /path/to/binary"
echo ""
echo "Application allowlisting has been configured successfully."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure sudo credential-caching timeout
# This ensures sudo requires re-authentication after a finite period

set -e

# Define the timeout value in minutes (5 minutes is a common secure default)
TIMEOUT_VALUE=5

# Define the sudoers drop-in directory and file
SUDOERS_DIR="/etc/sudoers.d"
SUDOERS_FILE="${SUDOERS_DIR}/00-timeout"

# Ensure the sudoers.d directory exists
if [ ! -d "$SUDOERS_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$SUDOERS_DIR"
    chmod 750 "$SUDOERS_DIR"
fi

# Define the configuration line
CONFIG_LINE="Defaults timestamp_timeout=${TIMEOUT_VALUE}"

# Function to check if the configuration is already correctly set
check_config() {
    if [ -f "$SUDOERS_FILE" ]; then
        if grep -q "^Defaults[[:space:]]*timestamp_timeout=${TIMEOUT_VALUE}$" "$SUDOERS_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to validate sudoers syntax
validate_sudoers() {
    local file="$1"
    if visudo -c -f "$file" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        return 0
    else
        return 1
    fi
}

# Check if configuration already exists and is correct
if check_config; then
    echo "Sudo timeout is already configured correctly (${TIMEOUT_VALUE} minutes)"
    exit 0
fi

# Create a temporary file for the new configuration
TEMP_FILE=$(mktemp)

# Write the configuration to the temporary file
cat > "$TEMP_FILE" << EOF
# Sudo credential caching timeout configuration
# Authentication credentials expire after ${TIMEOUT_VALUE} minutes
# This ensures users must re-authenticate after the timeout period
${CONFIG_LINE}
EOF

# Validate the syntax of the temporary file
if ! validate_sudoers "$TEMP_FILE"; then
    echo "ERROR: Generated sudoers configuration has invalid syntax"
    rm -f "$TEMP_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Remove any existing timestamp_timeout settings from other sudoers files
# to prevent conflicts (check main sudoers file)
if grep -q "^[^#]*timestamp_timeout" /etc/sudoers 2>/dev/null; then
    # Create backup and remove the setting from main sudoers
    cp /etc/sudoers /etc/sudoers.bak
    sed -i '/^[^#]*timestamp_timeout/d' /etc/sudoers
    if ! visudo -c >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        # Restore backup if validation fails
        mv /etc/sudoers.bak /etc/sudoers
        echo "WARNING: Could not safely modify /etc/sudoers, continuing with drop-in file"
    else
        rm -f /etc/sudoers.bak
        echo "Removed existing timestamp_timeout from /etc/sudoers"
    fi
fi

# Check for conflicting settings in other drop-in files
for f in ${SUDOERS_DIR}/*; do
    if [ -f "$f" ] && [ "$f" != "$SUDOERS_FILE" ]; then
        if grep -q "timestamp_timeout" "$f" 2>/dev/null; then
            # Comment out conflicting lines
            sed -i 's/^\([^#]*timestamp_timeout\)/#\1/' "$f"
            echo "Commented out conflicting timestamp_timeout in $f"
        fi
    fi
done

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure sudo credential-caching timeout
# This ensures sudo requires re-authentication after a finite period

set -e

# Define the timeout value in minutes (5 minutes is a common secure default)
TIMEOUT_VALUE=5

# Define the sudoers drop-in directory and file
SUDOERS_DIR="/etc/sudoers.d"
SUDOERS_FILE="${SUDOERS_DIR}/00-timeout"

# Ensure the sudoers.d directory exists
if [ ! -d "$SUDOERS_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$SUDOERS_DIR"
    chmod 750 "$SUDOERS_DIR"
fi

# Define the configuration line
CONFIG_LINE="Defaults timestamp_timeout=${TIMEOUT_VALUE}"

# Function to check if the configuration is already correctly set
check_config() {
    if [ -f "$SUDOERS_FILE" ]; then
        if grep -q "^Defaults[[:space:]]*timestamp_timeout=${TIMEOUT_VALUE}$" "$SUDOERS_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to validate sudoers syntax
validate_sudoers() {
    local file="$1"
    if visudo -c -f "$file" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        return 0
    else
        return 1
    fi
}

# Check if configuration already exists and is correct
if check_config; then
    echo "Sudo timeout is already configured correctly (${TIMEOUT_VALUE} minutes)"
    exit 0
fi

# Create a temporary file for the new configuration
TEMP_FILE=$(mktemp)

# Write the configuration to the temporary file
cat > "$TEMP_FILE" << EOF
# Sudo credential caching timeout configuration
# Authentication credentials expire after ${TIMEOUT_VALUE} minutes
# This ensures users must re-authenticate after the timeout period
${CONFIG_LINE}
EOF

# Validate the syntax of the temporary file
if ! validate_sudoers "$TEMP_FILE"; then
    echo "ERROR: Generated sudoers configuration has invalid syntax"
    rm -f "$TEMP_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Remove any existing timestamp_timeout settings from other sudoers files
# to prevent conflicts (check main sudoers file)
if grep -q "^[^#]*timestamp_timeout" /etc/sudoers 2>/dev/null; then
    # Create backup and remove the setting from main sudoers
    cp /etc/sudoers /etc/sudoers.bak
    sed -i '/^[^#]*timestamp_timeout/d' /etc/sudoers
    if ! visudo -c >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        # Restore backup if validation fails
        mv /etc/sudoers.bak /etc/sudoers
        echo "WARNING: Could not safely modify /etc/sudoers, continuing with drop-in file"
    else
        rm -f /etc/sudoers.bak
        echo "Removed existing timestamp_timeout from /etc/sudoers"
    fi
fi

# Check for conflicting settings in other drop-in files
for f in ${SUDOERS_DIR}/*; do
    if [ -f "$f" ] && [ "$f" != "$SUDOERS_FILE" ]; then
        if grep -q "timestamp_timeout" "$f" 2>/dev/null; then
            # Comment out conflicting lines
            sed -i 's/^\([^#]*timestamp_timeout\)/#\1/' "$f"
            echo "Commented out conflicting timestamp_timeout in $f"
        fi
    fi
done

# Move the temporary file to the final location
mv "$TEMP_FILE" "$SUDOERS_FILE"

# Set proper permissions
chmod 440 "$SUDOERS_FILE"
chown root:root "$SUDOERS_FILE"

# Final validation
if ! visudo -c >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "ERROR: Final sudoers validation failed, removing configuration"
    rm -f "$SUDOERS_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

echo "Successfully configured sudo timeout to ${TIMEOUT_VALUE} minutes"
echo "Configuration file: ${SUDOERS_FILE}"

# Verify the setting is active
if sudo -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "timestamp_timeout" || [ -f "$SUDOERS_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Sudo credential caching will expire after ${TIMEOUT_VALUE} minutes"
fi

exit 0
```

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to disable IEEE 1394 (FireWire) kernel module on RHEL 8
# This prevents the firewire-core module from loading and being activated on demand

set -euo pipefail

MODPROBE_CONF="/etc/modprobe.d/firewire.conf"
MODULES_LOAD_CONF="/etc/modules-load.d/firewire.conf"

# Create or update modprobe configuration to blacklist and disable firewire-core
# Using install directive with /bin/false ensures the module cannot be loaded
cat > "$MODPROBE_CONF" << 'EOF'
# Disable IEEE 1394 (FireWire) to reduce attack surface
# This prevents DMA attacks and potential implementation flaws
install firewire-core /bin/false
blacklist firewire-core
blacklist firewire-ohci
blacklist firewire-sbp2
blacklist firewire-net
EOF

# Ensure proper permissions on the configuration file
chmod 644 "$MODPROBE_CONF"

# Create modules-load.d entry to ensure modules are not loaded at boot
# This is a belt-and-suspenders approach
cat > "$MODULES_LOAD_CONF" << 'EOF'
# Ensure FireWire modules are not loaded at boot
# Empty file to override any other configuration
EOF

chmod 644 "$MODULES_LOAD_CONF"

# If the module is currently loaded, unload it
# This makes the change effective immediately without requiring a reboot
if lsmod | grep -q "^firewire_core"; then
    # First try to unload dependent modules
    for mod in firewire_sbp2 firewire_net firewire_ohci; do
        if lsmod | grep -q "^${mod//-/_}"; then
            modprobe -r "${mod}" 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    done
    # Then unload the core module
    modprobe -r firewire-core 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Rebuild the module dependency database to ensure changes take effect
depmod -a

echo "FireWire (IEEE 1394) support has been disabled."
echo "Configuration written to: $MODPROBE_CONF"
echo "The firewire-core module and related modules are now blacklisted and cannot be loaded."

GDM_CONF="/etc/gdm/custom.conf"

    if grep -q "^AutomaticLoginEnable" "$GDM_CONF"; then
        # Update existing AutomaticLoginEnable setting
        sed -i 's/^AutomaticLoginEnable=.*/AutomaticLoginEnable=false/' "$GDM_CONF"
    else
        # Add AutomaticLoginEnable=false after [daemon] section
        sed -i '/^\[daemon\]/a AutomaticLoginEnable=false' "$GDM_CONF"

        sed -i '/^AutomaticLogin=/d' "$GDM_CONF"

    # Add [daemon] section with AutomaticLoginEnable=false at the beginning
    sed -i '1i [daemon]\nAutomaticLoginEnable=false\n' "$GDM_CONF"

if grep -q "^TimedLoginEnable" "$GDM_CONF"; then
    sed -i 's/^TimedLoginEnable=.*/TimedLoginEnable=false/' "$GDM_CONF"
elif grep -q "^\[daemon\]" "$GDM_CONF"; then
    # Add TimedLoginEnable=false after AutomaticLoginEnable
    if ! grep -q "^TimedLoginEnable" "$GDM_CONF"; then

    fi
fi

# Remove TimedLogin user if present
if grep -q "^TimedLogin=" "$GDM_CONF"; then
    sed -i '/^TimedLogin=/d' "$GDM_CONF"
fi

# Ensure proper permissions

echo "Automatic graphical login has been disabled in $GDM_CONF"

# Verify the settings
echo "Current daemon section settings:"
sed -n '/^\[daemon\]/,/^\[/p' "$GDM_CONF" | head -n -1

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Check if we're on RHEL 8
if [[ -f /etc/redhat-release ]]; then
    if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release; then
        echo "Warning: This script is designed for RHEL 8" >&2
    fi
fi

# Install usbguard if not already installed
if ! rpm -q usbguard &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing usbguard..."
    dnf install -y usbguard
fi

RULES_FILE="/etc/usbguard/rules.conf"
USBGUARD_CONF="/etc/usbguard/usbguard-daemon.conf"

# Function to check if usbguard-daemon is running
is_usbguard_running() {
    systemctl is-active --quiet usbguard 2>/dev/null
}

# Function to generate rules for currently connected devices
generate_initial_policy() {
    local temp_rules
    temp_rules=$(mktemp)
    
    # Check if usbguard is running and can generate rules
    if is_usbguard_running; then
        # If usbguard is already running, use it to generate rules for current devices
        usbguard generate-policy > "$temp_rules" 2>/dev/null || true
    else
        # If usbguard is not running, we need to start it temporarily or use generate-policy directly
        # generate-policy works without the daemon running
        usbguard generate-policy > "$temp_rules" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    # Check if we got any rules
    if [[ -s "$temp_rules" ]]; then
        echo "$temp_rules"
    else
        rm -f "$temp_rules"
        echo ""
    fi
}

# Function to check if current rules file already has valid rules
has_valid_rules() {
    if [[ -f "$RULES_FILE" ]] && [[ -s "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
        # Check if file has any non-comment, non-empty lines
        if grep -qE '^[^#]' "$RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to compare rule sets (ignoring order and whitespace differences)
rules_equivalent() {
    local file1="$1"
    local file2="$2"
    
    # Extract and sort device hashes/identifiers from both files
    local rules1 rules2
    rules1=$(grep -E '^allow|^block|^reject' "$file1" 2>/dev/null | sort || true)
    rules2=$(grep -E '^allow|^block|^reject' "$file2" 2>/dev/null | sort || true)
    
    [[ "$rules1" == "$rules2" ]]
}

# Ensure the rules.conf file exists with proper permissions
ensure_rules_file() {
    if [[ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
        touch "$RULES_FILE"
    fi
    chmod 600 "$RULES_FILE"
    chown root:root "$RULES_FILE"
}

# Ensure usbguard configuration is correct
configure_usbguard() {
    if [[ -f "$USBGUARD_CONF" ]]; then
        # Ensure RuleFile points to the correct location
        if ! grep -q "^RuleFile=$RULES_FILE" "$USBGUARD_CONF"; then
            if grep -q "^RuleFile=" "$USBGUARD_CONF"; then
                sed -i "s|^RuleFile=.*|RuleFile=$RULES_FILE|" "$USBGUARD_CONF"
            else
                echo "RuleFile=$RULES_FILE" >> "$USBGUARD_CONF"
            fi
        fi
        
        # Ensure ImplicitPolicyTarget is set to block (secure default)
        if ! grep -q "^ImplicitPolicyTarget=block" "$USBGUARD_CONF"; then
            if grep -q "^ImplicitPolicyTarget=" "$USBGUARD_CONF"; then
                sed -i "s|^ImplicitPolicyTarget=.*|ImplicitPolicyTarget=block|" "$USBGUARD_CONF"
            else
                echo "ImplicitPolicyTarget=block" >> "$USBGUARD_CONF"
            fi
        fi
        
        # Ensure PresentDevicePolicy allows devices present at boot
        if ! grep -q "^PresentDevicePolicy=apply-policy" "$USBGUARD_CONF"; then
            if grep -q "^PresentDevicePolicy=" "$USBGUARD_CONF"; then
                sed -i "s|^PresentDevicePolicy=.*|PresentDevicePolicy=apply-policy|" "$USBGUARD_CONF"
            else
                echo "PresentDevicePolicy=apply-policy" >> "$USBGUARD_CONF"
            fi
        fi
    fi
}

# Main logic
echo "Checking USBGuard configuration..."

# Ensure rules file exists
ensure_rules_file

# Configure usbguard daemon
configure_usbguard

# Check if we already have valid rules
if has_valid_rules; then
    echo "Existing rules found in $RULES_FILE"
    
    # Generate current policy to compare
    new_rules_file=$(generate_initial_policy)
    
    if [[ -n "$new_rules_file" ]] && [[ -f "$new_rules_file" ]]; then
        if rules_equivalent "$RULES_FILE" "$new_rules_file"; then
            echo "Current rules already match connected devices. No changes needed."
            rm -f "$new_rules_file"
        else
            echo "Existing rules found. Keeping current policy to maintain idempotency."
            echo "If you want to regenerate rules, remove $RULES_FILE and re-run this script."
            rm -f "$new_rules_file"
        fi
    else
        echo "Could not generate new rules for comparison. Keeping existing rules."
    fi
else
    echo "No valid rules found. Generating initial policy from connected devices..."
    
    new_rules_file=$(generate_initial_policy)
    
    if [[ -n "$new_rules_file" ]] && [[ -f "$new_rules_file" ]] && [[ -s "$new_rules_file" ]]; then
        # Backup existing rules file if it exists
        if [[ -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
            cp "$RULES_FILE" "${RULES_FILE}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
        fi
        
        # Install new rules
        cp "$new_rules_file" "$RULES_FILE"
        chmod 600 "$RULES_FILE"
        chown root:root "$RULES_FILE"
        
        echo "Generated initial policy with the following rules:"
        cat "$RULES_FILE"
        
        rm -f "$new_rules_file"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not generate rules from connected devices."
        echo "Creating minimal policy to allow all currently connected devices..."
        
        # Fallback: create a policy that allows all devices (less secure but prevents lockout)
        # This should rarely happen on a system with USB devices
        if [[ ! -s "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
            echo "# Auto-generated fallback policy - review and restrict as needed" > "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "# No USB devices were detected during policy generation" >> "$RULES_FILE"
            chmod 600 "$RULES_FILE"
            chown root:root "$RULES_FILE"
        fi
        
        [[ -n "${new_rules_file:-}" ]] && rm -f "$new_rules_file"
    fi
fi

# Verify rules file has correct permissions
chmod 600 "$RULES_FILE"
chown root:root "$RULES_FILE"

# Enable and start usbguard service for persistence across reboots
echo "Enabling USBGuard service..."

# If service is already running, we may need to restart to pick up new rules
if is_usbguard_running; then
    echo "USBGuard is already running. Reloading rules..."
    # Try to reload rules without restart to avoid disruption
    usbguard list-rules &>/dev/null || systemctl restart usbguard
else
    echo "Starting USBGuard service..."
    systemctl start usbguard
fi

# Enable service for persistence across reboots
systemctl enable usbguard

# Verify the service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet usbguard; then
    echo "USBGuard is active and running."
else
    echo "Warning: USBGuard service failed to start. Check 'systemctl status usbguard' for details." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Verify service is enabled for boot persistence
if systemctl is-enabled --quiet usbguard; then
    echo "USBGuard is enabled and will persist across reboots."
else
    echo "Warning: USBGuard service is not enabled for boot." >&2
    exit 1
fi

echo ""
echo "USBGuard configuration complete."
echo "Rules file: $RULES_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Current allowed devices:"
usbguard list-devices 2>/dev/null || echo "Could not list devices"
echo ""
echo "To review or modify rules, edit $RULES_FILE"
echo "After editing, reload with: usbguard list-rules"

        # Backup existing rules file if it exists
        if [[ -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
            cp "$RULES_FILE" "${RULES_FILE}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
        fi
        
        # Install new rules
        cp "$new_rules_file" "$RULES_FILE"
        chmod 600 "$RULES_FILE"
        chown root:root "$RULES_FILE"
        
        echo "Generated initial policy with the following rules:"
        cat "$RULES_FILE"
        
        rm -f "$new_rules_file"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not generate rules from connected devices."
        echo "Creating minimal policy to allow all currently connected devices..."
        
        # Fallback: create a policy that allows all devices (less secure but prevents lockout)
        # This should rarely happen on a system with USB devices
        if [[ ! -s "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
            echo "# Auto-generated fallback policy - review and restrict as needed" > "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "# No USB devices were detected during policy generation" >> "$RULES_FILE"
            chmod 600 "$RULES_FILE"
            chown root:root "$RULES_FILE"
        fi
        
        [[ -n "${new_rules_file:-}" ]] && rm -f "$new_rules_file"
    fi
fi

# Verify rules file has correct permissions
chmod 600 "$RULES_FILE"
chown root:root "$RULES_FILE"

# Enable and start usbguard service for persistence across reboots
echo "Enabling USBGuard service..."

# If service is already running, we may need to restart to pick up new rules
if is_usbguard_running; then
    echo "USBGuard is already running. Reloading rules..."
    # Try to reload rules without restart to avoid disruption
    usbguard list-rules &>/dev/null || systemctl restart usbguard
else
    echo "Starting USBGuard service..."
    systemctl start usbguard
fi

# Enable service for persistence across reboots
systemctl enable usbguard

# Verify the service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet usbguard; then
    echo "USBGuard is active and running."
else
    echo "Warning: USBGuard service failed to start. Check 'systemctl status usbguard' for details." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Verify service is enabled for boot persistence
if systemctl is-enabled --quiet usbguard; then
    echo "USBGuard is enabled and will persist across reboots."
else
    echo "Warning: USBGuard service is not enabled for boot." >&2
    exit 1
fi

echo ""
echo "USBGuard configuration complete."
echo "Rules file: $RULES_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Current allowed devices:"
usbguard list-devices 2>/dev/null || echo "Could not list devices"
echo ""
echo "To review or modify rules, edit $RULES_FILE"
echo "After editing, reload with: usbguard list-rules"
```

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot in GNOME desktop
# on RHEL 8 and lock the setting system-wide

set -e

# Define the dconf database directory and files
DCONF_LOCAL_DB="/etc/dconf/db/local.d"
DCONF_LOCKS_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d/locks"
DCONF_PROFILE_DIR="/etc/dconf/profile"

# Create directories if they don't exist
mkdir -p "$DCONF_LOCAL_DB"
mkdir -p "$DCONF_LOCKS_DIR"
mkdir -p "$DCONF_PROFILE_DIR"

# Create or update the dconf profile for user
PROFILE_FILE="$DCONF_PROFILE_DIR/user"
if [ ! -f "$PROFILE_FILE" ]; then
    cat > "$PROFILE_FILE" << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:local
EOF
else
    # Check if local database is already in the profile
    if ! grep -q "^system-db:local$" "$PROFILE_FILE"; then
        # Add system-db:local after user-db:user if it exists, otherwise append
        if grep -q "^user-db:user$" "$PROFILE_FILE"; then
            sed -i '/^user-db:user$/a system-db:local' "$PROFILE_FILE"
        else
            echo "system-db:local" >> "$PROFILE_FILE"
        fi
    fi
fi

# Create the settings file to disable Ctrl-Alt-Del
SETTINGS_FILE="$DCONF_LOCAL_DB/00-disable-ctrl-alt-del"
cat > "$SETTINGS_FILE" << 'EOF'
[org/gnome/settings-daemon/plugins/media-keys]
logout=''

[org/gnome/desktop/wm/keybindings]
close=''
EOF

# Create the locks file to prevent user override
LOCKS_FILE="$DCONF_LOCKS_DIR/00-disable-ctrl-alt-del"
cat > "$LOCKS_FILE" << 'EOF'
/org/gnome/settings-daemon/plugins/media-keys/logout
/org/gnome/desktop/wm/keybindings/close
EOF

# Update the dconf database
dconf update

echo "Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot via GNOME has been disabled and locked system-wide."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to configure audit rules for directory deletion tracking
# on RHEL 8 for users with UID >= 1000

AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/delete.rules"

# Define the audit rules for directory deletion (rmdir syscall)
# For both 32-bit and 64-bit architectures
RULE_64BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S rmdir -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete"
RULE_32BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S rmdir -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete"

# Function to check if a rule exists in a file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -qxF "$rule" "$file"
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule if it doesn't exist
add_rule_if_missing() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    if ! rule_exists "$rule" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        echo "Added rule: $rule"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Rule already exists: $rule"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Create the rules file if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "Created $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Track if any changes were made
CHANGES_MADE=0

# Add 64-bit rule
if add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    CHANGES_MADE=1
fi

# Add 32-bit rule
if add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    CHANGES_MADE=1
fi

# Ensure proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# If changes were made, reload the audit rules
if [[ $CHANGES_MADE -eq 1 ]]; then
    echo "Reloading audit rules..."
    
    # Check if auditd is running
    if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
        # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
        if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
            augenrules --load
        else
            # Fallback to auditctl if augenrules is not available
            auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        fi
        echo "Audit rules reloaded successfully"
    else
        echo "auditd is not running. Rules will be loaded on next auditd start."
    fi
else
    echo "No changes needed. All rules already in place."
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled
if ! systemctl is-enabled --quiet auditd; then
    systemctl enable auditd
    echo "Enabled auditd service"
fi

# Verify the rules are loaded (for informational purposes)
echo ""
echo "Current loaded delete-related audit rules:"
auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -E "delete|rmdir" || echo "No delete rules currently loaded (auditd may need restart)"

echo ""
echo "Rules configured in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE:"
cat "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to configure SSH X11UseLocalhost on RHEL 8
# This ensures X11 forwarding proxy is bound only to loopback interface

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
DROP_IN_FILE="${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/99-x11uselocalhost.conf"

# Function to check if a parameter is set correctly in sshd_config or drop-in files
check_sshd_param() {
    local param="$1"
    local expected_value="$2"
    
    # Check main config and drop-in files
    if sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep -qi "^${param} ${expected_value}$"; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to remove any existing X11UseLocalhost settings from main config
remove_from_main_config() {
    if grep -Ei '^\s*X11UseLocalhost\s+' "$SSHD_CONFIG" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        sed -i '/^\s*X11UseLocalhost\s\+/Id' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    fi
}

# Function to set X11UseLocalhost in drop-in file
set_x11uselocalhost() {
    # Ensure the drop-in directory exists
    if [ ! -d "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
        mkdir -p "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"
        chmod 755 "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"
    fi
    
    # Remove any existing X11UseLocalhost from main config to avoid conflicts
    remove_from_main_config
    
    # Create or update drop-in file
    echo "X11UseLocalhost yes" > "$DROP_IN_FILE"
    chmod 600 "$DROP_IN_FILE"
}

# Main execution

# Check if already configured correctly
if check_sshd_param "x11uselocalhost" "yes"; then
    echo "X11UseLocalhost is already set to 'yes'. No changes needed."
else
    echo "Configuring X11UseLocalhost to 'yes'..."
    set_x11uselocalhost
    
    # Verify the configuration is valid
    if sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "SSH configuration is valid."
        
        # Restart sshd to apply changes
        if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
            systemctl reload sshd 2>/dev/null || systemctl restart sshd
            echo "SSHD service reloaded."
        fi
        
        # Verify the change took effect
        if check_sshd_param "x11uselocalhost" "yes"; then
            echo "X11UseLocalhost successfully configured to 'yes'."
        else
            echo "Warning: Configuration may not have taken effect as expected."
            exit 1
        fi
    else
        echo "Error: SSH configuration test failed."
        exit 1
    fi
fi

exit 0

SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
DROP_IN_FILE="${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/99-x11uselocalhost.conf"

# Function to check if a parameter is set correctly in sshd_config or drop-in files
check_sshd_param() {
    local param="$1"
    local expected_value="$2"
    
    # Check main config and drop-in files
    if sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep -qi "^${param} ${expected_value}$"; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to remove any existing X11UseLocalhost settings from main config
remove_from_main_config() {
    if grep -Ei '^\s*X11UseLocalhost\s+' "$SSHD_CONFIG" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        sed -i '/^\s*X11UseLocalhost\s\+/Id' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    fi
}

# Function to set X11UseLocalhost in drop-in file
set_x11uselocalhost() {
    # Ensure the drop-in directory exists
    if [ ! -d "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
        mkdir -p "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"
        chmod 755 "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"
    fi
    
    # Remove any existing X11UseLocalhost from main config to avoid conflicts
    remove_from_main_config
    
    # Create or update drop-in file
    echo "X11UseLocalhost yes" > "$DROP_IN_FILE"
    chmod 600 "$DROP_IN_FILE"
}

# Main execution

# Check if already configured correctly
if check_sshd_param "x11uselocalhost" "yes"; then
    echo "X11UseLocalhost is already set to 'yes'. No changes needed."
else
    echo "Configuring X11UseLocalhost to 'yes'..."
    set_x11uselocalhost
    
    # Verify the configuration is valid
    if sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "SSH configuration is valid."
        
        # Restart sshd to apply changes
        if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
            systemctl reload sshd 2>/dev/null || systemctl restart sshd
            echo "SSHD service reloaded."
        fi
        
        # Verify the change took effect
        if check_sshd_param "x11uselocalhost" "yes"; then
            echo "X11UseLocalhost successfully configured to 'yes'."
        else
            echo "Warning: Configuration may not have taken effect as expected."
            exit 1
        fi
    else
        echo "Error: SSH configuration test failed."
        exit 1
    fi
fi

exit 0
```

#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 System-Wide Cryptographic Policy Remediation Script
# Sets the cryptographic policy to DEFAULT profile

set -euo pipefail

# Define the target policy
TARGET_POLICY="DEFAULT"

# Function to check if running as root
check_root() {
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "ERROR: This script must be run as root" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Function to get current crypto policy
get_current_policy() {
    update-crypto-policies --show 2>/dev/null || echo "UNKNOWN"
}

# Function to set crypto policy
set_crypto_policy() {
    local policy="$1"
    echo "Setting system-wide cryptographic policy to: ${policy}"
    update-crypto-policies --set "${policy}"
}

# Function to verify policy is set correctly
verify_policy() {
    local expected="$1"
    local current
    current=$(get_current_policy)
    
    if [[ "${current}" == "${expected}" ]]; then
        echo "SUCCESS: Cryptographic policy is set to ${expected}"
        return 0
    else
        echo "ERROR: Policy verification failed. Expected: ${expected}, Got: ${current}" >&2
        return 1
    fi
}

# Function to verify backend configuration files
verify_backends() {
    local policy="$1"
    local errors=0
    
    echo "Verifying backend configuration files..."
    
    # Check if the policy symlink exists and points to correct policy
    local policy_link="/etc/crypto-policies/back-ends"
    if [[ -d "${policy_link}" ]]; then
        echo "Backend directory exists: ${policy_link}"
    else
        echo "WARNING: Backend directory not found: ${policy_link}" >&2
        ((errors++))
    fi
    
    # Verify the current policy file
    local current_policy_file="/etc/crypto-policies/state/current"
    if [[ -f "${current_policy_file}" ]]; then
        local file_content
        file_content=$(cat "${current_policy_file}")
        if [[ "${file_content}" == "${policy}" ]]; then
            echo "Policy state file correctly shows: ${policy}"
        else
            echo "WARNING: Policy state file shows: ${file_content}, expected: ${policy}" >&2
            ((errors++))
        fi
    else
        echo "WARNING: Policy state file not found: ${current_policy_file}" >&2
        ((errors++))
    fi
    
    # Check common backend files exist
    local backend_files=(
        "opensslcnf.config"
        "openssl.config"
        "nss.config"
        "java.config"
        "krb5.config"
        "bind.config"
        "gnutls.config"
    )
    
    for backend in "${backend_files[@]}"; do
        local backend_path="${policy_link}/${backend}"
        if [[ -e "${backend_path}" ]]; then
            echo "Backend configured: ${backend}"
        fi
    done
    
    return ${errors}
}

# Function to ensure crypto-policies package is installed
ensure_package_installed() {
    if ! rpm -q crypto-policies &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Installing crypto-policies package..."
        dnf install -y crypto-policies
    else
        echo "crypto-policies package is already installed"
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "=========================================="
    echo "RHEL 8 Cryptographic Policy Remediation"
    echo "=========================================="
    
    # Check for root privileges
    check_root
    
    # Ensure required package is installed
    ensure_package_installed
    
    # Get current policy
    local current_policy
    current_policy=$(get_current_policy)
    echo "Current cryptographic policy: ${current_policy}"
    
    # Check if policy is already set correctly (idempotent check)
    if [[ "${current_policy}" == "${TARGET_POLICY}" ]]; then
        echo "Cryptographic policy is already set to ${TARGET_POLICY}"
        echo "No changes needed."
    else
        echo "Changing policy from ${current_policy} to ${TARGET_POLICY}..."
        set_crypto_policy "${TARGET_POLICY}"
    fi
    
    # Verify the policy is correctly set
    if ! verify_policy "${TARGET_POLICY}"; then
        echo "ERROR: Failed to set cryptographic policy" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Verify backend configurations
    if ! verify_backends "${TARGET_POLICY}"; then
        echo "WARNING: Some backend verifications had issues" >&2
    fi
    
    echo ""
    echo "=========================================="
    echo "Remediation Complete"
    echo "=========================================="
    echo "The system-wide cryptographic policy has been set to: ${TARGET_POLICY}"
    echo "This policy will persist across reboots."
    echo ""
    echo "Note: Running applications may need to be restarted to use the new policy."
    echo "A system reboot is recommended to ensure all services use the updated policy."
    
    exit 0
}

# Run main function
main

#!/bin/bash

# Kernel parameter to disable source-routed packets
PARAM="net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_source_route"
VALUE="0"

# Apply the setting at runtime
sysctl -w "${PARAM}=${VALUE}"

# Ensure the setting persists across reboots
SYSCTL_CONF_DIR="/etc/sysctl.d"
SYSCTL_CONF_FILE="${SYSCTL_CONF_DIR}/99-disable-source-route.conf"

# Create the sysctl.d directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "${SYSCTL_CONF_DIR}"

# Check if the parameter is already set correctly in any sysctl config file
if grep -rqs "^${PARAM}\s*=\s*${VALUE}$" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Parameter ${PARAM} is already configured correctly for persistence."
else
    # Remove any existing conflicting settings for this parameter
    sed -i "/^${PARAM}\s*=/d" /etc/sysctl.conf 2>/dev/null
    for file in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf; do
        if [ -f "$file" ]; then
            sed -i "/^${PARAM}\s*=/d" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        fi
    done
    
    # Add the correct setting to our dedicated config file
    echo "${PARAM} = ${VALUE}" >> "${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE}"
    echo "Added ${PARAM} = ${VALUE} to ${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE}"
fi

# Verify the runtime setting
CURRENT_VALUE=$(sysctl -n "${PARAM}")
if [ "${CURRENT_VALUE}" = "${VALUE}" ]; then
    echo "Successfully configured ${PARAM} = ${VALUE}"
    exit 0
else
    echo "Failed to set ${PARAM} to ${VALUE}. Current value: ${CURRENT_VALUE}"
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

set -e

MOUNT_POINT="/boot"
OPTION="nosuid"
FSTAB="/etc/fstab"

# Check if /boot is a separate mount point
if ! mountpoint -q "$MOUNT_POINT" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "/boot is not a separately mounted filesystem. No action needed."
    exit 0
fi

echo "/boot is a separate mount point. Checking for $OPTION option..."

# Function to check if option is present in mount options string
has_option() {
    local options="$1"
    local opt="$2"
    echo "$options" | tr ',' '\n' | grep -qx "$opt"
}

# Function to add option to mount options string
add_option() {
    local options="$1"
    local opt="$2"
    if [ -z "$options" ] || [ "$options" = "defaults" ]; then
        echo "defaults,$opt"
    elif has_option "$options" "$opt"; then
        echo "$options"
    else
        echo "$options,$opt"
    fi
}

# Check current mount options
current_options=$(findmnt -n -o OPTIONS "$MOUNT_POINT" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [ -z "$current_options" ]; then
    echo "Error: Could not determine current mount options for $MOUNT_POINT"
    exit 1
fi

# Check if nosuid is already in effect on running system
if has_option "$current_options" "$OPTION"; then
    echo "$OPTION is already active on $MOUNT_POINT"
    running_ok=true
else
    echo "$OPTION is NOT currently active on $MOUNT_POINT"
    running_ok=false
fi

# Update fstab if needed
fstab_updated=false

if grep -qE "^[^#]*[[:space:]]$MOUNT_POINT[[:space:]]" "$FSTAB"; then
    # Get current fstab options for /boot
    fstab_line=$(grep -E "^[^#]*[[:space:]]$MOUNT_POINT[[:space:]]" "$FSTAB" | head -1)
    fstab_options=$(echo "$fstab_line" | awk '{print $4}')
    
    if has_option "$fstab_options" "$OPTION"; then
        echo "$OPTION is already configured in $FSTAB for $MOUNT_POINT"
    else
        echo "Adding $OPTION to $FSTAB for $MOUNT_POINT"
        new_options=$(add_option "$fstab_options" "$OPTION")
        
        # Create backup
        cp "$FSTAB" "${FSTAB}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
        
        # Update fstab - escape special characters for sed
        escaped_mount=$(echo "$MOUNT_POINT" | sed 's/\//\\\//g')
        sed -i -E "s|(^[^#]*[[:space:]]$escaped_mount[[:space:]]+[^[:space:]]+[[:space:]]+)[^[:space:]]+|\1$new_options|" "$FSTAB"
        
        fstab_updated=true
        echo "Updated $FSTAB with $OPTION for $MOUNT_POINT"
    fi
else
    echo "Warning: $MOUNT_POINT not found in $FSTAB. Cannot persist mount options."
    echo "The mount may be configured elsewhere (e.g., systemd mount unit)."
    
    # Check for systemd mount unit
    boot_unit=$(systemctl list-units --type=mount --all 2>/dev/null | grep -E "boot\.mount" | awk '{print $1}' || true)
    if [ -n "$boot_unit" ]; then
        echo "Found systemd mount unit: $boot_unit"
        echo "You may need to create an override for this unit to add $OPTION"
    fi
fi

# Apply to running system if not already active
if [ "$running_ok" = false ]; then
    echo "Remounting $MOUNT_POINT with $OPTION option..."
    if mount -o remount,"$OPTION" "$MOUNT_POINT"; then
        echo "Successfully remounted $MOUNT_POINT with $OPTION"
    else
        echo "Error: Failed to remount $MOUNT_POINT with $OPTION"
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# Verify the change
echo ""
echo "Verification:"
final_options=$(findmnt -n -o OPTIONS "$MOUNT_POINT")
if has_option "$final_options" "$OPTION"; then
    echo "SUCCESS: $MOUNT_POINT is now mounted with $OPTION"
    echo "Current mount options: $final_options"
else
    echo "FAILED: $OPTION is not active on $MOUNT_POINT"
    echo "Current mount options: $final_options"
    exit 1
fi

if grep -qE "^[^#]*[[:space:]]$MOUNT_POINT[[:space:]]" "$FSTAB"; then
    fstab_check=$(grep -E "^[^#]*[[:space:]]$MOUNT_POINT[[:space:]]" "$FSTAB" | head -1 | awk '{print $4}')
    if has_option "$fstab_check" "$OPTION"; then
        echo "SUCCESS: $OPTION is configured in $FSTAB (will persist across reboots)"
    else
        echo "WARNING: $OPTION is NOT in $FSTAB - change may not persist"
    fi
fi

exit 0

# Set the desired value for kernel.perf_event_paranoid
# Value 2 restricts unprivileged users to per-process kernel and CPU profiling only
PARAM_NAME="kernel.perf_event_paranoid"
PARAM_VALUE="2"
SYSCTL_CONF_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-perf_event_paranoid.conf"

# Apply the setting immediately at runtime
current_value=$(sysctl -n "${PARAM_NAME}" 2>/dev/null)
if [ "$current_value" != "$PARAM_VALUE" ]; then
    sysctl -w "${PARAM_NAME}=${PARAM_VALUE}"
fi

# Ensure persistence across reboots
# Check if the configuration file exists and has the correct setting
if [ -f "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" ]; then
    # Check if the parameter is already set correctly in the file
    if grep -q "^${PARAM_NAME}\s*=\s*${PARAM_VALUE}$" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Already configured correctly, nothing to do
        :
    elif grep -q "^${PARAM_NAME}\s*=" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Parameter exists but with different value, update it
        sed -i "s/^${PARAM_NAME}\s*=.*/${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}/" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
    else
        # Parameter not in file, append it
        echo "${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}" >> "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
    fi

#!/bin/bash

# Set the desired value for kernel.perf_event_paranoid
# Value 2 restricts unprivileged users to per-process kernel and CPU profiling only
PARAM_NAME="kernel.perf_event_paranoid"
PARAM_VALUE="2"
SYSCTL_CONF_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-perf_event_paranoid.conf"

# Apply the setting immediately at runtime
current_value=$(sysctl -n "${PARAM_NAME}" 2>/dev/null)
if [ "$current_value" != "$PARAM_VALUE" ]; then
    sysctl -w "${PARAM_NAME}=${PARAM_VALUE}"
fi

# Ensure persistence across reboots
# Check if the configuration file exists and has the correct setting
if [ -f "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" ]; then
    # Check if the parameter is already set correctly in the file
    if grep -q "^${PARAM_NAME}\s*=\s*${PARAM_VALUE}$" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Already configured correctly, nothing to do
        :
    elif grep -q "^${PARAM_NAME}\s*=" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Parameter exists but with different value, update it
        sed -i "s/^${PARAM_NAME}\s*=.*/${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}/" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
    else
        # Parameter not in file, append it
        echo "${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}" >> "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
    fi
else
    # File doesn't exist, create it with the parameter
    echo "${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}" > "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
fi

# Also check and remove any conflicting settings in other sysctl configuration files
# to ensure our setting takes precedence
for conf_file in /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf; do
    if [ -f "$conf_file" ] && [ "$conf_file" != "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" ]; then
        if grep -q "^${PARAM_NAME}\s*=" "$conf_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            # Comment out conflicting entries in other files
            sed -i "s/^${PARAM_NAME}\s*=.*/#&/" "$conf_file"
        fi
    fi
done

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to ensure audit rules capture file deletion events (unlinkat syscall)
# for users with UID >= 1000, tagged with "delete" identifier

AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/delete.rules"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"

# Determine architecture
ARCH=$(uname -m)

# Define the rules we need to add
RULE_64="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete"
RULE_32="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete"

# Function to check if a rule already exists in any rules file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local arch_pattern=$(echo "$rule" | grep -oP 'arch=b\d+')
    local syscall_pattern=$(echo "$rule" | grep -oP '-S \w+')
    local key_pattern=$(echo "$rule" | grep -oP '-k \w+')
    
    # Check in all rules files
    if grep -r -E "arch=${arch_pattern#arch=}.*${syscall_pattern#-S }.*auid>=1000.*auid!=(-1|unset|4294967295).*-k delete" "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/" 2>/dev/null | grep -q .; then
        return 0
    fi
    
    # Also check the main auditd rules file
    if [ -f "/etc/audit/audit.rules" ]; then
        if grep -E "arch=${arch_pattern#arch=}.*${syscall_pattern#-S }.*auid>=1000.*auid!=(-1|unset|4294967295).*-k delete" "/etc/audit/audit.rules" 2>/dev/null | grep -q .; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule if it doesn't exist
add_rule_if_missing() {
    local rule="$1"
    local arch="$2"
    
    # Check if rule already exists (in any format)
    if grep -r -E "arch=${arch}.*-S unlinkat.*auid>=1000.*auid!=(-1|unset|4294967295).*-k delete" "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/" 2>/dev/null | grep -q .; then
        echo "Rule for arch=${arch} unlinkat already exists"
        return 0
    fi
    
    # Add the rule to our rules file
    echo "$rule" >> "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    echo "Added rule: $rule"
}

# Create or clear the rules file if we need to add rules
# First, check what rules we need to add
NEED_64=false
NEED_32=false

if [ "$ARCH" = "x86_64" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "aarch64" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "ppc64le" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "s390x" ]; then
    # 64-bit system - need both 32-bit and 64-bit rules
    if ! grep -r -E "arch=b64.*-S unlinkat.*auid>=1000.*auid!=(-1|unset|4294967295).*-k delete" "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/" 2>/dev/null | grep -q .; then
        NEED_64=true
    fi
    if ! grep -r -E "arch=b32.*-S unlinkat.*auid>=1000.*auid!=(-1|unset|4294967295).*-k delete" "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/" 2>/dev/null | grep -q .; then
        NEED_32=true
    fi
else
    # 32-bit system - only need 32-bit rules
    if ! grep -r -E "arch=b32.*-S unlinkat.*auid>=1000.*auid!=(-1|unset|4294967295).*-k delete" "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/" 2>/dev/null | grep -q .; then
        NEED_32=true
    fi
fi

# Only create/modify the file if we need to add rules
if [ "$NEED_64" = true ] || [ "$NEED_32" = true ]; then
    # Remove any existing delete.rules file to avoid duplicates
    if [ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]; then
        # Keep any non-unlinkat rules
        grep -v "unlinkat" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.tmp" 2>/dev/null || true
        mv "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.tmp" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
    
    # Add the required rules
    if [ "$ARCH" = "x86_64" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "aarch64" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "ppc64le" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "s390x" ]; then
        # 64-bit system
        if [ "$NEED_64" = true ]; then
            echo "$RULE_64" >> "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
            echo "Added 64-bit unlinkat rule"
        fi
        if [ "$NEED_32" = true ]; then
            echo "$RULE_32" >> "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
            echo "Added 32-bit unlinkat rule"
        fi
    else
        # 32-bit system
        if [ "$NEED_32" = true ]; then
            echo "$RULE_32" >> "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
            echo "Added 32-bit unlinkat rule"
        fi
    fi
    
    # Set proper permissions
    chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    chown root:root "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled
systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null || true

# Load the new rules
# Use augenrules to merge rules and load them
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Alternatively, use auditctl to load rules immediately
if command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    if [ "$ARCH" = "x86_64" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "aarch64" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "ppc64le" ] || [ "$ARCH" = "s390x" ]; then
        auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete 2>/dev/null || true
        auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete 2>/dev/null || true
    else
        auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k delete 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
fi

echo "Audit rules for file deletion (unlinkat) have been configured."
echo "Rules are stored in: ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
echo "Current audit rules:"
auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -E "unlinkat.*delete" || echo "Rules will be active after auditd restart"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to enable account lockout for root on RHEL 8
# This ensures root is subject to the same failed-attempt lockout policy as regular users

set -e

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root"
    exit 1
fi

# Ensure authselect is managing PAM configuration
# First, check current authselect profile
CURRENT_PROFILE=$(authselect current -r 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [[ -z "$CURRENT_PROFILE" ]]; then
    # No profile selected, use sssd as default (common on RHEL 8)
    echo "No authselect profile detected, selecting sssd profile with faillock"
    authselect select sssd with-faillock --force
    CURRENT_PROFILE="sssd"
else
    # Check if with-faillock feature is already enabled
    if ! authselect current | grep -q "with-faillock"; then
        echo "Enabling faillock feature on current profile"
        authselect enable-feature with-faillock 2>/dev/null || \
            authselect select "$CURRENT_PROFILE" with-faillock --force
    fi
fi

# Configure faillock to include root account
# The faillock configuration file on RHEL 8
FAILLOCK_CONF="/etc/security/faillock.conf"

# Backup the original file if not already backed up
if [[ -f "$FAILLOCK_CONF" ]] && [[ ! -f "${FAILLOCK_CONF}.orig" ]]; then
    cp "$FAILLOCK_CONF" "${FAILLOCK_CONF}.orig"
fi

# Create or update faillock.conf with root lockout enabled
# Check if even_deny_root is already set
if [[ -f "$FAILLOCK_CONF" ]]; then
    # Remove any existing even_deny_root lines (commented or not) to ensure idempotency
    sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*even_deny_root/d' "$FAILLOCK_CONF"
    
    # Remove any existing root_unlock_time lines
    sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*root_unlock_time/d' "$FAILLOCK_CONF"
    
    # Check if deny is set, if not set a default
    if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*deny[[:space:]]*=" "$FAILLOCK_CONF"; then
        echo "deny = 3" >> "$FAILLOCK_CONF"
    fi
    
    # Check if unlock_time is set, if not set a default
    if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*unlock_time[[:space:]]*=" "$FAILLOCK_CONF"; then
        echo "unlock_time = 600" >> "$FAILLOCK_CONF"
    fi
    
    # Check if fail_interval is set, if not set a default
    if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*fail_interval[[:space:]]*=" "$FAILLOCK_CONF"; then
        echo "fail_interval = 900" >> "$FAILLOCK_CONF"
    fi
else
    # Create the file with default secure settings
    cat > "$FAILLOCK_CONF" << 'EOF'
# Configuration for pam_faillock
# Deny access after 3 failed attempts
deny = 3

# Time interval in seconds during which failures are counted
fail_interval = 900

# Time in seconds before the account is unlocked (0 = manual unlock required)
unlock_time = 600

# Also apply lockout policy to root account
even_deny_root

# Time in seconds before root account is unlocked
root_unlock_time = 600

# Directory where failure records are kept
dir = /var/run/faillock

# Audit failed attempts
audit

# Log to syslog on lock
silent
EOF
fi

# Add even_deny_root and root_unlock_time to the config
echo "even_deny_root" >> "$FAILLOCK_CONF"
echo "root_unlock_time = 600" >> "$FAILLOCK_CONF"

# Ensure the faillock directory exists
mkdir -p /var/run/faillock
chmod 755 /var/run/faillock

# Verify PAM configuration includes pam_faillock correctly
# Check system-auth and password-auth files
for pamfile in /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth; do
    if [[ -f "$pamfile" ]]; then
        # Verify pam_faillock is present (should be managed by authselect)
        if ! grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pamfile"; then
            echo "WARNING: pam_faillock.so not found in $pamfile"
            echo "Please ensure authselect with-faillock feature is properly enabled"
        fi
    fi
done

# Apply authselect changes
authselect apply-changes 2>/dev/null || true

echo "Root account lockout configuration completed successfully."
echo ""
echo "Current faillock settings:"
echo "=========================="
grep -v "^#" "$FAILLOCK_CONF" | grep -v "^$" || true
echo ""
echo "To test the configuration, you can:"
echo "  1. Attempt to login as root with wrong password multiple times"
echo "  2. Check lockout status with: faillock --user root"
echo "  3. Reset lockout with: faillock --user root --reset"

# Time interval in seconds during which failures are counted
fail_interval = 900

# Time in seconds before the account is unlocked (0 = manual unlock required)
unlock_time = 600

# Also apply lockout policy to root account
even_deny_root

# Time in seconds before root account is unlocked
root_unlock_time = 600

# Directory where failure records are kept
dir = /var/run/faillock

# Audit failed attempts
audit

# Log to syslog on lock
silent
EOF
fi

# Add even_deny_root and root_unlock_time to the config
echo "even_deny_root" >> "$FAILLOCK_CONF"
echo "root_unlock_time = 600" >> "$FAILLOCK_CONF"

# Ensure the faillock directory exists
mkdir -p /var/run/faillock
chmod 755 /var/run/faillock

# Verify PAM configuration includes pam_faillock correctly
# Check system-auth and password-auth files
for pamfile in /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth; do
    if [[ -f "$pamfile" ]]; then
        # Verify pam_faillock is present (should be managed by authselect)
        if ! grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pamfile"; then
            echo "WARNING: pam_faillock.so not found in $pamfile"
            echo "Please ensure authselect with-faillock feature is properly enabled"
        fi
    fi
done

# Apply authselect changes
authselect apply-changes 2>/dev/null || true

echo "Root account lockout configuration completed successfully."
echo ""
echo "Current faillock settings:"
echo "=========================="
grep -v "^#" "$FAILLOCK_CONF" | grep -v "^$" || true
echo ""
echo "To test the configuration, you can:"
echo "  1. Attempt to login as root with wrong password multiple times"
echo "  2. Check lockout status with: faillock --user root"
echo "  3. Reset lockout with: faillock --user root --reset"
```

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to disable ATM kernel module on RHEL 8
# This script is idempotent - safe to run multiple times

set -e

MODULE_NAME="atm"
MODPROBE_CONF="/etc/modprobe.d/atm.conf"

# Create the modprobe.d directory if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -d /etc/modprobe.d ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/modprobe.d
fi

# Define the lines we need in the configuration
INSTALL_LINE="install ${MODULE_NAME} /bin/false"
BLACKLIST_LINE="blacklist ${MODULE_NAME}"

# Function to check if a line exists in the file
line_exists() {
    local line="$1"
    local file="$2"
    grep -qxF "$line" "$file" 2>/dev/null
}

# Create or update the configuration file
NEEDS_UPDATE=false

if [ ! -f "$MODPROBE_CONF" ]; then
    NEEDS_UPDATE=true
else
    if ! line_exists "$INSTALL_LINE" "$MODPROBE_CONF"; then
        NEEDS_UPDATE=true
    fi
    if ! line_exists "$BLACKLIST_LINE" "$MODPROBE_CONF"; then
        NEEDS_UPDATE=true
    fi
fi

if [ "$NEEDS_UPDATE" = true ]; then
    # Create a temporary file with the correct content
    TEMP_CONF=$(mktemp)
    
    # Start with existing content if file exists, filtering out old atm entries
    if [ -f "$MODPROBE_CONF" ]; then
        grep -v "^install ${MODULE_NAME}" "$MODPROBE_CONF" 2>/dev/null | grep -v "^blacklist ${MODULE_NAME}" > "$TEMP_CONF" || true
    fi
    
    # Add the required lines if not present
    if ! grep -qxF "$INSTALL_LINE" "$TEMP_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "$INSTALL_LINE" >> "$TEMP_CONF"
    fi
    
    if ! grep -qxF "$BLACKLIST_LINE" "$TEMP_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "$BLACKLIST_LINE" >> "$TEMP_CONF"
    fi
    
    # Move the temp file to the final location
    mv "$TEMP_CONF" "$MODPROBE_CONF"
    chmod 644 "$MODPROBE_CONF"
    
    echo "Configuration updated in $MODPROBE_CONF"
else
    echo "Configuration already correct in $MODPROBE_CONF"
fi

# Unload the module if it's currently loaded
if lsmod | grep -q "^${MODULE_NAME}"; then
    echo "Attempting to unload ${MODULE_NAME} module..."
    if modprobe -r "$MODULE_NAME" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Module ${MODULE_NAME} unloaded successfully"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not unload ${MODULE_NAME} module. A reboot may be required."
    fi
else
    echo "Module ${MODULE_NAME} is not currently loaded"
fi

echo "ATM kernel module has been disabled and blacklisted"

#!/bin/bash

# Disable IPv6 router advertisements for all interfaces

# Sysctl parameter to set
SYSCTL_PARAM="net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra"
SYSCTL_VALUE="0"

# Apply the setting at runtime
sysctl -w "${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${SYSCTL_VALUE}"

# Ensure the setting persists across reboots
# Check if the setting already exists in sysctl.d
SYSCTL_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-ipv6-accept-ra.conf"

# Check if any existing file in sysctl.d or sysctl.conf already has this setting
EXISTING_FILES=$(grep -rl "^${SYSCTL_PARAM}" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/ 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$EXISTING_FILES" ]; then
    # Update existing configuration(s)
    for file in $EXISTING_FILES; do
        sed -i "s/^${SYSCTL_PARAM}.*/${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${SYSCTL_VALUE}/" "$file"
    done
else
    # Create new configuration file
    echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${SYSCTL_VALUE}" > "${SYSCTL_FILE}"
fi

# Also apply to default interface configuration for new interfaces
SYSCTL_PARAM_DEFAULT="net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_ra"

# Apply at runtime for default
sysctl -w "${SYSCTL_PARAM_DEFAULT}=${SYSCTL_VALUE}"

# Check if default setting exists
EXISTING_FILES_DEFAULT=$(grep -rl "^${SYSCTL_PARAM_DEFAULT}" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/ 2>/dev/null)

if [ -n "$EXISTING_FILES_DEFAULT" ]; then
    for file in $EXISTING_FILES_DEFAULT; do
        sed -i "s/^${SYSCTL_PARAM_DEFAULT}.*/${SYSCTL_PARAM_DEFAULT} = ${SYSCTL_VALUE}/" "$file"
    done
else
    # Append to our configuration file if it exists, or create it
    if [ -f "${SYSCTL_FILE}" ]; then
        if ! grep -q "^${SYSCTL_PARAM_DEFAULT}" "${SYSCTL_FILE}"; then
            echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM_DEFAULT} = ${SYSCTL_VALUE}" >> "${SYSCTL_FILE}"
        fi
    else
        echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM_DEFAULT} = ${SYSCTL_VALUE}" >> "${SYSCTL_FILE}"
    fi
fi

# Reload sysctl to ensure all settings are applied
sysctl --system

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the script is run as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# The USB device authorization service on RHEL 8 is usbguard
SERVICE_NAME="usbguard"

# Check if usbguard is installed, if not install it
if ! rpm -q usbguard &>/dev/null; then
    echo "usbguard is not installed. Installing..."
    dnf install -y usbguard
    if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Failed to install usbguard" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# Generate initial policy if rules file is empty or doesn't exist
RULES_FILE="/etc/usbguard/rules.conf"
if [[ ! -s "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    echo "Generating initial USBGuard policy based on currently connected devices..."
    usbguard generate-policy > "$RULES_FILE"
    chmod 600 "$RULES_FILE"
fi

# Enable the service to start at boot (idempotent - won't fail if already enabled)
if ! systemctl is-enabled "$SERVICE_NAME" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Enabling $SERVICE_NAME service..."
    systemctl enable "$SERVICE_NAME"
    if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Failed to enable $SERVICE_NAME service" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
else
    echo "$SERVICE_NAME service is already enabled"
fi

# Start the service if not currently active (idempotent)
if ! systemctl is-active "$SERVICE_NAME" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Starting $SERVICE_NAME service..."
    systemctl start "$SERVICE_NAME"
    if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Failed to start $SERVICE_NAME service" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
else
    echo "$SERVICE_NAME service is already active"
fi

# Verify the service is enabled and active
echo "Verifying $SERVICE_NAME service status..."
systemctl is-enabled "$SERVICE_NAME"
systemctl is-active "$SERVICE_NAME"

echo "USB device authorization service ($SERVICE_NAME) is enabled and active."
exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure auditd is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and started
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Define the audit rules for delete_module syscall
# We need to handle both 32-bit and 64-bit architectures

AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/modules.rules"

# Define the rules we want to add
RULE_64BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules"
RULE_32BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules"

# Create or update the rules file
# First, check if the file exists and contains our rules

add_rule_if_missing() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    # Check if rule already exists in the file
    if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        # Normalize whitespace for comparison
        local normalized_rule=$(echo "$rule" | tr -s ' ')
        if grep -q -F "$normalized_rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    # Add the rule
    echo "$rule" >> "$file"
    return 1
}

# Create the rules file if it doesn't exist
touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Track if we need to reload rules
RULES_CHANGED=0

# Add 64-bit rule if not present
if ! grep -q "^-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null && \
   ! grep -q "^-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k modules$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "$RULE_64BIT" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    RULES_CHANGED=1
fi

# Add 32-bit rule if not present
if ! grep -q "^-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null && \
   ! grep -q "^-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k modules$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "$RULE_32BIT" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    RULES_CHANGED=1
fi

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Load the rules into the running kernel
# Check if rules are already loaded
LOADED_RULES=$(auditctl -l 2>/dev/null)

RULE_64_LOADED=0
RULE_32_LOADED=0

if echo "$LOADED_RULES" | grep -q "arch=b64.*delete_module.*auid>=1000.*auid!=-1.*key=modules" || \
   echo "$LOADED_RULES" | grep -q "arch=b64.*delete_module.*auid>=1000.*auid!=4294967295.*key=modules"; then
    RULE_64_LOADED=1
fi

if echo "$LOADED_RULES" | grep -q "arch=b32.*delete_module.*auid>=1000.*auid!=-1.*key=modules" || \
   echo "$LOADED_RULES" | grep -q "arch=b32.*delete_module.*auid>=1000.*auid!=4294967295.*key=modules"; then
    RULE_32_LOADED=1
fi

# Add rules to running kernel if not already loaded
if [ $RULE_64_LOADED -eq 0 ]; then
    auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules 2>/dev/null || true
fi

if [ $RULE_32_LOADED -eq 0 ]; then
    auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Regenerate the aggregated rules file if rules changed
if [ $RULES_CHANGED -eq 1 ]; then
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Verify the configuration
echo "Verifying audit rules configuration..."
echo "Rules in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE:"
cat "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Currently loaded rules for delete_module:"
auditctl -l | grep delete_module || echo "No delete_module rules currently loaded (may require reboot if auditd is in immutable mode)"

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to configure rsyslog to forward all logs to remote loghost
# For RHEL 8 systems

set -e

RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR="/etc/rsyslog.d"
REMOTE_LOG_CONF="${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}/99-remote-log.conf"
REMOTE_HOST="loghost"
FORWARD_RULE="*.* @@${REMOTE_HOST}:514"

# Ensure rsyslog is installed
if ! rpm -q rsyslog &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y rsyslog
fi

# Ensure rsyslog.d directory exists
mkdir -p "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}"

# Check if the forwarding rule already exists in the main config or drop-in files
RULE_EXISTS=false

# Check main rsyslog.conf
if grep -qE "^\*\.\*[[:space:]]+@@${REMOTE_HOST}(:514)?$" /etc/rsyslog.conf 2>/dev/null; then
    RULE_EXISTS=true
fi

# Check existing drop-in files
for conf_file in "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}"/*.conf; do
    if [ -f "$conf_file" ]; then
        if grep -qE "^\*\.\*[[:space:]]+@@${REMOTE_HOST}(:514)?$" "$conf_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            RULE_EXISTS=true
            break
        fi
    fi
done

# If rule doesn't exist, create the configuration file
if [ "$RULE_EXISTS" = false ]; then
    cat > "${REMOTE_LOG_CONF}" << EOF
# Forward all log messages to centralized log server
# Using @@ for TCP (reliable) forwarding to port 514
${FORWARD_RULE}
EOF
    echo "Created ${REMOTE_LOG_CONF} with forwarding rule to ${REMOTE_HOST}"
    RESTART_NEEDED=true
else
    echo "Forwarding rule to ${REMOTE_HOST} already exists"
    RESTART_NEEDED=false
fi

# Ensure proper permissions on the config file if it exists
if [ -f "${REMOTE_LOG_CONF}" ]; then
    chmod 644 "${REMOTE_LOG_CONF}"
    chown root:root "${REMOTE_LOG_CONF}"
fi

# Enable and start rsyslog service
systemctl enable rsyslog

# Restart rsyslog if configuration was changed, otherwise just ensure it's running
if [ "$RESTART_NEEDED" = true ]; then
    systemctl restart rsyslog
    echo "rsyslog service restarted with new configuration"
else
    if ! systemctl is-active --quiet rsyslog; then
        systemctl start rsyslog
        echo "rsyslog service started"
    else

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to configure rsyslog to forward all logs to remote loghost
# For RHEL 8 systems

set -e

RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR="/etc/rsyslog.d"
REMOTE_LOG_CONF="${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}/99-remote-log.conf"
REMOTE_HOST="loghost"
FORWARD_RULE="*.* @@${REMOTE_HOST}:514"

# Ensure rsyslog is installed
if ! rpm -q rsyslog &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y rsyslog
fi

# Ensure rsyslog.d directory exists
mkdir -p "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}"

# Check if the forwarding rule already exists in the main config or drop-in files
RULE_EXISTS=false

# Check main rsyslog.conf
if grep -qE "^\*\.\*[[:space:]]+@@${REMOTE_HOST}(:514)?$" /etc/rsyslog.conf 2>/dev/null; then
    RULE_EXISTS=true
fi

# Check existing drop-in files
for conf_file in "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}"/*.conf; do
    if [ -f "$conf_file" ]; then
        if grep -qE "^\*\.\*[[:space:]]+@@${REMOTE_HOST}(:514)?$" "$conf_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            RULE_EXISTS=true
            break
        fi
    fi
done

# If rule doesn't exist, create the configuration file
if [ "$RULE_EXISTS" = false ]; then
    cat > "${REMOTE_LOG_CONF}" << EOF
# Forward all log messages to centralized log server
# Using @@ for TCP (reliable) forwarding to port 514
${FORWARD_RULE}
EOF
    echo "Created ${REMOTE_LOG_CONF} with forwarding rule to ${REMOTE_HOST}"
    RESTART_NEEDED=true
else
    echo "Forwarding rule to ${REMOTE_HOST} already exists"
    RESTART_NEEDED=false
fi

# Ensure proper permissions on the config file if it exists
if [ -f "${REMOTE_LOG_CONF}" ]; then
    chmod 644 "${REMOTE_LOG_CONF}"
    chown root:root "${REMOTE_LOG_CONF}"
fi

# Enable and start rsyslog service
systemctl enable rsyslog

# Restart rsyslog if configuration was changed, otherwise just ensure it's running
if [ "$RESTART_NEEDED" = true ]; then
    systemctl restart rsyslog
    echo "rsyslog service restarted with new configuration"
else
    if ! systemctl is-active --quiet rsyslog; then
        systemctl start rsyslog
        echo "rsyslog service started"
    else
        echo "rsyslog service is already running"
    fi
fi

echo "Log forwarding to ${REMOTE_HOST} is configured and active"

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Determine the audit rules directory based on the rule-loading mechanism
if [[ -d /etc/audit/rules.d ]]; then
    AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
    USE_RULES_D=true
else
    AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit"
    USE_RULES_D=false
fi

# Define the rules we need to add
# lremovexattr is the syscall that removes extended attributes from symbolic links
# We need rules for:
# 1. 64-bit syscalls (arch=b64) for unprivileged users (auid>=1000 and auid!=-1)
# 2. 64-bit syscalls (arch=b64) for root user (auid=0)
# 3. 32-bit syscalls (arch=b32) for unprivileged users (auid>=1000 and auid!=-1)
# 4. 32-bit syscalls (arch=b32) for root user (auid=0)

RULE_64_UNPRIV="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lremovexattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=perm_mod"
RULE_64_ROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lremovexattr -F auid=0 -F key=perm_mod"
RULE_32_UNPRIV="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S lremovexattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=perm_mod"
RULE_32_ROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S lremovexattr -F auid=0 -F key=perm_mod"

# Function to check if a rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        # Normalize whitespace and compare
        local normalized_rule
        normalized_rule=$(echo "$rule" | tr -s ' ')
        while IFS= read -r line; do
            local normalized_line
            normalized_line=$(echo "$line" | tr -s ' ' | sed 's/^[[:space:]]*//;s/[[:space:]]*$//')
            if [[ "$normalized_line" == "$normalized_rule" ]]; then
                return 0
            fi
        done < "$file"
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if a rule exists in any file in a directory
rule_exists_in_dir() {
    local rule="$1"
    local dir="$2"
    
    for file in "$dir"/*.rules 2>/dev/null; do
        if [[ -f "$file" ]] && rule_exists_in_file "$rule" "$file"; then
            return 0
        fi
    done
    return 1
}

# Function to add a rule if it doesn't exist
add_rule_if_missing() {
    local rule="$1"
    local rules_file="$2"
    
    if $USE_RULES_D; then
        if ! rule_exists_in_dir "$rule" "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"; then
            echo "$rule" >> "$rules_file"
        fi
    else
        if ! rule_exists_in_file "$rule" "$rules_file"; then
            echo "$rule" >> "$rules_file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Determine the target file for rules
if $USE_RULES_D; then
    # Use a dedicated file for these rules in rules.d
    RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/75-lremovexattr-perm_mod.rules"
    
    # Create the file if it doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
        touch "$RULES_FILE"
    fi
else
    # Use the main audit.rules file
    RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/audit.rules"
fi

# Add each rule if it doesn't already exist
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64_UNPRIV" "$RULES_FILE"
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64_ROOT" "$RULES_FILE"
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32_UNPRIV" "$RULES_FILE"
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32_ROOT" "$RULES_FILE"

# Load the rules into the running audit system
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null && $USE_RULES_D; then
    augenrules --load
elif command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    # Load rules directly using auditctl
    auditctl -R "$RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
if systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    # Service is running, try to reload if possible
    # Note: auditd doesn't support standard reload, rules are loaded above
    :
else
    # Start and enable the service
    systemctl enable auditd
    systemctl start auditd
fi

exit 0
