#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rules for lsetxattr system call
# Rules for 64-bit system calls
RULE_64_ROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lsetxattr -F auid=0 -F key=perm_mod"
RULE_64_USERS="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lsetxattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod"

# Rules for 32-bit system calls
RULE_32_ROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S lsetxattr -F auid=0 -F key=perm_mod"
RULE_32_USERS="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S lsetxattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod"

# Array of all rules
declare -a RULES=(
    "$RULE_64_ROOT"
    "$RULE_64_USERS"
    "$RULE_32_ROOT"
    "$RULE_32_USERS"
)

# Function to add rule to a file if not present
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    # Check if rule already exists in the file
    if ! grep -qxF "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
    fi
}

# Function to check if rule exists in any file in a directory
rule_exists_in_dir() {
    local rule="$1"
    local dir="$2"
    
    if [ -d "$dir" ]; then
        grep -rqxF "$rule" "$dir"/*.rules 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Determine which loading mechanism is in use and configure accordingly
# We'll configure for both mechanisms to ensure rules work regardless

# For augenrules mechanism - rules go in /etc/audit/rules.d/
RULES_D_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
RULES_FILE="${RULES_D_DIR}/30-lsetxattr-perm_mod.rules"

# For auditctl mechanism - rules go in /etc/audit/audit.rules
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Ensure the rules.d directory exists
mkdir -p "$RULES_D_DIR"

# Create or update the rules file for augenrules
# First, check if our rules file exists, if not create it
if [ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]; then
    touch "$RULES_FILE"
fi

# Add each rule to our rules file for augenrules
for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
    # Check if the rule already exists in any file in rules.d
    if ! rule_exists_in_dir "$rule" "$RULES_D_DIR"; then
        add_rule_to_file "$rule" "$RULES_FILE"
    fi
done

# Also add rules to /etc/audit/audit.rules for auditctl mechanism
# This file is used when auditctl loads rules directly
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
        if ! grep -qxF "$rule" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
            # Insert before any -e 2 line (which locks the rules) or at end
            if grep -q "^-e 2" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
                # Insert before -e 2
                sed -i "/^-e 2/i $rule" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
            else
                add_rule_to_file "$rule" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
            fi
        fi
    done
fi

# Set proper permissions on the rules files
chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null
chown root:root "$RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null

if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null
    chown root:root "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null
fi

# Reload the audit rules
# Try augenrules first (preferred on RHEL 8)
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null
elif command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    # Fall back to auditctl
    auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
if command -v systemctl &>/dev/null; then
    systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null
    # Note: auditd cannot be restarted normally, use service command or specific method
    if systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
        # auditd is running, rules have been loaded above
        :
    else
        systemctl start auditd 2>/dev/null
    fi
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Disable IPv6 packet forwarding for all interfaces
# This script is idempotent and ensures the setting persists across reboots

SYSCTL_PARAM="net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding"
DESIRED_VALUE="0"
SYSCTL_CONF_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-ipv6-forwarding.conf"

# Apply the setting at runtime
current_value=$(sysctl -n "${SYSCTL_PARAM}" 2>/dev/null)
if [ "$current_value" != "$DESIRED_VALUE" ]; then
    sysctl -w "${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${DESIRED_VALUE}"
fi

# Ensure the setting persists across reboots
# Check if the setting already exists in sysctl configuration files
found=0
for conf_file in /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do
    if [ -f "$conf_file" ]; then
        if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=" "$conf_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            # Setting exists, update it if needed
            current_conf_value=$(grep "^[[:space:]]*${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=" "$conf_file" | tail -1 | cut -d= -f2 | tr -d '[:space:]')
            if [ "$current_conf_value" != "$DESIRED_VALUE" ]; then
                sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=.*/${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}/" "$conf_file"
            fi
            found=1
        fi
    fi
done

# If no existing configuration found, create one
if [ "$found" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}" > "${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE}"
fi

# Verify the runtime setting
final_value=$(sysctl -n "${SYSCTL_PARAM}" 2>/dev/null)
if [ "$final_value" = "$DESIRED_VALUE" ]; then
    echo "IPv6 forwarding has been disabled successfully."
    exit 0
else
    echo "Failed to disable IPv6 forwarding."
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the authselect package is installed
if ! rpm -q authselect &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y authselect
fi

# Check if authselect is currently managing the configuration
if authselect check &>/dev/null; then
    # authselect is already managing the configuration
    # Verify a profile is selected
    current_profile=$(authselect current --raw 2>/dev/null)
    if [ -n "$current_profile" ]; then
        # A profile is already selected and active, configuration is valid
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# If we reach here, either authselect is not managing config or no profile is selected
# Select the minimal profile as a safe, supported default
# The 'minimal' profile is a standard RHEL 8 profile that provides basic local authentication

# First, try to select sssd profile which is commonly used and well-supported
# If that's not appropriate, fall back to minimal
if authselect list | grep -q "^- sssd"; then
    authselect select sssd --force 2>/dev/null
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Fall back to minimal profile which should always be available
if authselect list | grep -q "^- minimal"; then
    authselect select minimal --force 2>/dev/null
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# If minimal is not available, try the default profile
authselect select sssd --force 2>/dev/null || authselect select minimal --force 2>/dev/null

# Verify the configuration is now valid
if authselect check &>/dev/null; then
    exit 0
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to configure authselect properly" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# If that's not appropriate, fall back to minimal
if authselect list | grep -q "^- sssd"; then
    authselect select sssd --force 2>/dev/null
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Fall back to minimal profile which should always be available
if authselect list | grep -q "^- minimal"; then
    authselect select minimal --force 2>/dev/null
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# If minimal is not available, try the default profile
authselect select sssd --force 2>/dev/null || authselect select minimal --force 2>/dev/null

# Verify the configuration is now valid
if authselect check &>/dev/null; then
    exit 0
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to configure authselect properly" >&2
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to fully disable systemd-coredump service on RHEL 8

set -e

# The systemd unit responsible for core dump handling
COREDUMP_SOCKET="systemd-coredump.socket"

# Stop the socket if it's currently running
if systemctl is-active --quiet "$COREDUMP_SOCKET" 2>/dev/null; then
    systemctl stop "$COREDUMP_SOCKET"
fi

# Disable the socket to prevent it from starting on boot
if systemctl is-enabled --quiet "$COREDUMP_SOCKET" 2>/dev/null; then
    systemctl disable "$COREDUMP_SOCKET"
fi

# Mask the socket to prevent it from being started manually or by other services
# This is idempotent - masking an already masked unit is a no-op
systemctl mask "$COREDUMP_SOCKET"

# Also handle the systemd-coredump service itself
COREDUMP_SERVICE="systemd-coredump@.service"

# Mask the service template to prevent any instances from being started
systemctl mask "$COREDUMP_SERVICE"

# Configure the kernel to not produce core dumps by setting core pattern to nothing
# Create or update the sysctl configuration
SYSCTL_CONF="/etc/sysctl.d/50-coredump.conf"
KERNEL_CORE_PATTERN="kernel.core_pattern=|/bin/false"

if [ -f "$SYSCTL_CONF" ]; then
    if ! grep -qF "$KERNEL_CORE_PATTERN" "$SYSCTL_CONF"; then
        # Update existing file if the setting is different
        sed -i '/^kernel.core_pattern/d' "$SYSCTL_CONF"
        echo "$KERNEL_CORE_PATTERN" >> "$SYSCTL_CONF"
    fi
else
    echo "$KERNEL_CORE_PATTERN" > "$SYSCTL_CONF"
fi

# Apply the sysctl setting immediately
sysctl -w kernel.core_pattern="|/bin/false" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

# Disable core dumps via limits.conf
LIMITS_CONF="/etc/security/limits.d/50-coredump.conf"
LIMITS_CONTENT="* hard core 0
* soft core 0"

if [ ! -f "$LIMITS_CONF" ] || ! grep -qF "* hard core 0" "$LIMITS_CONF"; then
    echo "$LIMITS_CONTENT" > "$LIMITS_CONF"
fi

# Update systemd coredump configuration to disable processing
COREDUMP_CONF="/etc/systemd/coredump.conf"
COREDUMP_CONF_D="/etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d"

# Create coredump.conf.d directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "$COREDUMP_CONF_D"

# Create a drop-in configuration to disable coredump storage
COREDUMP_DROPIN="$COREDUMP_CONF_D/disable-coredump.conf"
DROPIN_CONTENT="[Coredump]
Storage=none
ProcessSizeMax=0"

if [ ! -f "$COREDUMP_DROPIN" ] || ! grep -qF "Storage=none" "$COREDUMP_DROPIN"; then
    echo "$DROPIN_CONTENT" > "$COREDUMP_DROPIN"
fi

# Reload systemd to pick up any configuration changes
systemctl daemon-reload

echo "Core dump handling has been fully disabled."
echo "- systemd-coredump.socket: masked"
echo "- systemd-coredump@.service: masked"
echo "- kernel.core_pattern: set to /bin/false"
echo "- Core dump limits: set to 0"
echo "- systemd coredump storage: disabled"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure sudo to require invoking user's password
# and disable rootpw, targetpw, and runaspw options on RHEL 8

set -euo pipefail

SUDOERS_DIR="/etc/sudoers.d"
CONFIG_FILE="${SUDOERS_DIR}/00-require-user-password"

# Ensure sudoers.d directory exists
if [[ ! -d "${SUDOERS_DIR}" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "${SUDOERS_DIR}"
    chmod 750 "${SUDOERS_DIR}"
fi

# Define the desired configuration content
# These settings ensure:
# - !rootpw: Do NOT prompt for root's password
# - !targetpw: Do NOT prompt for target user's password
# - !runaspw: Do NOT prompt for run-as user's password
# By default, sudo prompts for the invoking user's password, which is what we want
DESIRED_CONFIG="# Ensure sudo prompts for the invoking user's password only
# Disable prompting for root password
Defaults !rootpw
# Disable prompting for target user's password
Defaults !targetpw
# Disable prompting for run-as user's password
Defaults !runaspw"

# Function to validate sudoers syntax
validate_sudoers() {
    local file="$1"
    if visudo -c -f "${file}" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        return 0
    else
        return 1
    fi
}

# Check if the configuration file already exists and has correct content
if [[ -f "${CONFIG_FILE}" ]]; then
    CURRENT_CONFIG=$(cat "${CONFIG_FILE}")
    if [[ "${CURRENT_CONFIG}" == "${DESIRED_CONFIG}" ]]; then
        echo "Configuration already in place. No changes needed."
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Create a temporary file for the new configuration
TEMP_FILE=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "${TEMP_FILE}"' EXIT

echo "${DESIRED_CONFIG}" > "${TEMP_FILE}"

# Validate the syntax before applying
if ! validate_sudoers "${TEMP_FILE}"; then
    echo "ERROR: Generated sudoers configuration has syntax errors." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Apply the configuration
cp "${TEMP_FILE}" "${CONFIG_FILE}"
chmod 440 "${CONFIG_FILE}"
chown root:root "${CONFIG_FILE}"

# Verify the entire sudoers configuration is still valid
if ! visudo -c >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "ERROR: Overall sudoers configuration has syntax errors after applying changes." >&2
    rm -f "${CONFIG_FILE}"
    exit 1
fi

# Also check and remove conflicting settings from main sudoers file if present
# We'll remove any lines that set rootpw, targetpw, or runaspw (without the !)
MAIN_SUDOERS="/etc/sudoers"
if grep -qE '^\s*Defaults\s+(rootpw|targetpw|runaspw)' "${MAIN_SUDOERS}" 2>/dev/null; then
    # Create backup
    cp "${MAIN_SUDOERS}" "${MAIN_SUDOERS}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
    
    # Create temp file for modified sudoers
    TEMP_SUDOERS=$(mktemp)
    
    # Remove conflicting lines (those that enable rootpw, targetpw, or runaspw)
    sed -E '/^\s*Defaults\s+(rootpw|targetpw|runaspw)/d' "${MAIN_SUDOERS}" > "${TEMP_SUDOERS}"
    
    # Validate before applying
    if validate_sudoers "${TEMP_SUDOERS}"; then
        cp "${TEMP_SUDOERS}" "${MAIN_SUDOERS}"
        chmod 440 "${MAIN_SUDOERS}"
        echo "Removed conflicting settings from main sudoers file."
    else
        echo "WARNING: Could not safely modify main sudoers file." >&2
    fi
    
    rm -f "${TEMP_SUDOERS}"
fi

# Final validation
if visudo -c >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "Successfully configured sudo to require invoking user's password only."
    echo "Configuration applied to: ${CONFIG_FILE}"
    exit 0
else
    echo "ERROR: Final validation failed." >&2
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent remediation script to audit chacl execution
# Ensures audit rules persist across reboots for both compiled and direct-load methods

set -e

# Define the audit rules for chacl
# Rule for regular users (auid >= 1000)
RULE_USER="-a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/chacl -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"
# Rule for root (auid = 0)
RULE_ROOT="-a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/chacl -F perm=x -F auid=0 -k perm_mod"

# Patterns for matching existing rules (for idempotency checks)
PATTERN_USER="^-a always,exit.*path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid>=1000.*auid!=(-1|unset|4294967295).*-k perm_mod"
PATTERN_ROOT="^-a always,exit.*path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid=0.*-k perm_mod"

# Function to check if a rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local pattern="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -qE "$pattern" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if rule is loaded in running audit system
rule_loaded() {
    local pattern="$1"
    auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -qE "$pattern"
    return $?
}

# Function to add rule to a file if not present
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local pattern="$2"
    local file="$3"
    
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$pattern" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        echo "Added rule to $file"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Rule already exists in $file"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Ensure audit package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit package..."
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure auditd is enabled and running
systemctl enable auditd
if ! systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Determine the rules directory
RULES_DIR=""
if [[ -d /etc/audit/rules.d ]]; then
    RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
fi

# Handle compiled rules method (rules.d directory)
if [[ -n "$RULES_DIR" ]]; then
    RULES_FILE="$RULES_DIR/30-perm_mod.rules"
    
    # Create the file if it doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
        touch "$RULES_FILE"
        chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    RULES_CHANGED=0
    
    # Add user rule if not present
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$PATTERN_USER" "$RULES_FILE"; then
        # Check other files in rules.d for this rule
        FOUND=0
        for f in "$RULES_DIR"/*.rules; do
            if [[ -f "$f" ]] && rule_exists_in_file "$PATTERN_USER" "$f"; then
                FOUND=1
                echo "User rule already exists in $f"
                break
            fi
        done
        if [[ $FOUND -eq 0 ]]; then
            echo "$RULE_USER" >> "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "Added user rule to $RULES_FILE"
            RULES_CHANGED=1
        fi
    else
        echo "User rule already exists in $RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Add root rule if not present
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$PATTERN_ROOT" "$RULES_FILE"; then
        # Check other files in rules.d for this rule
        FOUND=0
        for f in "$RULES_DIR"/*.rules; do
            if [[ -f "$f" ]] && rule_exists_in_file "$PATTERN_ROOT" "$f"; then
                FOUND=1
                echo "Root rule already exists in $f"
                break
            fi
        done
        if [[ $FOUND -eq 0 ]]; then
            echo "$RULE_ROOT" >> "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "Added root rule to $RULES_FILE"
            RULES_CHANGED=1
        fi
    else
        echo "Root rule already exists in $RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Regenerate compiled rules if changes were made
    if [[ $RULES_CHANGED -eq 1 ]]; then
        if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
            echo "Regenerating audit rules with augenrules..."
            augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi
fi

# Handle direct-load method (audit.rules file)
AUDIT_RULES="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
if [[ -f "$AUDIT_RULES" ]]; then
    DIRECT_CHANGED=0
    
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$PATTERN_USER" "$AUDIT_RULES"; then
        echo "$RULE_USER" >> "$AUDIT_RULES"
        echo "Added user rule to $AUDIT_RULES"
        DIRECT_CHANGED=1
    else
        echo "User rule already exists in $AUDIT_RULES"
    fi
    
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$PATTERN_ROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES"; then
        echo "$RULE_ROOT" >> "$AUDIT_RULES"
        echo "Added root rule to $AUDIT_RULES"
        DIRECT_CHANGED=1
    else
        echo "Root rule already exists in $AUDIT_RULES"
    fi
fi

# Load rules into running kernel if not already loaded
KERNEL_LOAD_NEEDED=0

if ! rule_loaded "path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid>=1000"; then
    KERNEL_LOAD_NEEDED=1
fi

if ! rule_loaded "path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid=0"; then
    KERNEL_LOAD_NEEDED=1
fi

if [[ $KERNEL_LOAD_NEEDED -eq 1 ]]; then
    echo "Loading rules into running audit system..."
    
    # Add rules directly to running system
    if ! rule_loaded "path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid>=1000"; then
        auditctl $RULE_USER 2>/dev/null || true
        echo "Loaded user rule into kernel"
    fi
    
    if ! rule_loaded "path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid=0"; then
        auditctl $RULE_ROOT 2>/dev/null || true
        echo "Loaded root rule into kernel"
    fi
else
    echo "Rules already loaded in running audit system"
fi

echo "Audit rules for chacl have been configured successfully."
echo "Verifying loaded rules:"
auditctl -l | grep -E "chacl" || echo "Note: Rules may require auditd restart to appear"

exit 0

                FOUND=1
                echo "Root rule already exists in $f"
                break
            fi
        done
        if [[ $FOUND -eq 0 ]]; then
            echo "$RULE_ROOT" >> "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "Added root rule to $RULES_FILE"
            RULES_CHANGED=1
        fi
    else
        echo "Root rule already exists in $RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Regenerate compiled rules if changes were made
    if [[ $RULES_CHANGED -eq 1 ]]; then
        if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
            echo "Regenerating audit rules with augenrules..."
            augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi
fi

# Handle direct-load method (audit.rules file)
AUDIT_RULES="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
if [[ -f "$AUDIT_RULES" ]]; then
    DIRECT_CHANGED=0
    
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$PATTERN_USER" "$AUDIT_RULES"; then
        echo "$RULE_USER" >> "$AUDIT_RULES"
        echo "Added user rule to $AUDIT_RULES"
        DIRECT_CHANGED=1
    else
        echo "User rule already exists in $AUDIT_RULES"
    fi
    
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$PATTERN_ROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES"; then
        echo "$RULE_ROOT" >> "$AUDIT_RULES"
        echo "Added root rule to $AUDIT_RULES"
        DIRECT_CHANGED=1
    else
        echo "Root rule already exists in $AUDIT_RULES"
    fi
fi

# Load rules into running kernel if not already loaded
KERNEL_LOAD_NEEDED=0

if ! rule_loaded "path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid>=1000"; then
    KERNEL_LOAD_NEEDED=1
fi

if ! rule_loaded "path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid=0"; then
    KERNEL_LOAD_NEEDED=1
fi

if [[ $KERNEL_LOAD_NEEDED -eq 1 ]]; then
    echo "Loading rules into running audit system..."
    
    # Add rules directly to running system
    if ! rule_loaded "path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid>=1000"; then
        auditctl $RULE_USER 2>/dev/null || true
        echo "Loaded user rule into kernel"
    fi
    
    if ! rule_loaded "path=/usr/bin/chacl.*auid=0"; then
        auditctl $RULE_ROOT 2>/dev/null || true
        echo "Loaded root rule into kernel"
    fi
else
    echo "Rules already loaded in running audit system"
fi

echo "Audit rules for chacl have been configured successfully."
echo "Verifying loaded rules:"
auditctl -l | grep -E "chacl" || echo "Note: Rules may require auditd restart to appear"

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to disable core dumps on RHEL 8

set -e

# 1. Configure /etc/security/limits.conf to prevent core dumps
LIMITS_FILE="/etc/security/limits.conf"
LIMITS_ENTRY="* hard core 0"

if ! grep -q "^\* hard core 0$" "$LIMITS_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    # Remove any existing "* hard core" lines to avoid duplicates
    sed -i '/^\*[[:space:]]\+hard[[:space:]]\+core/d' "$LIMITS_FILE"
    echo "$LIMITS_ENTRY" >> "$LIMITS_FILE"
fi

# 2. Configure /etc/security/limits.d/ to ensure setting persists
LIMITS_D_FILE="/etc/security/limits.d/99-disable-coredumps.conf"
if [ ! -f "$LIMITS_D_FILE" ] || ! grep -q "^\* hard core 0$" "$LIMITS_D_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "* hard core 0" > "$LIMITS_D_FILE"
fi

# 3. Configure sysctl to disable core dumps via kernel parameter
SYSCTL_CONF="/etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-coredumps.conf"
SYSCTL_ENTRY="kernel.core_pattern = |/bin/false"

if [ ! -f "$SYSCTL_CONF" ] || ! grep -q "^kernel.core_pattern = |/bin/false$" "$SYSCTL_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "$SYSCTL_ENTRY" > "$SYSCTL_CONF"
fi

# 4. Also set fs.suid_dumpable to 0 to prevent setuid programs from dumping core
SUID_ENTRY="fs.suid_dumpable = 0"
if ! grep -q "^fs.suid_dumpable = 0$" "$SYSCTL_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "$SUID_ENTRY" >> "$SYSCTL_CONF"
fi

# 5. Apply sysctl settings immediately
sysctl -w kernel.core_pattern="|/bin/false" >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
sysctl -w fs.suid_dumpable=0 >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

# 6. Reload sysctl configuration
sysctl --system >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

# 7. Configure systemd to disable core dumps
SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF="/etc/systemd/coredump.conf"
if [ -f "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF" ]; then
    # Ensure Storage=none is set
    if grep -q "^Storage=" "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"; then
        sed -i 's/^Storage=.*/Storage=none/' "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"
    elif grep -q "^#Storage=" "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"; then
        sed -i 's/^#Storage=.*/Storage=none/' "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"
    else
        echo "Storage=none" >> "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"
    fi
    
    # Ensure ProcessSizeMax=0 is set
    if grep -q "^ProcessSizeMax=" "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"; then
        sed -i 's/^ProcessSizeMax=.*/ProcessSizeMax=0/' "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"
    elif grep -q "^#ProcessSizeMax=" "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"; then
        sed -i 's/^#ProcessSizeMax=.*/ProcessSizeMax=0/' "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"
    else
        echo "ProcessSizeMax=0" >> "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF"
    fi
else
    # Create the file with proper settings
    mkdir -p /etc/systemd
    cat > "$SYSTEMD_COREDUMP_CONF" << EOF
[Coredump]
Storage=none
ProcessSizeMax=0
EOF
fi

# 8. Create systemd drop-in to set soft limit for all services
SYSTEMD_SYSTEM_CONF_D="/etc/systemd/system.conf.d"
mkdir -p "$SYSTEMD_SYSTEM_CONF_D"
SYSTEMD_CORELIMIT_CONF="$SYSTEMD_SYSTEM_CONF_D/99-disable-coredumps.conf"

if [ ! -f "$SYSTEMD_CORELIMIT_CONF" ] || ! grep -q "^DefaultLimitCORE=0$" "$SYSTEMD_CORELIMIT_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    cat > "$SYSTEMD_CORELIMIT_CONF" << EOF
[Manager]
DefaultLimitCORE=0
EOF
fi

# 9. Reload systemd daemon to pick up configuration changes
systemctl daemon-reload >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

# 10. Apply ulimit for current shell session (for any subsequent processes)
ulimit -c 0 2>/dev/null || true

echo "Core dumps have been disabled successfully."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to configure audit rules for /etc/shadow monitoring on RHEL 8

set -euo pipefail

SHADOW_FILE="/etc/shadow"
AUDIT_RULE="-w /etc/shadow -p wa -k user_group_modification"
RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
RULES_FILE="${RULES_DIR}/shadow-audit.rules"

# Ensure audit package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
systemctl enable auditd
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Function to check if the rule already exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        # Check for equivalent rule (handling different whitespace)
        grep -qE "^-w[[:space:]]+/etc/shadow[[:space:]]+-p[[:space:]]+wa[[:space:]]+-k[[:space:]]+user_group_modification" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if rule is currently loaded in kernel
rule_loaded_in_kernel() {
    auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -qE "^-w[[:space:]]+/etc/shadow[[:space:]]+-p[[:space:]]+wa[[:space:]]+-k[[:space:]]+user_group_modification"
    return $?
}

# Ensure rules directory exists
mkdir -p "${RULES_DIR}"

# Check if rule already exists in any rules.d file
rule_in_rules_d=false
for f in "${RULES_DIR}"/*.rules; do
    if [[ -f "$f" ]] && rule_exists_in_file "$f"; then
        rule_in_rules_d=true
        break
    fi
done

# Add rule to rules.d if not present
if [[ "$rule_in_rules_d" == "false" ]]; then
    echo "$AUDIT_RULE" >> "$RULES_FILE"
    chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE"
fi

# Also ensure rule exists in /etc/audit/audit.rules for systems using auditctl directly
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
if [[ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        # Add before any -e 2 line if present, otherwise append
        if grep -q "^-e 2" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
            sed -i "/^-e 2/i $AUDIT_RULE" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        else
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        fi
    fi
fi

# Load the rule into the running kernel if not already present
if ! rule_loaded_in_kernel; then
    # Check if audit rules are locked
    if auditctl -s 2>/dev/null | grep -q "enabled 2"; then
        echo "Warning: Audit rules are locked. Rule will be active after reboot."
    else
        auditctl -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k user_group_modification || {
            echo "Warning: Could not load rule into running kernel. Rule will be active after reboot."
        }
    fi
fi

# Regenerate rules using augenrules if available
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
fi

echo "Audit rule for /etc/shadow configured successfully."
exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to disable direct root login via SSH on RHEL 8

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
DROP_IN_FILE="${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/01-permitrootlogin.conf"

# Ensure the script is run as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Function to remove PermitRootLogin from main sshd_config if it exists
remove_from_main_config() {
    if grep -Eq '^\s*PermitRootLogin\s+' "$SSHD_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Comment out any existing PermitRootLogin directives in main config
        sed -i 's/^\s*PermitRootLogin\s/# PermitRootLogin /' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    fi
}

# Function to set PermitRootLogin in drop-in directory (RHEL 8 best practice)
configure_drop_in() {
    # Create the sshd_config.d directory if it doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -d "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR" ]]; then
        mkdir -p "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"
        chmod 755 "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"
    fi

    # Check if Include directive exists in main config for drop-in files
    if ! grep -Eq '^\s*Include\s+/etc/ssh/sshd_config\.d/\*\.conf' "$SSHD_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Add Include directive at the beginning of the file if not present
        # This is the RHEL 8 default but we ensure it exists
        if ! grep -q 'Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf' "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
            sed -i '1i Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
        fi
    fi

    # Create or update the drop-in configuration file
    echo "PermitRootLogin no" > "$DROP_IN_FILE"
    chmod 600 "$DROP_IN_FILE"
}

# Function to configure directly in main config as fallback
configure_main_config() {
    if grep -Eq '^\s*PermitRootLogin\s+' "$SSHD_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Replace existing PermitRootLogin directive
        sed -i 's/^\s*PermitRootLogin\s.*$/PermitRootLogin no/' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    elif grep -Eq '^\s*#\s*PermitRootLogin\s+' "$SSHD_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Uncomment and set to no
        sed -i 's/^\s*#\s*PermitRootLogin\s.*$/PermitRootLogin no/' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    else
        # Add the directive if it doesn't exist at all
        echo "PermitRootLogin no" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    fi
}

# Main configuration logic
# RHEL 8 uses drop-in configuration by default, but we handle both cases

# Remove any conflicting directives from the main config
remove_from_main_config

# Use drop-in configuration (preferred method for RHEL 8)
configure_drop_in

# Also ensure it's set in main config as a backup
configure_main_config

# Validate the SSH configuration
if sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "SSH configuration is valid."
else
    echo "ERROR: SSH configuration validation failed!" >&2
    sshd -t
    exit 1
fi

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
    systemctl reload sshd 2>/dev/null || systemctl restart sshd
    echo "SSH service reloaded successfully."
else
    systemctl enable --now sshd
    echo "SSH service enabled and started."
fi

# Verify the configuration is active
CURRENT_SETTING=$(sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep -i "^permitrootlogin" | awk '{print $2}')
if [[ "$CURRENT_SETTING" == "no" ]]; then
    echo "SUCCESS: PermitRootLogin is set to 'no'. Direct root SSH login is disabled."
    exit 0
else
    echo "WARNING: PermitRootLogin is currently set to '$CURRENT_SETTING'." >&2
    echo "Please verify the configuration manually." >&2
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Get list of local interactive users (UID >= 1000 and < 65534, with valid shell)
while IFS=: read -r username _ uid _ _ home shell; do
    # Skip system users (UID < 1000) and nobody (65534)
    if [[ $uid -lt 1000 ]] || [[ $uid -eq 65534 ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Skip users with nologin or false shells
    if [[ "$shell" == */nologin ]] || [[ "$shell" == */false ]] || [[ "$shell" == "" ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Skip if home directory doesn't exist or is not a directory
    if [[ ! -d "$home" ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Process non-hidden files and directories in the user's home directory
    # Find all non-hidden items (not starting with .) directly under home and recursively
    # but exclude hidden directories and their contents
    find "$home" -mindepth 1 \( -name ".*" -prune \) -o \( -type f -print \) -o \( -type d -print \) 2>/dev/null | while read -r item; do
        # Skip if item doesn't exist (race condition protection)
        if [[ ! -e "$item" ]]; then
            continue
        fi
        
        # Get current permissions
        current_mode=$(stat -c "%a" "$item" 2>/dev/null)
        if [[ -z "$current_mode" ]]; then
            continue
        fi
        
        # Convert to numeric for comparison
        owner_perm=$((current_mode / 100))
        group_perm=$(((current_mode / 10) % 10))
        other_perm=$((current_mode % 10))
        
        # Calculate new permissions
        new_owner_perm=$owner_perm
        new_group_perm=$group_perm
        new_other_perm=0
        
        # Owner should be no more than rwx (7)
        if [[ $new_owner_perm -gt 7 ]]; then
            new_owner_perm=7
        fi
        
        # Group should be no more than r-x (5)
        # Remove write permission if present
        if [[ $((new_group_perm & 2)) -ne 0 ]]; then
            new_group_perm=$((new_group_perm & 5))
        fi
        # Ensure group is no more than 5
        if [[ $new_group_perm -gt 5 ]]; then
            new_group_perm=5
        fi
        
        # Other should be 0
        new_other_perm=0
        
        # Calculate new mode
        new_mode=$((new_owner_perm * 100 + new_group_perm * 10 + new_other_perm))
        
        # Apply new permissions if they changed
        if [[ "$current_mode" != "$new_mode" ]]; then
            chmod "$new_mode" "$item" 2>/dev/null
        fi
    done
    
done < /etc/passwd

# Idempotent script to enforce at least one lowercase character in passwords
# on RHEL 8 systems using pwquality configuration

PWQUALITY_CONF="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"

# Ensure the pwquality.conf file exists
if [[ ! -f "$PWQUALITY_CONF" ]]; then
    touch "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
fi

# Check if lcredit is already configured
if grep -q "^lcredit" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    # Update existing lcredit setting to require at least 1 lowercase character
    # lcredit = -1 means at least 1 lowercase character is required
    sed -i 's/^lcredit[[:space:]]*=.*/lcredit = -1/' "$PWQUALITY_CONF"

    # Uncomment and set the value if it exists as a comment
    sed -i 's/^#[[:space:]]*lcredit[[:space:]]*=.*/lcredit = -1/' "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
else
    # Add the lcredit setting if it doesn't exist
    echo "lcredit = -1" >> "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
fi

# Verify the change was applied
if grep -q "^lcredit[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*-1" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    echo "Password quality policy updated: at least 1 lowercase character required"
    exit 0
else
    echo "Failed to update password quality policy"
    exit 1
fi

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rule for unix_update privileged command
# on RHEL 8 systems

# Define the rule file and the rule content
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
RULE_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/privileged-unix_update.rules"
UNIX_UPDATE_PATH="/usr/sbin/unix_update"

# Check if unix_update exists
if [ ! -f "${UNIX_UPDATE_PATH}" ]; then
    # Try to find it in case it's in a different location
    UNIX_UPDATE_PATH=$(find /usr -name "unix_update" -type f 2>/dev/null | head -1)
    if [ -z "${UNIX_UPDATE_PATH}" ]; then
        echo "Warning: unix_update not found on this system. Creating rule for default path /usr/sbin/unix_update"
        UNIX_UPDATE_PATH="/usr/sbin/unix_update"
    fi
fi

# Define the audit rule
# -a always,exit: Always generate audit record on syscall exit
# -F path=: Monitor this specific file
# -F perm=x: Monitor execution
# -F auid>=1000: Only for users with login UID >= 1000 (non-system users)
# -F auid!=unset: Exclude processes without a login UID set (system services)
# -k privileged: Tag with key "privileged" for easy searching
AUDIT_RULE="-a always,exit -F path=${UNIX_UPDATE_PATH} -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k privileged"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
if [ ! -d "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}" ]; then
    mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"
fi

# Check if the rule already exists in the rule file
if [ -f "${RULE_FILE}" ]; then
    # Check if exact rule already exists
    if grep -qF "${AUDIT_RULE}" "${RULE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Audit rule for unix_update already exists in ${RULE_FILE}"
    else
        # Rule file exists but doesn't contain our exact rule
        # Check if there's any unix_update rule and update it
        if grep -q "unix_update" "${RULE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
            # Replace existing unix_update rule with our rule
            sed -i "/unix_update/d" "${RULE_FILE}"
        fi
        echo "${AUDIT_RULE}" >> "${RULE_FILE}"
        echo "Updated audit rule for unix_update in ${RULE_FILE}"
    fi
else
    # Create the rule file with the rule
    echo "${AUDIT_RULE}" > "${RULE_FILE}"
    echo "Created audit rule for unix_update in ${RULE_FILE}"
fi

# Set proper permissions on the rule file
chmod 640 "${RULE_FILE}"
chown root:root "${RULE_FILE}"

# Check if the rule is already loaded in the running audit configuration
RUNNING_RULES=$(auditctl -l 2>/dev/null)
if echo "${RUNNING_RULES}" | grep -qF "path=${UNIX_UPDATE_PATH}"; then
    # Check if it matches our exact criteria
    if echo "${RUNNING_RULES}" | grep -q "path=${UNIX_UPDATE_PATH}.*perm=x.*auid>=1000.*auid!=-1.*key=privileged"; then
        echo "Audit rule for unix_update is already active in running configuration"
    else
        # Remove old rule and add new one
        auditctl -d always,exit -F path="${UNIX_UPDATE_PATH}" 2>/dev/null
        auditctl ${AUDIT_RULE}
        echo "Updated running audit rule for unix_update"
    fi
else
    # Load the rule into the running configuration
    auditctl ${AUDIT_RULE}
    echo "Loaded audit rule for unix_update into running configuration"
fi

# Verify the rule is loaded
echo ""
echo "Verification - Current audit rules for unix_update:"
auditctl -l | grep -i unix_update || echo "No running rules found for unix_update"

echo ""
echo "Verification - Persistent rule file content:"
cat "${RULE_FILE}"

# Optionally regenerate the audit.rules file if augenrules is used
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null
    echo ""
    echo "Regenerated audit rules using augenrules"
fi

echo ""
echo "Audit configuration for privileged unix_update command completed successfully."
echo "The rule will persist across reboots via ${RULE_FILE}"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rules for unsuccessful file open attempts
# by regular users (UID >= 1000) on RHEL 8

set -e

AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/access.rules"

# Define the audit rules we need to add
# These rules capture failed open/openat/open_by_handle_at syscalls for users with auid >= 1000
# Failure codes: EACCES (permission denied) = 13, EPERM (operation not permitted) = 1

RULES=(
    # 32-bit syscalls
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S open -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S open -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S openat -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S openat -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S open_by_handle_at -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S open_by_handle_at -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    # 64-bit syscalls
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S open -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S open -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S openat -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S openat -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S open_by_handle_at -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S open_by_handle_at -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access"
)

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d

# Create or update the rules file
# We'll rebuild it completely to ensure idempotency
{
    echo "## Audit rules for unsuccessful file access attempts"
    echo "## Auto-generated - do not edit manually"
    echo ""
    for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
        echo "$rule"
    done
} > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
chown root:root "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Regenerate the main audit rules from all .rules files in rules.d
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    augenrules --load
else
    # Fallback: manually load rules if augenrules is not available
    auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
systemctl enable auditd
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    # Service is running, rules were loaded by augenrules above
    # Force a reload of rules to ensure they're active
    service auditd restart 2>/dev/null || systemctl restart auditd 2>/dev/null || true
else
    systemctl start auditd
fi

echo "Audit rules for file access monitoring have been configured successfully."
echo "Rules file: $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
echo "Rules will persist across reboots."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure auditing for fremovexattr system call
# This monitors removal of extended attributes which can affect DAC

set -e

# Define the audit rules
# fremovexattr is the system call that removes extended attributes from files
# We need rules for:
# 1. 32-bit syscalls (arch=b32) for UID >= 1000
# 2. 64-bit syscalls (arch=b64) for UID >= 1000
# 3. 32-bit syscalls (arch=b32) for UID = 0 (root)
# 4. 64-bit syscalls (arch=b64) for UID = 0 (root)

RULE_KEY="perm_mod"

# Rules for users with auid >= 1000 (regular users)
RULE_32_AUID1000="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fremovexattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${RULE_KEY}"
RULE_64_AUID1000="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fremovexattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${RULE_KEY}"

# Rules for root (auid=0)
RULE_32_ROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fremovexattr -F auid=0 -k ${RULE_KEY}"
RULE_64_ROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fremovexattr -F auid=0 -k ${RULE_KEY}"

add_rule_to_file() {

AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64_AUID1000" "$PERM_MOD_RULES_FILE"

add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64_ROOT" "$PERM_MOD_RULES_FILE"

add_rule_to_kernel "$RULE_64_ROOT"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure SSH StrictModes on RHEL 8
# This ensures SSH verifies ownership and permissions of user's home directory
# and SSH-related files before accepting login

set -e

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
DROP_IN_FILE="${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/99-strict-modes.conf"

# Function to check if running as root
check_root() {
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Function to backup sshd_config if not already backed up
backup_config() {
    if [[ ! -f "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak" ]]; then
        cp -p "${SSHD_CONFIG}" "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak"
    fi
}

# Function to remove any existing StrictModes settings from main config
# to avoid conflicts with drop-in file
remove_existing_strictmodes() {
    if grep -qE "^\s*StrictModes" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
        sed -i '/^\s*StrictModes/d' "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
    fi
}

# Function to create drop-in directory if it doesn't exist
create_dropin_dir() {
    if [[ ! -d "${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}" ]]; then
        mkdir -p "${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}"
        chmod 700 "${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}"
    fi
}

# Function to ensure Include directive exists in main config
ensure_include_directive() {
    if ! grep -qE "^\s*Include\s+${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/\*\.conf" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
        # Check if any Include directive exists
        if grep -qE "^\s*Include" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
            # Add our include if not already present
            if ! grep -qE "^\s*Include\s+/etc/ssh/sshd_config\.d/\*\.conf" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
                # Insert at the beginning after any initial comments
                sed -i '1,/^[^#]/ { /^[^#]/i Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf
                }' "${SSHD_CONFIG}" 2>/dev/null || \
                sed -i '1i Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf' "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
            fi
        else
            # No Include directive exists, add one at the beginning
            sed -i '1i Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf' "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to configure StrictModes via drop-in file
configure_strictmodes() {
    local current_setting=""
    
    # Check if drop-in file exists and has correct setting
    if [[ -f "${DROP_IN_FILE}" ]]; then
        current_setting=$(grep -E "^\s*StrictModes\s+yes" "${DROP_IN_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || true)
    fi
    
    if [[ -z "${current_setting}" ]]; then
        # Create or update the drop-in file
        cat > "${DROP_IN_FILE}" << 'EOF'
# Enforce strict checking of file modes and ownership of the user's files
# and home directory before accepting login
# This prevents attacks where an attacker modifies another user's SSH files
StrictModes yes
EOF
        chmod 600 "${DROP_IN_FILE}"
        echo "StrictModes has been set to 'yes' in ${DROP_IN_FILE}"
    else
        echo "StrictModes is already set to 'yes' in ${DROP_IN_FILE}"
    fi
}

# Function to validate sshd configuration
validate_config() {
    if ! sshd -t; then
        echo "ERROR: SSH configuration validation failed" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    echo "SSH configuration validated successfully"
}

# Function to restart sshd service
restart_sshd() {
    if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
        systemctl reload sshd || systemctl restart sshd
        echo "SSHD service reloaded/restarted successfully"
    else
        systemctl start sshd
        echo "SSHD service started"
    fi
}

# Function to ensure sshd is enabled for persistence across reboots
enable_sshd() {
    if ! systemctl is-enabled --quiet sshd; then
        systemctl enable sshd
        echo "SSHD service enabled for persistence across reboots"
    else
        echo "SSHD service is already enabled"
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Starting SSH StrictModes configuration on RHEL 8..."
    
    check_root
    backup_config
    create_dropin_dir
    ensure_include_directive
    remove_existing_strictmodes
    configure_strictmodes
    validate_config
    restart_sshd
    enable_sshd
    
    echo ""
    echo "Configuration complete. StrictModes is now enforced."
    echo "SSH will verify ownership and permissions of user home directories"
    echo "and SSH-related files before accepting authentication."
    
    # Verify the setting is active
    echo ""
    echo "Verifying active configuration:"
    sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep -i strictmodes || echo "Unable to query running config"
}

main "$@"

# Remediation script for auditing fchmodat syscall for users with UID >= 1000
# This script is idempotent and works with both auditctl and augenrules

set -e

AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/perm_mod.rules"
AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Define the audit rules for fchmodat syscall
RULE_32BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"
RULE_64BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"

# Function to check if a rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Normalize whitespace for comparison
    local normalized_rule=$(echo "$rule" | tr -s ' ')
    
    while IFS= read -r line; do
        # Skip comments and empty lines
        [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] && continue
        [[ -z "${line// }" ]] && continue
        
        local normalized_line=$(echo "$line" | tr -s ' ')
        if [[ "$normalized_line" == "$normalized_rule" ]]; then
            return 0
        fi
    done < "$file"
    
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule to a file if it doesn't exist
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$rule" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Ensure audit rules directory exists
if [[ ! -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"
fi

# Track if any changes were made
CHANGES_MADE=0

# For augenrules-based systems: add rules to /etc/audit/rules.d/
# Create the rules file if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Add 32-bit rule
if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_32BIT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    echo "Added 32-bit fchmodat audit rule to $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    CHANGES_MADE=1
else
    echo "32-bit fchmodat audit rule already exists in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Add 64-bit rule
if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64BIT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    echo "Added 64-bit fchmodat audit rule to $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    CHANGES_MADE=1
else
    echo "64-bit fchmodat audit rule already exists in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# For auditctl-based systems: also update /etc/audit/audit.rules if it exists
if [[ -f "$AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_32BIT" "$AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Added 32-bit fchmodat audit rule to $AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"
        CHANGES_MADE=1
    else
        echo "32-bit fchmodat audit rule already exists in $AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64BIT" "$AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Added 64-bit fchmodat audit rule to $AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"
        CHANGES_MADE=1
    else
        echo "64-bit fchmodat audit rule already exists in $AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Load the rules into the running kernel if changes were made
if [[ $CHANGES_MADE -eq 1 ]]; then
    # Check if augenrules is available and use it
    if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Loading rules using augenrules..."
        augenrules --load
    elif command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Loading rules using auditctl..."
        auditctl -R "$AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true
        # Also load individual rules directly
        auditctl $RULE_32BIT 2>/dev/null || true
        auditctl $RULE_64BIT 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    echo "Audit rules for fchmodat have been updated and loaded."
else
    echo "No changes needed. All fchmodat audit rules are already in place."
fi

# Verify rules are loaded in the running system
echo ""
echo "Verifying loaded fchmodat audit rules:"
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -E "fchmodat.*perm_mod" || echo "Note: Rules may require a service restart or reboot to take effect."
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled for persistence across reboots
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    if ! systemctl is-enabled auditd &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Enabling auditd service..."
        systemctl enable auditd
    fi
fi

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. The audit rules for fchmodat syscall are configured to:"
echo "  - Monitor both 32-bit and 64-bit invocations"
echo "  - Track users with login UID >= 1000"
echo "  - Tag events with key 'perm_mod'"
echo "  - Persist across reboots"

exit 0

        [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] && continue
        [[ -z "${line// }" ]] && continue
        
        local normalized_line=$(echo "$line" | tr -s ' ')
        if [[ "$normalized_line" == "$normalized_rule" ]]; then
            return 0
        fi
    done < "$file"
    
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule to a file if it doesn't exist
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$rule" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Ensure audit rules directory exists
if [[ ! -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"
fi

# Track if any changes were made
CHANGES_MADE=0

# For augenrules-based systems: add rules to /etc/audit/rules.d/
# Create the rules file if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Add 32-bit rule
if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_32BIT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    echo "Added 32-bit fchmodat audit rule to $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    CHANGES_MADE=1
else
    echo "32-bit fchmodat audit rule already exists in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Add 64-bit rule
if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64BIT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    echo "Added 64-bit fchmodat audit rule to $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    CHANGES_MADE=1
else
    echo "64-bit fchmodat audit rule already exists in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# For auditctl-based systems: also update /etc/audit/audit.rules if it exists
if [[ -f "$AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_32BIT" "$AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Added 32-bit fchmodat audit rule to $AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"
        CHANGES_MADE=1
    else
        echo "32-bit fchmodat audit rule already exists in $AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64BIT" "$AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Added 64-bit fchmodat audit rule to $AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"
        CHANGES_MADE=1
    else
        echo "64-bit fchmodat audit rule already exists in $AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Load the rules into the running kernel if changes were made
if [[ $CHANGES_MADE -eq 1 ]]; then
    # Check if augenrules is available and use it
    if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Loading rules using augenrules..."
        augenrules --load
    elif command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Loading rules using auditctl..."
        auditctl -R "$AUDITCTL_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true
        # Also load individual rules directly
        auditctl $RULE_32BIT 2>/dev/null || true
        auditctl $RULE_64BIT 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    echo "Audit rules for fchmodat have been updated and loaded."
else
    echo "No changes needed. All fchmodat audit rules are already in place."
fi

# Verify rules are loaded in the running system
echo ""
echo "Verifying loaded fchmodat audit rules:"
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -E "fchmodat.*perm_mod" || echo "Note: Rules may require a service restart or reboot to take effect."
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled for persistence across reboots
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    if ! systemctl is-enabled auditd &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Enabling auditd service..."
        systemctl enable auditd
    fi
fi

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. The audit rules for fchmodat syscall are configured to:"
echo "  - Monitor both 32-bit and 64-bit invocations"
echo "  - Track users with login UID >= 1000"
echo "  - Tag events with key 'perm_mod'"
echo "  - Persist across reboots"

exit 0

if [[ -f "$SYSCTL_CONF" ]]; then
    if grep -q "^${PARAM}\s*=" "$SYSCTL_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
        sed -i "s/^${PARAM}\s*=.*/${PARAM} = ${VALUE}/" "$SYSCTL_CONF"

    echo "Successfully disabled user namespaces (${PARAM}=${VALUE})"
else
    echo "Warning: Failed to verify setting. Current value: ${CURRENT_VALUE}" >&2

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to disable SSH logins with empty passwords on RHEL 8

set -e

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
DROP_IN_FILE="${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/01-disable-empty-passwords.conf"

# Function to check if sshd_config.d is used (RHEL 8.2+)
uses_config_dir() {
    if [[ -d "${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}" ]] && grep -q "^Include.*sshd_config\.d" "${SSHD_CONFIG}" 2>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to validate sshd configuration
validate_sshd_config() {
    if sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Backup original config if not already backed up
backup_config() {
    local config_file="$1"
    if [[ -f "${config_file}" ]] && [[ ! -f "${config_file}.bak.original" ]]; then
        cp -p "${config_file}" "${config_file}.bak.original"
    fi
}

# Main remediation logic
main() {
    # Check if running as root
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Error: This script must be run as root" >&2
        exit 1
    fi

    # Check if sshd is installed
    if ! command -v sshd &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Error: OpenSSH server is not installed" >&2
        exit 1
    fi

    backup_config "${SSHD_CONFIG}"

    if uses_config_dir; then
        # Use drop-in configuration file (preferred for RHEL 8.2+)
        mkdir -p "${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}"
        
        # Check if the setting already exists and is correct in the drop-in file
        if [[ -f "${DROP_IN_FILE}" ]] && grep -qE "^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords\s+no\s*$" "${DROP_IN_FILE}"; then
            echo "PermitEmptyPasswords is already set to 'no' in ${DROP_IN_FILE}"
        else
            # Remove any existing PermitEmptyPasswords lines from drop-in file
            if [[ -f "${DROP_IN_FILE}" ]]; then
                sed -i '/^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords/d' "${DROP_IN_FILE}"
            fi
            
            # Add the correct setting
            echo "PermitEmptyPasswords no" >> "${DROP_IN_FILE}"
            chmod 600 "${DROP_IN_FILE}"
            echo "Set PermitEmptyPasswords to 'no' in ${DROP_IN_FILE}"
        fi
        
        # Also ensure the main config doesn't have a conflicting setting that could take precedence
        # Comment out any PermitEmptyPasswords in main config to avoid confusion
        if grep -qE "^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
            sed -i 's/^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords/#&/' "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
            echo "Commented out PermitEmptyPasswords in main config (drop-in takes precedence)"
        fi
    else
        # Modify the main sshd_config directly (older RHEL 8 or custom setup)
        if grep -qE "^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords\s+no\s*$" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
            echo "PermitEmptyPasswords is already set to 'no' in ${SSHD_CONFIG}"
        elif grep -qE "^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
            # Setting exists but with wrong value, update it
            sed -i 's/^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords.*/PermitEmptyPasswords no/' "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
            echo "Updated PermitEmptyPasswords to 'no' in ${SSHD_CONFIG}"
        elif grep -qE "^#\s*PermitEmptyPasswords" "${SSHD_CONFIG}"; then
            # Setting is commented out, uncomment and set correctly
            sed -i 's/^#\s*PermitEmptyPasswords.*/PermitEmptyPasswords no/' "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
            echo "Uncommented and set PermitEmptyPasswords to 'no' in ${SSHD_CONFIG}"
        else
            # Setting doesn't exist, add it
            echo "" >> "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
            echo "# Disable empty password authentication (security hardening)" >> "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
            echo "PermitEmptyPasswords no" >> "${SSHD_CONFIG}"
            echo "Added PermitEmptyPasswords no to ${SSHD_CONFIG}"
        fi
    fi

    # Validate the configuration
    if ! validate_sshd_config; then
        echo "Error: SSH configuration validation failed" >&2
        exit 1
    fi

    # Restart sshd to apply changes
    if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
        systemctl reload sshd || systemctl restart sshd
        echo "SSH service reloaded successfully"
    elif systemctl is-enabled --quiet sshd; then
        systemctl start sshd
        echo "SSH service started"
    fi

    # Verify the setting is active
    if sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep -qi "^permitemptypasswords no"; then
        echo "Verification: PermitEmptyPasswords is correctly set to 'no'"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not verify the setting via sshd -T" >&2
    fi

    echo "Remediation complete: Empty password SSH authentication is now disabled"
}

main "$@"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to disable cramfs filesystem module on RHEL 8

set -e

MODPROBE_CONF="/etc/modprobe.d/cramfs.conf"
MODULES_LOAD_CONF="/etc/modules-load.d/cramfs.conf"

# Create or update modprobe configuration to disable cramfs
# This prevents the module from being loaded automatically or on demand

# Check if the disable line already exists
if grep -q "^install cramfs /bin/false" "$MODPROBE_CONF" 2>/dev/null && \
   grep -q "^blacklist cramfs" "$MODPROBE_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "cramfs module is already disabled in $MODPROBE_CONF"
else
    # Create the configuration file with proper settings
    cat > "$MODPROBE_CONF" << 'EOF'
# Disable cramfs filesystem module to reduce kernel attack surface
# Generated by security hardening script

# Prevent the module from being loaded
install cramfs /bin/false

# Blacklist the module
blacklist cramfs
EOF
    echo "Created/updated $MODPROBE_CONF to disable cramfs"
fi

# Remove any configuration that might load cramfs at boot
if [ -f "$MODULES_LOAD_CONF" ]; then
    rm -f "$MODULES_LOAD_CONF"
    echo "Removed $MODULES_LOAD_CONF"
fi

# Check if cramfs module is currently loaded and unload it
if lsmod | grep -q "^cramfs"; then
    echo "cramfs module is currently loaded, attempting to unload..."
    if rmmod cramfs 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Successfully unloaded cramfs module"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not unload cramfs module (may be in use)"
        echo "A reboot may be required to fully disable the module"
    fi
else
    echo "cramfs module is not currently loaded"
fi

# Ensure proper permissions on the configuration file
chmod 644 "$MODPROBE_CONF"

echo "cramfs filesystem module has been persistently disabled"
echo "Configuration file: $MODPROBE_CONF"

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to set umask 077 for C shell users on RHEL 8
# This ensures newly created files/directories don't grant permissions to group/other

CSHRC_FILE="/etc/csh.cshrc"
UMASK_VALUE="077"
UMASK_LINE="umask ${UMASK_VALUE}"

# Function to check if the correct umask is already set
check_umask_set() {
    if [[ -f "$CSHRC_FILE" ]]; then
        # Check if umask 077 is set (allowing for variations in whitespace)
        if grep -qE "^\s*umask\s+${UMASK_VALUE}\s*$" "$CSHRC_FILE"; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to remove any existing umask settings that are not 077
remove_existing_umask() {
    if [[ -f "$CSHRC_FILE" ]]; then
        # Remove any existing umask lines (we'll add the correct one)
        sed -i '/^\s*umask\s\+[0-9]\{3,4\}\s*$/d' "$CSHRC_FILE"
    fi
}

# Main remediation logic
main() {
    # Create the file if it doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -f "$CSHRC_FILE" ]]; then
        touch "$CSHRC_FILE"
        chmod 644 "$CSHRC_FILE"
    fi

    # Check if correct umask is already set
    if check_umask_set; then
        echo "umask ${UMASK_VALUE} is already configured in ${CSHRC_FILE}"
        exit 0
    fi

    # Remove any existing umask settings
    remove_existing_umask

    # Add the correct umask setting
    echo "$UMASK_LINE" >> "$CSHRC_FILE"

    # Verify the change was applied
    if check_umask_set; then
        echo "Successfully configured umask ${UMASK_VALUE} in ${CSHRC_FILE}"
        exit 0
    else
        echo "Failed to configure umask in ${CSHRC_FILE}"
        exit 1
    fi
}

main

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rule for sudoers file monitoring on RHEL 8

set -e

SUDOERS_FILE="/etc/sudoers"
AUDIT_KEY="privileged-actions"
AUDIT_RULE="-w ${SUDOERS_FILE} -p wa -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Ensure auditd is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
systemctl enable auditd
if ! systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Function to add rule to a rules file if not already present
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rules_file="$1"
    local rule_line="$2"
    
    # Create directory if it doesn't exist
    local rules_dir=$(dirname "$rules_file")
    if [[ ! -d "$rules_dir" ]]; then
        mkdir -p "$rules_dir"
    fi
    
    # Check if rule already exists in the file (handle variations in whitespace)
    if [[ -f "$rules_file" ]]; then
        # Normalize and check for existing rule
        if grep -qE "^-w\s+${SUDOERS_FILE}\s+-p\s+wa\s+-k\s+${AUDIT_KEY}\s*$" "$rules_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
        # Also check without strict spacing
        if grep -q "^${rule_line}$" "$rules_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    # Append the rule
    echo "$rule_line" >> "$rules_file"
}

# Define the rules files
# For augenrules (rules-merging program): /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules
# For auditctl (direct rule-loading utility): /etc/audit/audit.rules

RULES_D_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/50-sudoers.rules"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Add rule to rules.d directory for augenrules
add_rule_to_file "$RULES_D_FILE" "$AUDIT_RULE"

# Also ensure the rule is in audit.rules for direct auditctl loading
# First check if audit.rules exists and is not just a symlink to rules.d output
if [[ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    # Check if rule exists
    if ! grep -qE "^-w\s+${SUDOERS_FILE}\s+-p\s+wa\s+-k\s+${AUDIT_KEY}\s*$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        if ! grep -q "^${AUDIT_RULE}$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
            # Add before any -e 2 line if present, otherwise append
            if grep -q "^-e 2" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then

            else
                echo "$AUDIT_RULE" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
            fi
        fi
    fi
else

    echo "$AUDIT_RULE" > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Set proper permissions on rules files
chmod 640 "$RULES_D_FILE"
if [[ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Load the rule into the running kernel using auditctl
# Check if rule is already loaded
if ! auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -qE "^-w\s+${SUDOERS_FILE}\s+-p\s+wa\s+-k\s+${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
    auditctl -w "$SUDOERS_FILE" -p wa -k "$AUDIT_KEY" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Regenerate rules using augenrules if available
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
fi

echo "Audit rule for ${SUDOERS_FILE} has been configured successfully."
echo "Rule: ${AUDIT_RULE}"
echo "Query events with: ausearch -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rule for sudoers file monitoring on RHEL 8

set -e

SUDOERS_FILE="/etc/sudoers"
AUDIT_KEY="privileged-actions"
AUDIT_RULE="-w ${SUDOERS_FILE} -p wa -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Ensure auditd is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
systemctl enable auditd
if ! systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Function to add rule to a rules file if not already present
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rules_file="$1"
    local rule_line="$2"
    
    # Create directory if it doesn't exist
    local rules_dir=$(dirname "$rules_file")
    if [[ ! -d "$rules_dir" ]]; then
        mkdir -p "$rules_dir"
    fi
    
    # Check if rule already exists in the file (handle variations in whitespace)
    if [[ -f "$rules_file" ]]; then
        # Normalize and check for existing rule
        if grep -qE "^-w\s+${SUDOERS_FILE}\s+-p\s+wa\s+-k\s+${AUDIT_KEY}\s*$" "$rules_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
        # Also check without strict spacing
        if grep -q "^${rule_line}$" "$rules_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    # Append the rule
    echo "$rule_line" >> "$rules_file"
}

# Define the rules files
# For augenrules (rules-merging program): /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules
# For auditctl (direct rule-loading utility): /etc/audit/audit.rules

RULES_D_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/50-sudoers.rules"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Add rule to rules.d directory for augenrules
add_rule_to_file "$RULES_D_FILE" "$AUDIT_RULE"

    # Check if rule exists

        if ! grep -q "^${AUDIT_RULE}$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
            # Add before any -e 2 line if present, otherwise append

                sed -i "/^-e 2/i ${AUDIT_RULE}" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
            else

            fi
        fi
    fi
else
    # Create audit.rules with the rule
    echo "$AUDIT_RULE" > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Set proper permissions on rules files
chmod 640 "$RULES_D_FILE"
if [[ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Load the rule into the running kernel using auditctl

fi

echo "Query events with: ausearch -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Ensure /dev/shm is mounted with nodev option - immediate and persistent

MOUNT_POINT="/dev/shm"
OPTION="nodev"

# Function to check if option is already present in mount options
option_in_mount() {
    local mnt="$1"
    local opt="$2"
    findmnt -n -o OPTIONS "$mnt" 2>/dev/null | grep -qw "$opt"
}

# Function to add option to fstab entry
add_option_to_fstab() {
    local mnt="$1"
    local opt="$2"
    
    # Check if /dev/shm has an entry in fstab
    if grep -q "^[^#].*[[:space:]]${mnt}[[:space:]]" /etc/fstab; then
        # Entry exists, check if option is already there
        if grep "^[^#].*[[:space:]]${mnt}[[:space:]]" /etc/fstab | grep -qw "$opt"; then
            return 0  # Option already present
        else
            # Add option to existing entry
            sed -i "/^[^#].*[[:space:]]${mnt//\//\\/}[[:space:]]/ s/\(defaults[^[:space:]]*\)/\1,${opt}/" /etc/fstab
            # If defaults wasn't in the options, try a different approach
            if ! grep "^[^#].*[[:space:]]${mnt}[[:space:]]" /etc/fstab | grep -qw "$opt"; then
                sed -i "/^[^#].*[[:space:]]${mnt//\//\\/}[[:space:]]/ s/\([[:space:]]tmpfs[[:space:]]*\)\([^[:space:]]*\)/\1\2,${opt}/" /etc/fstab
            fi
        fi
    else
        # No entry exists, create one
        echo "tmpfs ${mnt} tmpfs defaults,${opt} 0 0" >> /etc/fstab
    fi
}

# Apply immediately if not already mounted with nodev
if ! option_in_mount "$MOUNT_POINT" "$OPTION"; then
    mount -o remount,"$OPTION" "$MOUNT_POINT"
fi

# Make persistent in /etc/fstab
add_option_to_fstab "$MOUNT_POINT" "$OPTION"

# Verify the changes
if option_in_mount "$MOUNT_POINT" "$OPTION"; then
    echo "SUCCESS: ${MOUNT_POINT} is currently mounted with ${OPTION} option"
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to apply ${OPTION} option to ${MOUNT_POINT}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

if grep -q "^[^#].*[[:space:]]${MOUNT_POINT}[[:space:]].*${OPTION}" /etc/fstab; then
    echo "SUCCESS: ${OPTION} option is configured persistently in /etc/fstab"
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to configure ${OPTION} option persistently in /etc/fstab" >&2
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

            # Update existing line

            if grep -q "^auth" "$pam_file"; then

#!/bin/bash

# Get minimum UID for interactive users from login.defs
min_uid=$(awk '/^\s*UID_MIN/{print $2}' /etc/login.defs)
if [[ -z "$min_uid" ]]; then
    min_uid=1000
fi

# Get all local interactive users (UID >= min_uid and has a valid home directory)
while IFS=: read -r username _ uid _ _ home_dir _; do
    # Skip if UID is below minimum or home directory doesn't exist
    if [[ "$uid" -lt "$min_uid" ]] || [[ ! -d "$home_dir" ]]; then
        continue
    fi

    # Skip if home directory is /dev/null, /, or empty
    if [[ "$home_dir" == "/" ]] || [[ "$home_dir" == "/dev/null" ]] || [[ -z "$home_dir" ]]; then
        continue
    fi

    # Get all groups the user belongs to
    user_groups=$(id -G "$username" 2>/dev/null)
    if [[ -z "$user_groups" ]]; then
        continue
    fi

    # Convert groups to an array for easy lookup
    declare -A valid_groups
    for gid in $user_groups; do
        valid_groups[$gid]=1
    done

    # Get the user's primary group
    primary_gid=$(id -g "$username" 2>/dev/null)
    if [[ -z "$primary_gid" ]]; then
        continue
    fi

    # Find all files and directories in the user's home directory
    # that are not group-owned by one of the user's groups
    while IFS= read -r -d '' file; do
        # Get the group ID of the file
        file_gid=$(stat -c '%g' "$file" 2>/dev/null)
        if [[ -z "$file_gid" ]]; then
            continue
        fi

        # Check if the file's group is in the user's groups
        if [[ -z "${valid_groups[$file_gid]}" ]]; then
            # File is not group-owned by any of user's groups, change to primary group
            chgrp "$primary_gid" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        fi
    done < <(find "$home_dir" -print0 2>/dev/null)

    # Clear the valid_groups array for next user
    unset valid_groups

done < /etc/passwd

exit 0

# Remediation script to disable ICMP redirect acceptance on all IPv4 interfaces
# This prevents man-in-the-middle attacks via forged ICMP redirect messages

# Define the sysctl parameters to set

    "net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects=0"
    "net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects=0"
)

# Configuration file for persistence
SYSCTL_CONF_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-icmp-redirects.conf"

        elif [[ "$line" =~ ^account[[:space:]] ]]; then
            in_auth=false
            in_account=true
            
            # Add account faillock at the beginning of account section
            if ! $account_faillock_added && ! $has_account; then
                echo "account     required                                     pam_faillock.so" >> "$temp_file"
                account_faillock_added=true
            fi
        elif [[ "$line" =~ ^password[[:space:]] ]] || [[ "$line" =~ ^session[[:space:]] ]]; then
            in_auth=false
            in_account=false
        fi

    done < "$pam_file"
    
    # Copy temp file to original
    cat "$temp_file" > "$pam_file"
    rm -f "$temp_file"

# Use authselect if available (RHEL 8 preferred method)
use_authselect() {
    if command -v authselect &> /dev/null; then
        # Check current profile

        current_profile=$(authselect current -r 2>/dev/null || echo "")
        
        if [[ -z "$current_profile" ]]; then

        else

                # Enable faillock feature
                authselect enable-feature with-faillock 2>/dev/null || {
                    # If enable-feature fails, try selecting with the feature
                    local profile_name
                    profile_name=$(echo "$current_profile" | awk '{print $1}')
                    authselect select "$profile_name" with-faillock --force 2>/dev/null || return 1
                }
            fi
        fi
        return

#!/bin/bash

# SSH Server Client Responsiveness Timeout Configuration Script
# This script configures SSH to probe clients and disconnect unresponsive ones after 600 seconds

set -e

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"

# Function to set or update a configuration parameter in sshd_config
set_sshd_param() {
    local param="$1"
    local value="$2"
    local config_file="$3"
    
    # Check if the parameter exists (commented or uncommented)
    if grep -qE "^\s*#?\s*${param}\s+" "$config_file"; then
        # Parameter exists, update it (handle both commented and uncommented)
        sed -i "s/^\s*#\?\s*${param}\s\+.*/${param} ${value}/" "$config_file"
    else
        # Parameter doesn't exist, append it
        echo "${param} ${value}" >> "$config_file"
    fi
}

# Backup the original config if backup doesn't exist
if [ ! -f "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak" ]; then
    cp "$SSHD_CONFIG" "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak"
fi

# Configure ClientAliveInterval - time in seconds between keepalive probes
# Setting to 300 seconds (5 minutes) means SSH will probe every 5 minutes
set_sshd_param "ClientAliveInterval" "300" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Configure ClientAliveCountMax - number of missed probes before disconnect
# With ClientAliveInterval=300 and ClientAliveCountMax=2, total timeout is 600 seconds
# After 2 missed probes (300*2=600 seconds), the client is disconnected
set_sshd_param "ClientAliveCountMax" "2" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Validate the SSH configuration
if ! sshd -t; then
    echo "ERROR: SSH configuration validation failed"
    # Restore backup if validation fails
    cp "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    exit 1
fi

# Restart the SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

# Verify the service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
    echo "SSH server configured successfully with client responsiveness timeout of 600 seconds"
    echo "ClientAliveInterval: 300 seconds"
    echo "ClientAliveCountMax: 2"
    echo "Total timeout before disconnect: 600 seconds (10 minutes)"
else
    echo "ERROR: SSH service failed to start"
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to ensure all local user accounts have a maximum password age of 365 days

MAX_PASS_AGE=365

# Get all local user accounts from /etc/shadow
# We process accounts that have a password (not locked with ! or * as the only content)
# and that are defined in /etc/passwd (local accounts)

while IFS=: read -r username password uid gid gecos home shell; do
    # Skip system accounts (UID < 1000) except for root (UID 0)
    # Also skip nobody and nfsnobody accounts
    if [[ "$username" == "nobody" ]] || [[ "$username" == "nfsnobody" ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Process root and regular users (UID >= 1000)
    if [[ "$uid" -eq 0 ]] || [[ "$uid" -ge 1000 ]]; then
        # Check if user exists in shadow file
        if grep -q "^${username}:" /etc/shadow 2>/dev/null; then
            # Get current max password age from shadow file
            current_max=$(chage -l "$username" 2>/dev/null | grep "Maximum number of days between password change" | cut -d: -f2 | tr -d ' ')

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to ensure all local user accounts have a maximum password age of 365 days

MAX_PASS_AGE=365

# Get all local user accounts from /etc/shadow
# We process accounts that have a password (not locked with ! or * as the only content)
# and that are defined in /etc/passwd (local accounts)

while IFS=: read -r username password uid gid gecos home shell; do
    # Skip system accounts (UID < 1000) except for root (UID 0)
    # Also skip nobody and nfsnobody accounts
    if [[ "$username" == "nobody" ]] || [[ "$username" == "nfsnobody" ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Process root and regular users (UID >= 1000)
    if [[ "$uid" -eq 0 ]] || [[ "$uid" -ge 1000 ]]; then
        # Check if user exists in shadow file
        if grep -q "^${username}:" /etc/shadow 2>/dev/null; then
            # Get current max password age from shadow file
            current_max=$(chage -l "$username" 2>/dev/null | grep "Maximum number of days between password change" | cut -d: -f2 | tr -d ' ')
            
            # Check if the account has a password set (not locked or no password)
            shadow_entry=$(grep "^${username}:" /etc/shadow)
            pass_field=$(echo "$shadow_entry" | cut -d: -f2)
            
            # Skip accounts that are locked (start with ! or *) or have no password
            if [[ "$pass_field" == "!!"* ]] || [[ "$pass_field" == "!" ]] || [[ "$pass_field" == "*" ]] || [[ -z "$pass_field" ]]; then
                continue
            fi
            
            # If current max is -1 (never expires), 99999 (default never), empty, or greater than 365, update it
            if [[ "$current_max" == "-1" ]] || [[ "$current_max" == "99999" ]] || [[ -z "$current_max" ]] || [[ "$current_max" == "never" ]] || [[ "$current_max" -gt "$MAX_PASS_AGE" ]]; then
                chage -M "$MAX_PASS_AGE" "$username"
                echo "Updated maximum password age to $MAX_PASS_AGE days for user: $username"
            fi
        fi
    fi
done < /etc/passwd

# Also update /etc/login.defs for new users
if grep -q "^PASS_MAX_DAYS" /etc/login.defs; then
    current_default=$(grep "^PASS_MAX_DAYS" /etc/login.defs | awk '{print $2}')
    if [[ "$current_default" -gt "$MAX_PASS_AGE" ]] || [[ "$current_default" == "99999" ]]; then
        sed -i "s/^PASS_MAX_DAYS.*/PASS_MAX_DAYS   $MAX_PASS_AGE/" /etc/login.defs
        echo "Updated PASS_MAX_DAYS in /etc/login.defs to $MAX_PASS_AGE"
    fi
else
    echo "PASS_MAX_DAYS   $MAX_PASS_AGE" >> /etc/login.defs
    echo "Added PASS_MAX_DAYS to /etc/login.defs with value $MAX_PASS_AGE"
fi

echo "Password maximum age remediation complete."

SSH_AGENT_PATH="/usr/bin/ssh-agent"
