# Rule for root (auid=0)
RULE_ROOT="-a always,exit -F path=${SSH_AGENT_PATH} -F perm=x -F auid=0 -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Check if ssh-agent exists
if [[ ! -f "${SSH_AGENT_PATH}" ]]; then
    echo "Warning: ${SSH_AGENT_PATH} does not exist on this system"
    # Continue anyway to set up the rule for when it might be installed
fi

# Function to check if a rule already exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        # Normalize whitespace and check if rule exists
        local normalized_rule
        normalized_rule=$(echo "$rule" | tr -s ' ')
        while IFS= read -r line; do
            # Skip comments and empty lines
            [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] && continue
            [[ -z "${line// /}" ]] && continue
            local normalized_line
            normalized_line=$(echo "$line" | tr -s ' ')
            if [[ "$normalized_line" == "$normalized_rule" ]]; then
                return 0
            fi
        done < "$file"
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule to a file if it doesn't exist
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$rule" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        echo "Added rule to ${file}: ${rule}"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Rule already exists in ${file}: ${rule}"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Determine which rule loading mechanism is in use
# Check if auditd is configured to use augenrules or auditctl

AUDITD_CONF="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"
USE_AUGENRULES=false

if [[ -f "$AUDITD_CONF" ]]; then
    # Check for the presence of rules.d directory usage indicator
    # augenrules uses /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules files
    # auditctl uses /etc/audit/audit.rules directly
    
    # On RHEL 8, the default is typically augenrules

    if systemctl cat auditd.service 2>/dev/null | grep -q "augenrules"; then

    elif [[ -d "/etc/audit/rules.d" ]]; then

    # On RHEL 8, if augenrules exists and rules.d directory exists, it's likely in use
    if [[ -d "/etc/audit/rules.d" ]]; then
        USE_AUGENRULES=true
    fi
fi

RULES_CHANGED=false

    RULES_FILE="${RULES_DIR}/99-ssh-agent.rules"
    
    # Ensure the rules directory exists
    mkdir -p "$RULES_DIR"
    
    # Create or update the rules file
    if [[ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
        touch "$RULES_FILE"
        chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Add rules if they don't exist
    if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_USER" "$RULES_FILE"; then
        RULES_CHANGED=true
    fi

        RULES_CHANGED=true
    fi
    
    # If rules changed, regenerate the main audit.rules file
    if [[ "$RULES_CHANGED" == "true" ]]; then
        echo "Regenerating audit rules with augenrules..."
        augenrules --load
    fi
else
    # Using auditctl directly - add rules to /etc/audit/audit.rules
    RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
    
    # Ensure the file exists
    if [[ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
        touch "$RULES_FILE"
        chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Add rules if they don't exist
    if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_USER" "$RULES_FILE"; then
        RULES_CHANGED=true
    fi
    
    if add_rule_to_file "$RULE_ROOT" "$RULES_FILE"; then
        RULES_CHANGED=true
    fi
    
    # Load the rules immediately
    if [[ "$RULES_CHANGED" == "true" ]]; then
        echo "Loading audit rules with auditctl..."
        auditctl -R "$RULES_FILE" || true
    fi
fi

# Verify rules are loaded in the running kernel
echo ""
echo "Verifying loaded audit rules for ssh-agent..."
if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "${SSH_AGENT_PATH}"; then
    echo "SUCCESS: Audit rules for ${SSH_AGENT_PATH} are active in the running kernel"
    auditctl -l | grep "${SSH_AGENT_PATH}"
else
    echo "Loading rules into running kernel..."
    # Load the rules directly into the running kernel
    auditctl $RULE_USER 2>/dev/null || echo "Note: User rule may already exist or failed to load"
    auditctl $RULE_ROOT 2>/dev/null || echo "Note: Root rule may already exist or failed to load"
    
    # Verify again
    if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "${SSH_AGENT_PATH}"; then
        echo "SUCCESS: Audit rules for ${SSH_AGENT_PATH} are now active"
        auditctl -l | grep "${SSH_AGENT_PATH}"
    else
        echo "WARNING: Could not verify audit rules are loaded"
    fi
fi

echo ""
echo "Audit configuration complete for ssh-agent execution monitoring."
echo "Rules are configured to persist across reboots."

#!/bin/bash

set -e

MOUNT_POINT="/dev/shm"
FSTAB_FILE="/etc/fstab"

# Function to check if nosuid is already in mount options
has_nosuid_in_fstab() {
    if grep -E "^\s*[^#].*\s+${MOUNT_POINT}\s+" "$FSTAB_FILE" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "nosuid"; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if /dev/shm has an entry in fstab
has_fstab_entry() {
    if grep -E "^\s*[^#].*\s+${MOUNT_POINT}\s+" "$FSTAB_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if currently mounted with nosuid
is_mounted_with_nosuid() {
    if mount | grep "on ${MOUNT_POINT} " | grep -q "nosuid"; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Ensure /dev/shm has nosuid in /etc/fstab
if has_fstab_entry; then
    # Entry exists, check if nosuid is present
    if ! has_nosuid_in_fstab; then
        # Add nosuid to existing entry
        # Get the current line and modify it
        sed -i -E "/^\s*[^#].*\s+\/dev\/shm\s+/{
            s/(defaults)/\1,nosuid/
            t done
            s/(tmpfs\s+)/\1nosuid,/
            t done
            s/(\s+tmpfs\s+)([^\s]+)/\1\2,nosuid/
            :done
        }" "$FSTAB_FILE"
        
        # Verify the change was made, if not try alternative approach
        if ! has_nosuid_in_fstab; then
            # More aggressive approach - add nosuid to options field
            awk '
            /^[^#].*[[:space:]]\/dev\/shm[[:space:]]/ {
                split($0, fields)
                if (fields[4] !~ /nosuid/) {
                    if (fields[4] == "") {
                        fields[4] = "nosuid"
                    } else {
                        fields[4] = fields[4] ",nosuid"
                    }
                }
                printf "%s %s %s %s %s %s\n", fields[1], fields[2], fields[3], fields[4], fields[5], fields[6]
                next
            }
            { print }
            ' "$FSTAB_FILE" > "${FSTAB_FILE}.tmp" && mv "${FSTAB_FILE}.tmp" "$FSTAB_FILE"
        fi
    fi
else
    # No entry exists, add one
    echo "tmpfs ${MOUNT_POINT} tmpfs defaults,nosuid 0 0" >> "$FSTAB_FILE"
fi

# Apply the change immediately by remounting
if ! is_mounted_with_nosuid; then
    mount -o remount,nosuid "$MOUNT_POINT"
fi

# Verify the configuration
echo "Verifying configuration..."

if has_nosuid_in_fstab; then
    echo "SUCCESS: /etc/fstab contains nosuid option for ${MOUNT_POINT}"
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to configure nosuid in /etc/fstab"
    exit 1
fi

if is_mounted_with_nosuid; then
    echo "SUCCESS: ${MOUNT_POINT} is currently mounted with nosuid"
else
    echo "ERROR: ${MOUNT_POINT} is not mounted with nosuid"
    exit 1
fi

echo "Configuration complete. ${MOUNT_POINT} is protected with nosuid."

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

GRUB_CONFIG="/etc/default/grub"
PARAM="vsyscall=none"

# Function to check if parameter exists in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
param_exists() {
    grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=.*${PARAM}" "$GRUB_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null
}

# Backup the original file if not already backed up
if [[ ! -f "${GRUB_CONFIG}.bak" ]]; then
    cp "$GRUB_CONFIG" "${GRUB_CONFIG}.bak"
fi

# Check if the parameter is already set
if param_exists; then
    echo "Parameter '${PARAM}' is already set in ${GRUB_CONFIG}"
else
    # Add the parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
    if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CONFIG"; then
        # Parameter line exists, append to it
        sed -i "s/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"${PARAM} /" "$GRUB_CONFIG"
        
        # Clean up any double spaces that might have been introduced
        sed -i 's/  */ /g' "$GRUB_CONFIG"
        
        # Remove trailing space before closing quote if present
        sed -i 's/ "/"/g' "$GRUB_CONFIG"
    else
        # GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX line doesn't exist, add it
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"${PARAM}\"" >> "$GRUB_CONFIG"
    fi
    echo "Added '${PARAM}' to ${GRUB_CONFIG}"
fi

# Regenerate GRUB configuration for all installed kernels
# This handles both BIOS and UEFI systems
if [[ -d /sys/firmware/efi ]]; then
    # UEFI system
    GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg"
    if [[ ! -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]]; then
        GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/centos/grub.cfg"
    fi
else
    # BIOS system
    GRUB_CFG="/boot/grub2/grub.cfg"
fi

if [[ -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]] || [[ -d "$(dirname "$GRUB_CFG")" ]]; then
    grub2-mkconfig -o "$GRUB_CFG"
    echo "GRUB configuration regenerated at ${GRUB_CFG}"
else
    # Fallback - try common locations
    if [[ -f /boot/grub2/grub.cfg ]]; then
        grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
        echo "GRUB configuration regenerated at /boot/grub2/grub.cfg"
    elif [[ -f /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg ]]; then

```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

GRUB_CONFIG="/etc/default/grub"
PARAM="vsyscall=none"

# Function to check if parameter exists in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
param_exists() {
    grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=.*${PARAM}" "$GRUB_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null
}

# Backup the original file if not already backed up
if [[ ! -f "${GRUB_CONFIG}.bak" ]]; then
    cp "$GRUB_CONFIG" "${GRUB_CONFIG}.bak"
fi

# Check if the parameter is already set
if param_exists; then
    echo "Parameter '${PARAM}' is already set in ${GRUB_CONFIG}"
else
    # Add the parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
    if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CONFIG"; then
        # Parameter line exists, append to it
        sed -i "s/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"${PARAM} /" "$GRUB_CONFIG"
        
        # Clean up any double spaces that might have been introduced
        sed -i 's/  */ /g' "$GRUB_CONFIG"
        
        # Remove trailing space before closing quote if present
        sed -i 's/ "/"/g' "$GRUB_CONFIG"
    else
        # GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX line doesn't exist, add it
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"${PARAM}\"" >> "$GRUB_CONFIG"
    fi
    echo "Added '${PARAM}' to ${GRUB_CONFIG}"
fi

# Regenerate GRUB configuration for all installed kernels
# This handles both BIOS and UEFI systems
if [[ -d /sys/firmware/efi ]]; then
    # UEFI system
    GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg"
    if [[ ! -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]]; then
        GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/centos/grub.cfg"
    fi
else
    # BIOS system
    GRUB_CFG="/boot/grub2/grub.cfg"
fi

if [[ -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]] || [[ -d "$(dirname "$GRUB_CFG")" ]]; then
    grub2-mkconfig -o "$GRUB_CFG"
    echo "GRUB configuration regenerated at ${GRUB_CFG}"
else
    # Fallback - try common locations
    if [[ -f /boot/grub2/grub.cfg ]]; then
        grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
        echo "GRUB configuration regenerated at /boot/grub2/grub.cfg"
    elif [[ -f /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg ]]; then
        grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg
        echo "GRUB configuration regenerated at /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not find GRUB configuration file location"
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# Verify the setting is in place
echo ""
echo "Verification:"
echo "-------------"
grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CONFIG"

# Check current kernel parameter (will only show after reboot)
if grep -q "vsyscall=none" /proc/cmdline 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Current running kernel already has vsyscall=none enabled"
else
    echo "Note: A reboot is required for the vsyscall=none setting to take effect on the running kernel"
fi

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. The vsyscall=none parameter has been configured."
echo "This setting will apply to all current and future kernel installations."

RULE_64BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lchown -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"

rule_exists_in_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        grep -qxF "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if a rule is loaded in the running audit configuration

    local rule="$1"
    auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -qF "$rule"

${RULE_64BIT}"

if [ "$RULES_CONTENT" != "$EXPECTED_CONTENT" ]; then
    NEED_WRITE=1
fi

    echo "Writing audit rules to ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    cat > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" << EOF

${RULE_64BIT}
EOF

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to limit concurrent login sessions to 10 per user on RHEL 8

set -euo pipefail

LIMITS_FILE="/etc/security/limits.conf"
LIMITS_D_FILE="/etc/security/limits.d/10-maxlogins.conf"
MAX_LOGINS=10

# Function to check if a limits.d entry already exists with correct value
check_limits_d() {
    if [[ -f "$LIMITS_D_FILE" ]]; then
        if grep -qE '^\*[[:space:]]+hard[[:space:]]+maxlogins[[:space:]]+'"$MAX_LOGINS"'$' "$LIMITS_D_FILE"; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to set the limit in limits.d (preferred method for RHEL 8)
set_limits_d() {
    # Create or update the limits.d file
    cat > "$LIMITS_D_FILE" << EOF
# Limit concurrent login sessions per user to prevent DoS conditions
# This file is managed by automation - do not edit manually

*               hard    maxlogins       $MAX_LOGINS
EOF
    chmod 644 "$LIMITS_D_FILE"
    echo "Created/updated $LIMITS_D_FILE with maxlogins limit of $MAX_LOGINS"
}

# Function to ensure limits.conf doesn't have conflicting entries
clean_limits_conf() {
    if [[ -f "$LIMITS_FILE" ]]; then
        # Remove any existing maxlogins entries for all users (*) to avoid conflicts
        if grep -qE '^\*[[:space:]]+(hard|soft|-)[[:space:]]+maxlogins' "$LIMITS_FILE"; then
            # Create backup
            cp "$LIMITS_FILE" "${LIMITS_FILE}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
            # Remove conflicting entries
            sed -i '/^\*[[:space:]]\+\(hard\|soft\|-\)[[:space:]]\+maxlogins/d' "$LIMITS_FILE"
            echo "Removed conflicting maxlogins entries from $LIMITS_FILE"
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to verify pam_limits is enabled in PAM configuration
ensure_pam_limits() {
    local pam_files=("/etc/pam.d/system-auth" "/etc/pam.d/password-auth" "/etc/pam.d/login" "/etc/pam.d/sshd")
    local pam_limits_line="session     required      pam_limits.so"
    
    for pam_file in "${pam_files[@]}"; do
        if [[ -f "$pam_file" ]]; then
            if ! grep -q "pam_limits.so" "$pam_file"; then
                echo "WARNING: pam_limits.so not found in $pam_file"
                echo "The limits may not be enforced for sessions using this PAM stack"
            fi
        fi
    done
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Configuring per-user concurrent session limit to $MAX_LOGINS..."
    
    # Check if already configured correctly
    if check_limits_d; then
        echo "Limit already correctly configured in $LIMITS_D_FILE"
    else
        # Clean up any conflicting entries in limits.conf
        clean_limits_conf
        
        # Set the limit in limits.d
        set_limits_d
    fi
    
    # Verify PAM configuration
    ensure_pam_limits
    
    # Verify the configuration
    echo ""
    echo "Current maxlogins configuration:"
    echo "================================"
    
    if [[ -f "$LIMITS_D_FILE" ]]; then
        echo "Contents of $LIMITS_D_FILE:"
        cat "$LIMITS_D_FILE"
    fi
    
    echo ""
    echo "Effective limits (from ulimit -a for reference):"
    echo "Max user processes and other limits are system-wide"
    echo ""
    echo "Configuration complete. The limit of $MAX_LOGINS concurrent sessions per user is now enforced."
    echo "This setting persists across reboots and applies to all users."
    echo ""
    echo "Note: Existing sessions are not affected. New login attempts beyond the limit will be denied."
}

# Run main function
main

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rules for fchownat syscall
# This captures ownership-change events for non-system users (UID >= 1000)

set -e

# Define the audit rules file
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/10-perm_mod.rules"

# Define the rules we need to add
# fchownat is the syscall that changes file ownership relative to a directory file descriptor
# auid>=1000 captures non-system users (standard user threshold on RHEL 8)
# auid!=-1 (or auid!=unset) ensures we only capture events from logged-in users
# -F arch=b32 and -F arch=b64 handle both 32-bit and 64-bit invocations

RULE_64BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchownat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"
RULE_32BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchownat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d

# Function to check if a rule exists in any rules file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    # Check in all rules files
    grep -r -F -- "$rule" /etc/audit/rules.d/ >/dev/null 2>&1
}

# Function to add rule if it doesn't exist
add_rule_if_missing() {
    local rule="$1"
    if ! rule_exists "$rule"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        echo "Added rule: $rule"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Rule already exists: $rule"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Track if we made changes
CHANGES_MADE=0

# Create or update the rules file
# First, ensure the file exists
touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Add 64-bit rule if missing
if ! rule_exists "$RULE_64BIT"; then
    echo "$RULE_64BIT" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "Added 64-bit fchownat audit rule"
    CHANGES_MADE=1
else
    echo "64-bit fchownat audit rule already exists"
fi

# Add 32-bit rule if missing
if ! rule_exists "$RULE_32BIT"; then
    echo "$RULE_32BIT" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "Added 32-bit fchownat audit rule"
    CHANGES_MADE=1
else
    echo "32-bit fchownat audit rule already exists"
fi

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Ensure auditd is enabled
if ! systemctl is-enabled auditd >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    systemctl enable auditd
    echo "Enabled auditd service"
fi

# Load the new rules if changes were made
if [ $CHANGES_MADE -eq 1 ]; then
    # Check if auditd is running
    if systemctl is-active auditd >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
        augenrules --load
        echo "Audit rules have been loaded"
    else
        # Start auditd if not running
        systemctl start auditd
        echo "Started auditd service with new rules"
    fi
else
    echo "No changes needed - audit rules already configured"
fi

# Verify the rules are active
echo ""
echo "Verifying active audit rules for fchownat:"
auditctl -l | grep fchownat || echo "Warning: fchownat rules not found in active ruleset"

echo ""
echo "Audit configuration complete. Rules will persist across reboots."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to set RHEL 8 default boot target to multi-user (non-graphical) mode

set -e

DESIRED_TARGET="multi-user.target"

# Get the current default target
CURRENT_TARGET=$(systemctl get-default 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")

# Check if already set to multi-user.target
if [ "$CURRENT_TARGET" = "$DESIRED_TARGET" ]; then
    echo "System is already configured to boot into $DESIRED_TARGET"
    exit 0
fi

# Set the default target to multi-user.target
echo "Changing default target from '$CURRENT_TARGET' to '$DESIRED_TARGET'"
systemctl set-default "$DESIRED_TARGET"

# Verify the change was successful
NEW_TARGET=$(systemctl get-default)
if [ "$NEW_TARGET" = "$DESIRED_TARGET" ]; then
    echo "Successfully set default boot target to $DESIRED_TARGET"
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to set default boot target. Current target is: $NEW_TARGET"
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to set RHEL 8 default boot target to multi-user (non-graphical) mode

set -e

DESIRED_TARGET="multi-user.target"

# Get the current default target
CURRENT_TARGET=$(systemctl get-default 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")

# Check if already set to multi-user.target
if [ "$CURRENT_TARGET" = "$DESIRED_TARGET" ]; then
    echo "System is already configured to boot into $DESIRED_TARGET"
    exit 0
fi

# Set the default target to multi-user.target
echo "Changing default target from '$CURRENT_TARGET' to '$DESIRED_TARGET'"
systemctl set-default "$DESIRED_TARGET"

# Verify the change was successful
NEW_TARGET=$(systemctl get-default)
if [ "$NEW_TARGET" = "$DESIRED_TARGET" ]; then
    echo "Successfully set default boot target to $DESIRED_TARGET"
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to set default boot target. Current target is: $NEW_TARGET"
    exit 1
fi

# Ensure the symlink exists and points to the correct target
if [ -L /etc/systemd/system/default.target ]; then
    LINK_TARGET=$(readlink -f /etc/systemd/system/default.target)
    if [[ "$LINK_TARGET" == *"$DESIRED_TARGET"* ]]; then
        echo "Symlink /etc/systemd/system/default.target correctly points to $DESIRED_TARGET"
    else
        echo "WARNING: Symlink exists but may not point to expected target: $LINK_TARGET"
    fi
fi

echo "Configuration complete. System will boot into $DESIRED_TARGET on next reboot."
```

unlock_time = 900
# Fail interval - time window for counting failures

        elif [[ "$line" =~ ^password[[:space:]] ]]; then

        elif [[ "$line" =~ ^session[[:space:]] ]]; then

echo "  - /etc/pam.d/password-auth"
echo ""
echo "Backup stored in: $backup_dir"
echo ""
echo "To check failed login attempts for a user, run:"
echo "  faillock --user <username>"
echo ""
echo "To reset a locked account, run:"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to enforce minimum character class requirement for passwords on RHEL 8
# This script ensures passwords must contain characters from at least 4 distinct character categories

set -e

PWQUALITY_CONF="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
PARAM_NAME="minclass"
PARAM_VALUE="4"

# Ensure the pwquality.conf file exists
if [[ ! -f "$PWQUALITY_CONF" ]]; then
    echo "Error: $PWQUALITY_CONF does not exist"
    exit 1
fi

# Check if the parameter already exists in the file
if grep -qE "^\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    # Parameter exists, update it to the correct value
    sed -i "s/^\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=.*/${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}/" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
elif grep -qE "^\s*#\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    # Parameter exists but is commented out, uncomment and set correct value
    sed -i "s/^\s*#\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=.*/${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}/" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
else
    # Parameter doesn't exist, append it to the file
    echo "${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}" >> "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
fi

# Verify the change was applied correctly
if grep -qE "^\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=\s*${PARAM_VALUE}\s*$" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    echo "Successfully configured ${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE} in ${PWQUALITY_CONF}"
else
    echo "Error: Failed to configure ${PARAM_NAME} in ${PWQUALITY_CONF}"
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Define the audit rules for monitoring unsuccessful file creation attempts
# Using 'creat' system call for file creation
# EACCES (permission denied) = -13
# EPERM (operation not permitted) = -1

RULE_32BIT='-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S creat -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k unsuccessful_file_creation'
RULE_64BIT='-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S creat -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k unsuccessful_file_creation'
RULE_32BIT_EPERM='-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S creat -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k unsuccessful_file_creation'
RULE_64BIT_EPERM='-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S creat -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k unsuccessful_file_creation'

# Ensure audit package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled
systemctl enable auditd

# Function to check if a rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -Fxq "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if rule is loaded in running config
rule_is_loaded() {
    local rule="$1"
    auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -Fq "$rule"
    return $?
}

# Determine the rules directory
# RHEL 8 uses /etc/audit/rules.d/ with augenrules
RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
RULES_FILE="${RULES_DIR}/50-unsuccessful-file-creation.rules"

# Create rules directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "$RULES_DIR"

# Also handle the legacy /etc/audit/audit.rules file
LEGACY_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Create or update the rules file in rules.d
# First, remove any existing file to ensure clean state
if [[ -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    rm -f "$RULES_FILE"
fi

# Write all rules to the file
cat > "$RULES_FILE" << EOF
## Audit rules for monitoring unsuccessful file creation attempts
## Records EACCES and EPERM failures for creat syscall
## For unprivileged users (auid >= 1000)

$RULE_32BIT
$RULE_64BIT
$RULE_32BIT_EPERM
$RULE_64BIT_EPERM
EOF

# Set proper permissions
chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE"

# Also add rules to /etc/audit/audit.rules if it exists and is being used
# This handles systems that might use audit.rules directly instead of augenrules
if [[ -f "$LEGACY_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    for rule in "$RULE_32BIT" "$RULE_64BIT" "$RULE_32BIT_EPERM" "$RULE_64BIT_EPERM"; do
        if ! rule_exists_in_file "$rule" "$LEGACY_RULES_FILE"; then
            # Add rule before any -e 2 line if present, otherwise append
            if grep -q "^-e 2" "$LEGACY_RULES_FILE"; then
                sed -i "/^-e 2/i $rule" "$LEGACY_RULES_FILE"
            else
                echo "$rule" >> "$LEGACY_RULES_FILE"
            fi
        fi
    done
fi

# Load rules into running configuration
# First try augenrules, then fall back to auditctl

# Check if augenrules is available and should be used
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
    # Generate rules from rules.d
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Directly load rules using auditctl as well to ensure they're active now
for rule in "$RULE_32BIT" "$RULE_64BIT" "$RULE_32BIT_EPERM" "$RULE_64BIT_EPERM"; do
    if ! rule_is_loaded "$rule"; then
        auditctl $rule 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
done

# Restart auditd to ensure all rules are properly loaded
# Note: auditd requires service restart, not reload, for rule changes
# Use -f to force restart even if rules are locked
if systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    service auditd restart 2>/dev/null || systemctl restart auditd 2>/dev/null || true
else
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Verify rules are loaded
echo "Verifying audit rules are loaded..."
sleep 1
if auditctl -l | grep -q "unsuccessful_file_creation"; then
    echo "SUCCESS: Audit rules for unsuccessful file creation attempts are now active."
    echo "Loaded rules:"
    auditctl -l | grep "unsuccessful_file_creation"
else
    echo "WARNING: Rules may not be fully loaded. Check auditd status."
    echo "Rules file has been created at: $RULES_FILE"
    echo "Rules will be loaded on next auditd restart or system reboot."
fi

echo ""
echo "Configuration files:"
echo "  - $RULES_FILE"
[[ -f "$LEGACY_RULES_FILE" ]] && echo "  - $LEGACY_RULES_FILE"

echo "Configuration files:"

#!/bin/bash

# The privileged command used to toggle SELinux boolean settings is setsebool
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/privileged-setsebool.rules"
SETSEBOOL_PATH="/usr/sbin/setsebool"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"

# Find the actual path to setsebool (it might vary)
if [ -x "${SETSEBOOL_PATH}" ]; then
    COMMAND_PATH="${SETSEBOOL_PATH}"
elif command -v setsebool &>/dev/null; then
    COMMAND_PATH=$(command -v setsebool)
else
    echo "ERROR: setsebool command not found on this system"
    exit 1
fi

# Define the audit rule
# -a always,exit: Always generate an audit record on syscall exit
# -F path=: Watch this specific executable
# -F perm=x: Trigger on execution
# -F auid>=1000: Only for real user IDs >= 1000 (login users)
# -F auid!=unset: Ignore processes that don't have a login UID set (system services)
# -k: Key/tag for easy searching
AUDIT_RULE="-a always,exit -F path=${COMMAND_PATH} -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k privileged-setsebool"

# Check if the rule already exists in any rules file
RULE_EXISTS=false
if grep -r -q "path=${COMMAND_PATH}.*privileged-setsebool" "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/" 2>/dev/null; then
    RULE_EXISTS=true
fi

# If rule doesn't exist, add it
if [ "${RULE_EXISTS}" = false ]; then
    echo "${AUDIT_RULE}" > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    echo "Added audit rule for setsebool to ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
else
    echo "Audit rule for setsebool already exists"
fi

# Ensure the rule file has correct permissions
if [ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]; then
    chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Load the rules into the running audit system
# First check if auditd is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
    echo "Audit rules reloaded"
else
    echo "auditd is not running - rules will be loaded on next start"
fi

# Ensure auditd is enabled to start on boot
systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the rule is in place (either in loaded rules or in config)
echo ""
echo "Verification:"
if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "path=${COMMAND_PATH}.*privileged-setsebool"; then
    echo "SUCCESS: Audit rule is active in running configuration"
else
    echo "INFO: Rule will be active after auditd restart or system reboot"
fi

if grep -q "path=${COMMAND_PATH}.*privileged-setsebool" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "SUCCESS: Audit rule is persistent in ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rule for setfiles command
# This ensures all executions of the privileged SELinux setfiles utility are audited

set -e

# Define the setfiles binary path and audit rule parameters
SETFILES_PATH="/usr/sbin/setfiles"
AUDIT_KEY="privileged"
AUID_MIN="1000"

# The audit rule to add
AUDIT_RULE="-a always,exit -F path=${SETFILES_PATH} -F perm=x -F auid>=${AUID_MIN} -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Function to check if rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        # Check for the essential components of the rule
        grep -q "path=${SETFILES_PATH}" "$file" 2>/dev/null && \
        grep -q "perm=x" "$file" 2>/dev/null && \
        grep -q "auid>=${AUID_MIN}" "$file" 2>/dev/null && \
        grep -q "auid!=unset\|auid!=-1\|auid!=4294967295" "$file" 2>/dev/null && \
        grep -q "k ${AUDIT_KEY}\|key=${AUDIT_KEY}" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule to a file if not present
add_rule_to_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local rule="$2"
    
    # Create directory if it doesn't exist
    mkdir -p "$(dirname "$file")"
    
    # Check if rule already exists
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        chmod 640 "$file"
    fi
}

# Verify setfiles binary exists
if [ ! -f "$SETFILES_PATH" ]; then
    echo "Warning: ${SETFILES_PATH} not found, but configuring audit rule anyway"
fi

# Determine the audit rules configuration approach
# RHEL 8 can use either:
# 1. /etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules files (processed by augenrules)
# 2. /etc/audit/audit.rules (loaded directly by auditd)

RULES_D_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
PRIVILEGED_RULES_FILE="${RULES_D_DIR}/30-privileged_commands.rules"

# Check if augenrules is being used
USE_AUGENRULES=false
if [ -d "$RULES_D_DIR" ]; then
    # Check if augenrules is configured (default on RHEL 8)
    if grep -q "^ExecStartPost=.*augenrules" /usr/lib/systemd/system/auditd.service 2>/dev/null || \
       [ -x /usr/sbin/augenrules ]; then
        USE_AUGENRULES=true
    fi
fi

# Add rule to rules.d directory for augenrules
if [ "$USE_AUGENRULES" = true ]; then
    # Check if rule exists in any file in rules.d
    RULE_FOUND=false
    if [ -d "$RULES_D_DIR" ]; then
        for rules_file in "$RULES_D_DIR"/*.rules; do
            if [ -f "$rules_file" ] && rule_exists_in_file "$rules_file"; then
                RULE_FOUND=true
                break
            fi
        done
    fi
    
    if [ "$RULE_FOUND" = false ]; then
        add_rule_to_file "$PRIVILEGED_RULES_FILE" "$AUDIT_RULE"
    fi
fi

# Also add to /etc/audit/audit.rules for systems that load it directly
# This ensures the rule works regardless of which method is used
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        # Insert rule before any -e 2 (immutable) line if present
        if grep -q "^-e 2" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
            sed -i "/^-e 2/i ${AUDIT_RULE}" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        else
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        fi
    fi
fi

# Load the rule into the running kernel audit system
# First check if auditd is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    # Check if the rule is already loaded in the kernel
    KERNEL_RULE_LOADED=false
    if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "path=${SETFILES_PATH}" && \
       auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "key=${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        KERNEL_RULE_LOADED=true
    fi
    
    if [ "$KERNEL_RULE_LOADED" = false ]; then
        # Check if audit rules are locked (immutable mode)
        if auditctl -s 2>/dev/null | grep -q "enabled 2"; then
            echo "Audit rules are in immutable mode. Rule will be loaded after reboot."
        else
            # Load the rule directly
            auditctl -a always,exit -F path=${SETFILES_PATH} -F perm=x -F auid>=${AUID_MIN} -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY} 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi
    
    # If using augenrules, regenerate the rules
    if [ "$USE_AUGENRULES" = true ] && [ -x /usr/sbin/augenrules ]; then
        /usr/sbin/augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
fi

echo "Audit rule for setfiles has been configured successfully."
exit 0

# Define the initialization files to check
INIT_FILES=(".bashrc" ".bash_profile" ".bash_login" ".profile" ".bash_logout" ".cshrc" ".tcshrc" ".login" ".logout" ".kshrc" ".zshrc" ".zprofile" ".zlogin" ".zlogout")

# Maximum allowed permissions (octal 0740)
MAX_PERMS=740

# Function to tighten permissions without loosening stricter ones
tighten_permissions() {
    local file="$1"
    local max_mode="$2"
    
    if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
        return
    fi
    
    # Get current permissions in octal
    current_perms=$(stat -c "%a" "$file" 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [[ -z "$current_perms" ]]; then
        return
    fi
    
    # Convert to integers for comparison
    current_owner=$((current_perms / 100))
    current_group=$(((current_perms / 10) % 10))
    current_other=$((current_perms % 10))
    
    max_owner=$((max_mode / 100))
    max_group=$(((max_mode / 10) % 10))
    max_other=$((max_mode % 10))
    
    # Calculate new permissions (bitwise AND to only remove permissions, never add)
    new_owner=$((current_owner & max_owner))
    new_group=$((current_group & max_group))
    new_other=$((current_other & max_other))
    
    new_perms="${new_owner}${new_group}${new_other}"
    
    # Only change if permissions need tightening
    if [[ "$current_perms" != "$new_perms" ]]; then
        chmod "$new_perms" "$file"
        echo "Tightened permissions on $file from $current_perms to $new_perms"
    fi
}

# Process root's home directory
ROOT_HOME=$(getent passwd root | cut -d: -f6)
if [[ -d "$ROOT_HOME" ]]; then
    for init_file in "${INIT_FILES[@]}"; do
        tighten_permissions "${ROOT_HOME}/${init_file}" "$MAX_PERMS"
    done
fi

    # Skip if UID is less than 1000 (system accounts, except root handled above)
    if [[ "$uid" -lt 1000 ]]; then

        continue

    # Skip if home directory doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -d "$home" ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Process each initialization file
    for init_file in "${INIT_FILES[@]}"; do
        tighten_permissions "${home}/${init_file}" "$MAX_PERMS"
    done
done < /etc/passwd

exit 0

SYSCTL_PARAM="kernel.dmesg_restrict"
DESIRED_VALUE="1"

# Apply the change immediately at runtime
current_value=$(sysctl -n "$SYSCTL_PARAM" 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [ "$current_value" != "$DESIRED_VALUE" ]; then
    sysctl -w "${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${DESIRED_VALUE}" >/dev/null

if [ -f "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" ]; then

        echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}" >> "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
    fi
else
    # Create the file with the setting
    echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}" > "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
fi

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Remove X Window System packages and related graphical components
# This is idempotent - dnf remove will succeed even if packages aren't installed

# List of X Windows and graphical display server related package groups and packages
XORG_PACKAGES=(
    "xorg-x11-server-Xorg"
    "xorg-x11-server-common"
    "xorg-x11-server-utils"
    "xorg-x11-server-Xwayland"
    "xorg-x11-drivers"
    "xorg-x11-drv-*"
    "xorg-x11-utils"
    "xorg-x11-xauth"
    "xorg-x11-xinit"
    "xorg-x11-fonts-*"
)

# Remove X.Org packages if they exist
for pkg in "${XORG_PACKAGES[@]}"; do
    if rpm -q "$pkg" &>/dev/null 2>&1 || rpm -qa | grep -q "^${pkg/\*/}"; then
        dnf remove -y "$pkg" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
done

# Remove graphical desktop environment groups if installed
DESKTOP_GROUPS=(
    "Server with GUI"
    "Workstation"
    "GNOME"
    "KDE Plasma Workspaces"
    "Xfce"
    "LXDE"
    "MATE"
    "Cinnamon"
    "base-x"
)

for group in "${DESKTOP_GROUPS[@]}"; do
    if dnf group list installed 2>/dev/null | grep -qi "$group"; then
        dnf group remove -y "$group" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
done

# Ensure the default target is multi-user (non-graphical)
# This is idempotent - setting it again if already set has no adverse effect
CURRENT_TARGET=$(systemctl get-default 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
if [ "$CURRENT_TARGET" != "multi-user.target" ]; then
    systemctl set-default multi-user.target
fi

# Disable and mask graphical target to prevent booting into GUI
systemctl disable graphical.target 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl mask graphical.target 2>/dev/null || true

# Disable display manager services if they exist
DISPLAY_MANAGERS=(
    "gdm"
    "lightdm"
    "sddm"
    "xdm"
    "kdm"
    "lxdm"
)

for dm in "${DISPLAY_MANAGERS[@]}"; do
    if systemctl list-unit-files "${dm}.service" &>/dev/null 2>&1; then
        systemctl stop "${dm}.service" 2>/dev/null || true
        systemctl disable "${dm}.service" 2>/dev/null || true
        systemctl mask "${dm}.service" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
done

# Clean up any remaining X11 related packages that might have been missed
dnf remove -y xorg-x11* 2>/dev/null || true

# Remove any orphaned dependencies
dnf autoremove -y 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the configuration
echo "Verification:"
echo "============="
echo "Default target: $(systemctl get-default)"
echo ""
echo "Checking for remaining X.Org packages:"
if rpm -qa | grep -i "xorg-x11-server" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "WARNING: Some X.Org server packages still present"
else
    echo "No X.Org server packages found - OK"
fi
echo ""
echo "Graphical target status:"
systemctl status graphical.target --no-pager 2>&1 | head -5 || echo "graphical.target is masked or not available"

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to disable USB video (webcam) kernel driver on RHEL 8
# This prevents the uvcvideo module from being loaded on demand or manually

MODPROBE_CONF="/etc/modprobe.d/uvcvideo.conf"
MODULES_LOAD_DENY="/etc/modules-load.d/uvcvideo.conf"

# Create or update modprobe configuration to blacklist and disable uvcvideo
# Using 'install /bin/false' prevents manual loading as well
cat > "$MODPROBE_CONF" << 'EOF'
# Disable USB video class driver to prevent webcam usage
# This prevents unauthorized surveillance or capture of information
blacklist uvcvideo
install uvcvideo /bin/false
EOF

# Ensure the modprobe.d file has correct permissions
chmod 644 "$MODPROBE_CONF"

# Create modules-load.d entry to ensure module is not loaded at boot
# Using blacklist here as well for defense in depth
cat > "$MODULES_LOAD_DENY" << 'EOF'
# Prevent uvcvideo from loading at boot
blacklist uvcvideo
EOF

# Ensure the modules-load.d file has correct permissions
chmod 644 "$MODULES_LOAD_DENY"

# If the module is currently loaded, unload it
if lsmod | grep -q "^uvcvideo"; then
    modprobe -r uvcvideo 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Rebuild the initramfs to ensure the blacklist is included
# This ensures the module cannot be loaded even during early boot
dracut -f 2>/dev/null || true

echo "USB video (uvcvideo) driver has been disabled."
echo "The change will persist across reboots."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent remediation script to disable systemd debug-shell service on RHEL 8
# This service provides unauthenticated root shell on virtual console - security risk

set -euo pipefail

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

SERVICE_NAME="debug-shell.service"

echo "Starting remediation of ${SERVICE_NAME}..."

# Stop the service if it's currently running
if systemctl is-active --quiet "${SERVICE_NAME}" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Stopping ${SERVICE_NAME}..."
    systemctl stop "${SERVICE_NAME}"
else
    echo "${SERVICE_NAME} is not currently running."
fi

# Disable the service to prevent it from starting at boot
if systemctl is-enabled --quiet "${SERVICE_NAME}" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Disabling ${SERVICE_NAME}..."
    systemctl disable "${SERVICE_NAME}"
else
    echo "${SERVICE_NAME} is already disabled or not found."
fi

# Mask the service to prevent it from being started manually or as a dependency
# Check if already masked
MASK_STATUS=$(systemctl is-enabled "${SERVICE_NAME}" 2>/dev/null || true)
if [[ "${MASK_STATUS}" != "masked" ]]; then
    echo "Masking ${SERVICE_NAME}..."
    systemctl mask "${SERVICE_NAME}"
else
    echo "${SERVICE_NAME} is already masked."
fi

# Verify the remediation
echo ""
echo "Verification:"
echo "============="

# Check if service is masked
FINAL_STATUS=$(systemctl is-enabled "${SERVICE_NAME}" 2>/dev/null || true)
if [[ "${FINAL_STATUS}" == "masked" ]]; then
    echo "[PASS] ${SERVICE_NAME} is masked"
else
    echo "[FAIL] ${SERVICE_NAME} is not masked (status: ${FINAL_STATUS})" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Check if service is inactive
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet "${SERVICE_NAME}" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "[PASS] ${SERVICE_NAME} is not running"
else
    echo "[FAIL] ${SERVICE_NAME} is still running" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Additional check: verify the mask symlink exists
MASK_LINK="/etc/systemd/system/${SERVICE_NAME}"
if [[ -L "${MASK_LINK}" ]] && [[ "$(readlink -f "${MASK_LINK}")" == "/dev/null" ]]; then
    echo "[PASS] Mask symlink correctly points to /dev/null"
else
    echo "[INFO] Mask symlink verification - link exists: $(ls -la "${MASK_LINK}" 2>/dev/null || echo 'not found')"
fi

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. ${SERVICE_NAME} is now disabled and masked."
echo "The service cannot be started manually or automatically, and this persists across reboots."

# Sets bpf_jit_harden to 2 (fully hardened mode for all users)

set -e

SYSCTL_PARAM="net.core.bpf_jit_harden"
HARDENED_VALUE="2"
SYSCTL_CONF_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-bpf-jit-harden.conf"

# Apply the setting at runtime
current_value=$(sysctl -n ${SYSCTL_PARAM} 2>/dev/null || echo "")

if [ "$current_value" != "$HARDENED_VALUE" ]; then
    sysctl -w ${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${HARDENED_VALUE}
    echo "Applied ${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${HARDENED_VALUE} at runtime"
else
    echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} is already set to ${HARDENED_VALUE} at runtime"
fi

# Ensure persistence across reboots
if [ -f "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" ]; then
    # Check if the correct setting already exists in the file
    if grep -q "^${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*${HARDENED_VALUE}$" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"; then

        # Remove any existing bpf_jit_harden settings and add the correct one
        sed -i "/^${SYSCTL_PARAM}/d" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
        echo "${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${HARDENED_VALUE}" >> "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
        echo "Updated ${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE} with ${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${HARDENED_VALUE}"

    chmod 644 "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
    echo "Created ${SYSCTL_CONF_FILE} with ${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${HARDENED_VALUE}"
fi

# Verify the settings
echo ""
echo "Verification:"
echo "Runtime value: $(sysctl -n ${SYSCTL_PARAM})"
echo "Persistent configuration:"
cat "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"

# Function to update or add fail_interval in a pam file for pam_faillock lines

    local temp_file

#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rule parameters
AUDIT_FILE="/etc/passwd"
AUDIT_KEY="audit_rules_usergroup_modification"
AUDIT_RULE="-w ${AUDIT_FILE} -p wa -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Define the audit rules directory and file
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/30-usergroup-modification.rules"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"

# Function to check if rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local rule="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -qF -- "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Check if the rule already exists in any rules file
RULE_FOUND=false
for rules_file in "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"/*.rules; do
    if [[ -f "$rules_file" ]]; then
        if rule_exists_in_file "$rules_file" "${AUDIT_RULE}"; then
            RULE_FOUND=true
            break
        fi
    fi
done

# Add the rule to our designated file if not found
if [[ "$RULE_FOUND" == "false" ]]; then
    # Check if file exists and if not, create it with proper header
    if [[ ! -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
        echo "## Audit rules for user/group modification" > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
    
    # Append the rule
    echo "${AUDIT_RULE}" >> "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    
    # Set proper permissions
    chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    chown root:root "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Also add to /etc/audit/audit.rules if it exists and doesn't contain the rule
# This ensures compatibility with systems that may read this file directly
AUDIT_RULES_LEGACY="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
if [[ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_LEGACY}" ]]; then
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "${AUDIT_RULES_LEGACY}" "${AUDIT_RULE}"; then
        # Insert before any -e 2 line if present, otherwise append
        if grep -q "^-e 2" "${AUDIT_RULES_LEGACY}"; then
            sed -i "/^-e 2/i ${AUDIT_RULE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_LEGACY}"
        else
            echo "${AUDIT_RULE}" >> "${AUDIT_RULES_LEGACY}"
        fi
    fi
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled
systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null

# Load the rules into the running kernel if auditd is active
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    # Check if the rule is already loaded in the kernel
    if ! auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -qF -- "-w ${AUDIT_FILE} -p wa -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        # Load the specific rule
        auditctl -w "${AUDIT_FILE}" -p wa -k "${AUDIT_KEY}" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    # Alternatively, reload all rules from the rules.d directory
    # Using augenrules to merge and load rules
    if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
        augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
fi

# Verify the rule is in place
echo "Verifying audit rule configuration..."

# Check persistent configuration
if grep -rq -- "${AUDIT_RULE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"/*.rules 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Persistent audit rule configured in ${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"
else
    echo "WARNING: Persistent rule may not be properly configured"
fi

# Check loaded rules if auditd is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q -- "-w ${AUDIT_FILE}.*-p wa.*-k ${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        echo "Audit rule is currently loaded in the kernel"
    else
        echo "NOTE: Rule will be loaded on next auditd restart or system reboot"
    fi
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to enable audit at boot for all kernels on RHEL 8
# This ensures auditing starts from the very beginning of the boot process

set -e

GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE="/etc/default/grub"
AUDIT_PARAM="audit=1"

# Function to check if audit=1 is already in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
check_audit_in_grub_cmdline() {
    if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"; then
        local cmdline_value
        cmdline_value=$(grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE" | sed 's/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=//' | tr -d '"')
        if echo "$cmdline_value" | grep -qE '(^|[[:space:]])audit=1([[:space:]]|$)'; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add audit=1 to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
add_audit_to_grub_cmdline() {
    if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"; then
        # Check if audit parameter exists with a different value
        if grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE" | grep -qE 'audit=[0-9]+'; then
            # Replace existing audit parameter
            sed -i 's/audit=[0-9]\+/audit=1/g' "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"
        else
            # Add audit=1 to existing GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
            sed -i 's/^\(GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="[^"]*\)"/\1 audit=1"/' "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"
            # Clean up any double spaces
            sed -i 's/  */ /g' "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"
            # Clean up space before closing quote if it's at the start
            sed -i 's/" audit=1"/"audit=1"/' "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"
        fi
    else
        # GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX doesn't exist, add it
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"audit=1\"" >> "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"
    fi
}

# Main execution

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Check if grub default file exists
if [[ ! -f "$GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE" ]]; then
    echo "Error: $GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE not found" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Check if audit=1 is already configured
if check_audit_in_grub_cmdline; then
    echo "audit=1 is already configured in $GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"
else
    echo "Adding audit=1 to $GRUB_DEFAULT_FILE"
    add_audit_to_grub_cmdline
fi

# Regenerate grub configuration for all installed kernels
# This handles both BIOS and UEFI systems
if [[ -d /sys/firmware/efi ]]; then
    # UEFI system
    GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg"
    if [[ ! -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]]; then
        GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/centos/grub.cfg"
    fi
else
    # BIOS system
    GRUB_CFG="/boot/grub2/grub.cfg"
fi

echo "Regenerating grub configuration at $GRUB_CFG"
grub2-mkconfig -o "$GRUB_CFG"

# Verify the configuration was applied
if grep -q "audit=1" "$GRUB_CFG"; then
    echo "Successfully configured audit=1 for all kernels"
else
    echo "Warning: audit=1 may not have been applied correctly to $GRUB_CFG" >&2
    exit 1
fi

echo "Configuration complete. Reboot required for changes to take effect."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to enforce dictionary word checking for passwords on RHEL 8
# This configures pwquality to reject passwords based on dictionary words

set -e

PWQUALITY_CONF="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
PWQUALITY_CONF_DIR="/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d"

# Ensure pwquality.conf exists
if [[ ! -f "$PWQUALITY_CONF" ]]; then
    touch "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
fi

# Function to set or update a parameter in pwquality.conf
set_pwquality_param() {
    local param="$1"
    local value="$2"
    local conf_file="$PWQUALITY_CONF"
    
    # Check if parameter exists (commented or uncommented)
    if grep -qE "^\s*#?\s*${param}\s*=" "$conf_file"; then
        # Parameter exists, update it
        sed -i "s/^\s*#\?\s*${param}\s*=.*/${param} = ${value}/" "$conf_file"
    else
        # Parameter doesn't exist, append it
        echo "${param} = ${value}" >> "$conf_file"
    fi
}

# Enable dictionary checking by setting dictcheck = 1
# dictcheck = 1 means passwords will be checked against dictionary words
set_pwquality_param "dictcheck" "1"

# Ensure cracklib dictionary is available for comprehensive checking

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to enforce dictionary word checking for passwords on RHEL 8
# This configures pwquality to reject passwords based on dictionary words

set -e

PWQUALITY_CONF="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
PWQUALITY_CONF_DIR="/etc/security/pwquality.conf.d"

# Ensure pwquality.conf exists
if [[ ! -f "$PWQUALITY_CONF" ]]; then
    touch "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
fi

# Function to set or update a parameter in pwquality.conf
set_pwquality_param() {
    local param="$1"
    local value="$2"
    local conf_file="$PWQUALITY_CONF"
    
    # Check if parameter exists (commented or uncommented)
    if grep -qE "^\s*#?\s*${param}\s*=" "$conf_file"; then
        # Parameter exists, update it
        sed -i "s/^\s*#\?\s*${param}\s*=.*/${param} = ${value}/" "$conf_file"
    else
        # Parameter doesn't exist, append it
        echo "${param} = ${value}" >> "$conf_file"
    fi
}

# dictcheck = 1 means passwords will be checked against dictionary words
set_pwquality_param "dictcheck" "1"

#!/bin/bash

# Account Lockout Policy Configuration Script for RHEL 8
# Configures pam_faillock to lock accounts after failed attempts
# with automatic unlock after 900 seconds

set -e

# Configuration parameters
DENY_ATTEMPTS=3
UNLOCK_TIME=900
FAIL_INTERVAL=900

# Backup function
backup_file() {
    local file="$1"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]] && [[ ! -f "${file}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d)" ]]; then
        cp -p "$file" "${file}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d)"
    fi
}

# Function to configure faillock.conf (RHEL 8 preferred method)
configure_faillock_conf() {
    local faillock_conf="/etc/security/faillock.conf"
    
    backup_file "$faillock_conf"
    
    # Create file if it doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -f "$faillock_conf" ]]; then
        touch "$faillock_conf"
    fi
    
    # Function to set or update a parameter in faillock.conf
    set_faillock_param() {
        local param="$1"
        local value="$2"
        local file="$faillock_conf"
        
        # Remove any commented or uncommented existing entries
        sed -i "/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*${param}[[:space:]]*=/d" "$file"
        
        # Add the parameter
        echo "${param} = ${value}" >> "$file"
    }
    
    # Set required parameters
    set_faillock_param "deny" "$DENY_ATTEMPTS"
    set_faillock_param "unlock_time" "$UNLOCK_TIME"
    set_faillock_param "fail_interval" "$FAIL_INTERVAL"
    set_faillock_param "audit" ""
    set_faillock_param "silent" ""
    
    # Ensure even_deny_root is set to also apply to root (optional but recommended)
    # Uncomment the following line if root should also be subject to lockout
    # set_faillock_param "even_deny_root" ""
    
    echo "Configured $faillock_conf"
}

# Function to ensure pam_faillock is properly configured in PAM files
configure_pam_files() {
    local pam_files=("/etc/pam.d/system-auth" "/etc/pam.d/password-auth")
    
    for pam_file in "${pam_files[@]}"; do
        if [[ ! -f "$pam_file" ]]; then
            echo "Warning: $pam_file not found, skipping"
            continue
        fi
        
        backup_file "$pam_file"
        
        # Check if using authselect (RHEL 8 standard)
        if command -v authselect &>/dev/null; then
            # Using authselect - we'll configure through that
            continue
        fi
        
        # Manual PAM configuration if not using authselect
        # Check if pam_faillock is already configured
        if ! grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
            # Add pam_faillock to auth section
            # Insert preauth before pam_unix
            sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix.so/i auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth' "$pam_file"
            # Insert authfail after pam_unix
            sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix.so/a auth        [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail' "$pam_file"
            # Add authsucc
            sed -i '/^auth.*sufficient.*pam_unix.so/a auth        sufficient    pam_faillock.so authsucc' "$pam_file"
            
            # Add to account section
            if ! grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file" | grep -q "account"; then
                sed -i '/^account.*pam_unix.so/i account     required      pam_faillock.so' "$pam_file"
            fi
        fi
        
        echo "Configured $pam_file"
    done
}

# Function to configure using authselect (RHEL 8 preferred method)
configure_authselect() {
    if ! command -v authselect &>/dev/null; then
        echo "authselect not available, using manual PAM configuration"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Get current profile
    local current_profile
    current_profile=$(authselect current -r 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "")
    
    if [[ -z "$current_profile" ]]; then
        # No profile selected, use sssd as default or minimal
        current_profile="sssd"
    fi
    
    # Check if we're using a custom profile
    if [[ "$current_profile" == custom/* ]]; then
        echo "Custom authselect profile detected: $current_profile"
        # For custom profiles, we need to modify the profile files directly
        local profile_name="${current_profile#custom/}"
        local profile_dir="/etc/authselect/custom/$profile_name"
        
        if [[ -d "$profile_dir" ]]; then
            # Ensure with-faillock feature is enabled in the profile
            authselect select "$current_profile" with-faillock --force 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    else
        # Standard profile - enable with-faillock feature
        echo "Enabling faillock feature with authselect"
        
        # Select the profile with faillock enabled
        authselect select "$current_profile" with-faillock --force 2>/dev/null || {
            # If that fails, try without force first to see current state
            echo "Attempting to enable faillock on profile: $current_profile"
            authselect enable-feature with-faillock 2>/dev/null || {
                # Feature might already be enabled or profile needs full select
                authselect select "$current_profile" with-faillock --force
            }
        }
    fi
    
    # Apply changes
    authselect apply-changes 2>/dev/null || true
    
    echo "authselect configured with faillock feature"
    return 0
}

# Function to verify the configuration
verify_configuration() {
    echo ""
    echo "Verifying configuration..."
    
    local errors=0
    
    # Check faillock.conf
    if [[ -f "/etc/security/faillock.conf" ]]; then
        local unlock_val
        unlock_val=$(grep -E "^[[:space:]]*unlock_time[[:space:]]*=" /etc/security/faillock.conf 2>/dev/null | tail -1 | sed 's/.*=[[:space:]]*//')
        
        if [[ -n "$unlock_val" ]] && [[ "$unlock_val" -ge 900 ]]; then
            echo "✓ unlock_time is set to $unlock_val (>= 900)"
        else
            echo "✗ unlock_time is not properly configured (value: $unlock_val)"
            ((errors++))
        fi
        
        local deny_val
        deny_val=$(grep -E "^[[:space:]]*deny[[:space:]]*=" /etc/security/faillock.conf 2>/dev/null | tail -1 | sed 's/.*=[[:space:]]*//')
        
        if [[ -n "$deny_val" ]] && [[ "$deny_val" -gt 0 ]]; then
            echo "✓ deny is set to $deny_val"
        else
            echo "✗ deny is not properly configured"
            ((errors++))
        fi
    else
        echo "✗ /etc/security/faillock.conf not found"
        ((errors++))
    fi
    
    # Check PAM files for pam_faillock
    for pam_file in /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth; do
        if [[ -f "$pam_file" ]]; then
            if grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
                echo "✓ pam_faillock.so is configured in $pam_file"
            else
                echo "✗ pam_faillock.so is NOT configured in $pam_file"
                ((errors++))
            fi
        fi
    done
    
    if [[ $errors -eq 0 ]]; then
        echo ""
        echo "Account lockout policy is properly configured."
        return 0
    else
        echo ""
        echo "Configuration has $errors error(s)."
        return 1
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Configuring account lockout policy on RHEL 8..."
    echo "Parameters:"
    echo "  - Deny after: $DENY_ATTEMPTS failed attempts"
    echo "  - Unlock time: $UNLOCK_TIME seconds"
    echo "  - Fail interval: $FAIL_INTERVAL seconds"
    echo ""
    
    # Ensure we're running as root
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Error: This script must be run as root"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Configure faillock.conf (central configuration)
    configure_faillock_conf
    
    # Configure PAM using authselect if available
    if configure_authselect; then
        echo "Successfully configured using authselect"
    else
        echo "Configuring PAM files manually"
        configure_pam_files
    fi
    
    # Verify the configuration
    verify_configuration
    
    echo ""
    echo "Account lockout configuration complete."
    echo "Users will be locked out after $DENY_ATTEMPTS failed attempts"
    echo "and automatically unlocked after $UNLOCK_TIME seconds."
    echo ""
    echo "To view locked accounts: faillock --user <username>"
    echo "To unlock an account: faillock --user <username> --reset"
}

main "$@"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash remediation script for auditing kernel module loading
# by ordinary users (UID >= 1000) on RHEL 8

set -e

# Determine system architecture
ARCH=$(uname -m)

# Define the audit rules directory and file
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/module-load.rules"

# Define the key for these rules
AUDIT_KEY="module-load"

# Create the rules directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"

# Define the 64-bit rule for init_module and finit_module syscalls
RULE_64="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S init_module,finit_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Define the 32-bit rule for init_module and finit_module syscalls
RULE_32="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S init_module,finit_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Function to check if a rule exists in the rules file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -Fxq "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Create or update the rules file
# Start fresh to ensure clean state, but preserve existing rules for this purpose
TEMP_FILE=$(mktemp)

# Add header if creating new file
echo "# Audit rules for kernel module loading by ordinary users" > "${TEMP_FILE}"
echo "# Generated by remediation script" >> "${TEMP_FILE}"

# Add 32-bit rule (applies to both 32-bit and 64-bit systems for 32-bit compatibility)
echo "${RULE_32}" >> "${TEMP_FILE}"

# Add 64-bit rule only on 64-bit systems
if [[ "${ARCH}" == "x86_64" ]] || [[ "${ARCH}" == "aarch64" ]] || [[ "${ARCH}" == "ppc64le" ]] || [[ "${ARCH}" == "s390x" ]]; then
    echo "${RULE_64}" >> "${TEMP_FILE}"
fi

# Check if the file already exists with the same content
if [[ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
    if diff -q "${TEMP_FILE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "Audit rules file already contains the correct rules. No changes needed."
        rm -f "${TEMP_FILE}"
    else
        # File exists but content is different, update it
        mv "${TEMP_FILE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        echo "Updated audit rules file: ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
else
    # File doesn't exist, create it
    mv "${TEMP_FILE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    echo "Created audit rules file: ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Load the rules into the running audit system
# Check if auditd is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
    augenrules --load > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
    echo "Audit rules loaded into running audit system."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash remediation script for auditing kernel module loading
# by ordinary users (UID >= 1000) on RHEL 8

set -e

# Determine system architecture
ARCH=$(uname -m)

# Define the audit rules directory and file
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/module-load.rules"

# Define the key for these rules
AUDIT_KEY="module-load"

# Create the rules directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"

# Define the 64-bit rule for init_module and finit_module syscalls
RULE_64="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S init_module,finit_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Define the 32-bit rule for init_module and finit_module syscalls
RULE_32="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S init_module,finit_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Function to check if a rule exists in the rules file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -Fxq "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Create or update the rules file
# Start fresh to ensure clean state, but preserve existing rules for this purpose
TEMP_FILE=$(mktemp)

# Add header if creating new file
echo "# Audit rules for kernel module loading by ordinary users" > "${TEMP_FILE}"
echo "# Generated by remediation script" >> "${TEMP_FILE}"

# Add 32-bit rule (applies to both 32-bit and 64-bit systems for 32-bit compatibility)
echo "${RULE_32}" >> "${TEMP_FILE}"

# Add 64-bit rule only on 64-bit systems
if [[ "${ARCH}" == "x86_64" ]] || [[ "${ARCH}" == "aarch64" ]] || [[ "${ARCH}" == "ppc64le" ]] || [[ "${ARCH}" == "s390x" ]]; then
    echo "${RULE_64}" >> "${TEMP_FILE}"
fi

# Check if the file already exists with the same content
if [[ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
    if diff -q "${TEMP_FILE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "Audit rules file already contains the correct rules. No changes needed."
        rm -f "${TEMP_FILE}"
    else
        # File exists but content is different, update it
        mv "${TEMP_FILE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        echo "Updated audit rules file: ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
else
    # File doesn't exist, create it
    mv "${TEMP_FILE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    echo "Created audit rules file: ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Load the rules into the running audit system
# Check if auditd is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
    augenrules --load > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
    echo "Audit rules loaded into running audit system."
else
    echo "Audit daemon is not running. Rules will be loaded on next start."
fi

# Verify the rules are in place
echo ""
echo "Verification - Rules in ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}:"
cat "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"

echo ""
echo "Verification - Active audit rules for module loading:"
auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -E "(init_module|finit_module)" || echo "Rules not yet active (auditd may need restart)"

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. Audit rules for kernel module loading have been configured."
```

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure AIDE integrity scan results to be emailed to root
# For RHEL 8 systems

set -e

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! rpm -q aide &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y aide
fi

# Ensure mailx is installed for email functionality
if ! rpm -q mailx &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y mailx
fi

# Initialize AIDE database if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz ]; then
    aide --init
    mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
fi

# Create the AIDE check script that emails results to root
AIDE_SCRIPT="/usr/local/bin/aide-check-and-email.sh"

cat > "$AIDE_SCRIPT" << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
# AIDE integrity check script that emails results to root

AIDE_OUTPUT=$(mktemp)
HOSTNAME=$(hostname)
DATE=$(date '+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')

# Run AIDE check and capture output
/usr/sbin/aide --check > "$AIDE_OUTPUT" 2>&1
AIDE_EXIT_CODE=$?

# Determine subject based on exit code
if [ $AIDE_EXIT_CODE -eq 0 ]; then
    SUBJECT="AIDE Integrity Check on $HOSTNAME - No Changes Detected - $DATE"
else
    SUBJECT="AIDE Integrity Check on $HOSTNAME - CHANGES DETECTED - $DATE"
fi

# Email the results to root
mail -s "$SUBJECT" root < "$AIDE_OUTPUT"

# Clean up
rm -f "$AIDE_OUTPUT"

exit $AIDE_EXIT_CODE
EOF

chmod 755 "$AIDE_SCRIPT"

# Configure cron to run the AIDE check daily
# Using /etc/cron.d for persistence and easier management
CRON_FILE="/etc/cron.d/aide-check"

cat > "$CRON_FILE" << EOF
# Daily AIDE integrity check with email notification to root
# Runs at 5:00 AM daily
SHELL=/bin/bash
PATH=/sbin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin
MAILTO=root
0 5 * * * root $AIDE_SCRIPT
EOF

chmod 644 "$CRON_FILE"

# Also configure via systemd timer as an alternative/backup method for RHEL 8
# This ensures the configuration works with both cron and systemd timer approaches

# Create systemd service for AIDE check
SYSTEMD_SERVICE="/etc/systemd/system/aide-check.service"

cat > "$SYSTEMD_SERVICE" << EOF
[Unit]
Description=AIDE Integrity Check with Email Notification
After=network.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=$AIDE_SCRIPT
StandardOutput=journal
StandardError=journal
EOF

chmod 644 "$SYSTEMD_SERVICE"

# Create systemd timer for daily execution
SYSTEMD_TIMER="/etc/systemd/system/aide-check.timer"

cat > "$SYSTEMD_TIMER" << EOF
[Unit]
Description=Daily AIDE Integrity Check Timer

[Timer]
OnCalendar=daily
RandomizedDelaySec=3600
Persistent=true

[Install]
WantedBy=timers.target
EOF

chmod 644 "$SYSTEMD_TIMER"

# Reload systemd daemon to recognize new units
systemctl daemon-reload

# Enable the timer to persist across reboots
systemctl enable aide-check.timer

# Start the timer if not already running
systemctl start aide-check.timer

# Ensure crond service is enabled and running
systemctl enable crond
systemctl start crond

# Configure AIDE to include email notification in its default configuration
# Update /etc/aide.conf to ensure proper reporting
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide.conf"

# Ensure the AIDE configuration has appropriate settings for detailed reporting
if ! grep -q "^report_url=stdout" "$AIDE_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    # Backup original config
    cp "$AIDE_CONF" "${AIDE_CONF}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Verify the setup
echo "AIDE email notification configuration complete."
echo "Configuration summary:"
echo "  - AIDE check script: $AIDE_SCRIPT"
echo "  - Cron configuration: $CRON_FILE"
echo "  - Systemd service: $SYSTEMD_SERVICE"
echo "  - Systemd timer: $SYSTEMD_TIMER"
echo ""
echo "Timer status:"
systemctl status aide-check.timer --no-pager || true
echo ""
echo "Cron status:"
systemctl status crond --no-pager || true

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

PARAM="pti=on"

# Check if the parameter is already in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" /etc/default/grub; then
    current_value=$(grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" /etc/default/grub | sed 's/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=//' | tr -d '"')
    
    # Check if pti=on is already present
    if echo "$current_value" | grep -qE '(^| )pti=on( |$)'; then
        echo "Kernel Page-Table Isolation (pti=on) is already enabled in GRUB configuration."
    else
        # Remove any existing pti= parameter and add pti=on
        new_value=$(echo "$current_value" | sed -E 's/(^| )pti=[^ ]*( |$)/ /g' | sed 's/  */ /g' | sed 's/^ //;s/ $//')
        if [[ -n "$new_value" ]]; then
            new_value="$new_value $PARAM"
        else
            new_value="$PARAM"
        fi
        
        # Update the GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX line
        sed -i "s|^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=.*|GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"$new_value\"|" /etc/default/grub
        echo "Added pti=on to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX in /etc/default/grub"
    fi
else
    # GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX doesn't exist, add it
    echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"$PARAM\"" >> /etc/default/grub
    echo "Created GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX with pti=on in /etc/default/grub"
fi

# Regenerate GRUB configuration
# Determine the correct grub.cfg location
if [[ -d /sys/firmware/efi ]]; then
    # UEFI system
    GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg"
    if [[ ! -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]]; then
        GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/centos/grub.cfg"
    fi
    if [[ ! -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]]; then
        GRUB_CFG="/boot/efi/EFI/BOOT/grub.cfg"
    fi
else
    # BIOS system
    GRUB_CFG="/boot/grub2/grub.cfg"
fi

if [[ -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]]; then
    grub2-mkconfig -o "$GRUB_CFG"
    echo "GRUB configuration regenerated at $GRUB_CFG"
else
    # Try common locations
    if [[ -f /boot/grub2/grub.cfg ]]; then
        grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
        echo "GRUB configuration regenerated at /boot/grub2/grub.cfg"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not find grub.cfg location. Please run grub2-mkconfig manually." >&2
    fi
fi

echo "Kernel Page-Table Isolation (pti=on) has been configured."
echo "A reboot is required for the changes to take effect."

#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rules for fchown syscall
RULE_32="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchown -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"
RULE_64="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchown -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"

# Function to check if a rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        grep -qF -- "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule to file if not present
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$rule" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
    fi
}

# Determine which audit rule mechanism to use
# RHEL 8 can use either auditd rules or augenrules

# Check if augenrules is being used
if [ -d /etc/audit/rules.d ]; then
    USE_AUGENRULES=1
else
    USE_AUGENRULES=0
fi

# Create the rules directory if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -d /etc/audit/rules.d ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d
fi

# Define the rules file for persistent rules
RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/perm_mod.rules"

# Add rules to the persistent rules file
add_rule_to_file "$RULE_32" "$RULES_FILE"
add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64" "$RULES_FILE"

# Also add to /etc/audit/audit.rules if it exists and is used
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    add_rule_to_file "$RULE_32" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Load the rules into the running kernel
# First check if auditctl is available
if command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    # Check if the rules are already loaded in the running system
    LOADED_RULES=$(auditctl -l 2>/dev/null)
    
    # Add 32-bit rule if not already loaded
    if ! echo "$LOADED_RULES" | grep -qF "arch=b32" | grep -qF "fchown" | grep -qF "auid>=1000" 2>/dev/null; then
        if ! echo "$LOADED_RULES" | grep -q "arch=b32.*fchown.*auid>=1000.*auid!=-1.*perm_mod" 2>/dev/null && \
           ! echo "$LOADED_RULES" | grep -q "arch=b32.*fchown.*auid>=1000.*auid!=unset.*perm_mod" 2>/dev/null && \
           ! echo "$LOADED_RULES" | grep -q "arch=b32.*fchown.*auid>=1000.*auid!=4294967295.*perm_mod" 2>/dev/null; then
            auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchown -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi

#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rules for fchown syscall
RULE_32="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchown -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"
RULE_64="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchown -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"

# Function to check if a rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        grep -qF -- "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule to file if not present
add_rule_to_file() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$rule" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
    fi
}

# Determine which audit rule mechanism to use
# RHEL 8 can use either auditd rules or augenrules

# Check if augenrules is being used
if [ -d /etc/audit/rules.d ]; then
    USE_AUGENRULES=1
else
    USE_AUGENRULES=0
fi

# Create the rules directory if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -d /etc/audit/rules.d ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d
fi

# Define the rules file for persistent rules
RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/perm_mod.rules"

# Add rules to the persistent rules file
add_rule_to_file "$RULE_32" "$RULES_FILE"
add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64" "$RULES_FILE"

# Also add to /etc/audit/audit.rules if it exists and is used
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    add_rule_to_file "$RULE_32" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    add_rule_to_file "$RULE_64" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Load the rules into the running kernel
# First check if auditctl is available
if command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    # Check if the rules are already loaded in the running system
    LOADED_RULES=$(auditctl -l 2>/dev/null)

    fi

chown root:root "$RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to disable CAN protocol kernel module on RHEL 8
# This script ensures the CAN protocol cannot be loaded and persists across reboots

set -e

MODPROBE_CONF_DIR="/etc/modprobe.d"
MODPROBE_CONF_FILE="${MODPROBE_CONF_DIR}/can.conf"
MODULE_NAME="can"

# Ensure the modprobe.d directory exists
if [ ! -d "${MODPROBE_CONF_DIR}" ]; then
    mkdir -p "${MODPROBE_CONF_DIR}"
fi

# Check if the install line already exists in the config file
INSTALL_LINE="install ${MODULE_NAME} /bin/false"
BLACKLIST_LINE="blacklist ${MODULE_NAME}"

# Create or update the configuration file
# Using a temporary variable to track if we need to add lines
NEED_INSTALL=true
NEED_BLACKLIST=true

if [ -f "${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}" ]; then
    # Check if install line exists
    if grep -q "^install[[:space:]]\+${MODULE_NAME}[[:space:]]\+/bin/false" "${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || \
       grep -q "^install[[:space:]]\+${MODULE_NAME}[[:space:]]\+/bin/true" "${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Update any existing install line to use /bin/false
        sed -i "s|^install[[:space:]]\+${MODULE_NAME}[[:space:]]\+.*|${INSTALL_LINE}|" "${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}"
        NEED_INSTALL=false
    fi
    
    # Check if blacklist line exists
    if grep -q "^blacklist[[:space:]]\+${MODULE_NAME}" "${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
        NEED_BLACKLIST=false
    fi
fi

# Add missing lines
if [ "${NEED_INSTALL}" = true ]; then
    echo "${INSTALL_LINE}" >> "${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}"
fi

if [ "${NEED_BLACKLIST}" = true ]; then
    echo "${BLACKLIST_LINE}" >> "${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}"
fi

# Set appropriate permissions on the config file
chmod 644 "${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}"

# If the module is currently loaded, unload it
if lsmod | grep -q "^${MODULE_NAME}[[:space:]]" 2>/dev/null; then
    modprobe -r "${MODULE_NAME}" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

echo "CAN protocol kernel module has been disabled successfully."
echo "Configuration file: ${MODPROBE_CONF_FILE}"
