#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to configure rsyslog to use GnuTLS for encrypted transport
# This ensures log records are protected in transit when forwarded to remote collectors

set -e

# Define the configuration file and settings
RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR="/etc/rsyslog.d"
RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF="${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}/00-gtls.conf"
RSYSLOG_MAIN_CONF="/etc/rsyslog.conf"

# The configuration directive to set default network stream driver to gtls
GTLS_DRIVER_DIRECTIVE='$DefaultNetstreamDriver gtls'

# Ensure rsyslog is installed
if ! rpm -q rsyslog &>/dev/null; then
    echo "rsyslog is not installed. Installing..."
    dnf install -y rsyslog
fi

# Ensure rsyslog-gnutls package is installed for GnuTLS support
if ! rpm -q rsyslog-gnutls &>/dev/null; then
    echo "rsyslog-gnutls is not installed. Installing..."
    dnf install -y rsyslog-gnutls
fi

# Create rsyslog.d directory if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -d "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}" ]; then
    mkdir -p "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}"
    chmod 755 "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}"
fi

# Function to check if the directive is already properly configured
check_gtls_configured() {
    # Check in the dedicated conf file
    if [ -f "${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}" ]; then
        if grep -qE '^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s+gtls\s*$' "${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}"; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    # Check in main rsyslog.conf
    if grep -qE '^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s+gtls\s*$' "${RSYSLOG_MAIN_CONF}" 2>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    
    # Check in any other conf.d files
    if grep -rqE '^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s+gtls\s*$' "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}/" 2>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    
    return 1
}

# Function to remove any conflicting or incorrect DefaultNetstreamDriver settings
remove_conflicting_settings() {
    # Remove from main conf if it has a different value
    if grep -qE '^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s+' "${RSYSLOG_MAIN_CONF}" 2>/dev/null; then
        if ! grep -qE '^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s+gtls\s*$' "${RSYSLOG_MAIN_CONF}"; then
            sed -i '/^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s\+/d' "${RSYSLOG_MAIN_CONF}"
        fi
    fi
    
    # Remove from conf.d files if they have different values (except our target file)
    for conf_file in "${RSYSLOG_CONF_DIR}"/*.conf; do
        if [ -f "${conf_file}" ] && [ "${conf_file}" != "${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}" ]; then
            if grep -qE '^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s+' "${conf_file}"; then
                if ! grep -qE '^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s+gtls\s*$' "${conf_file}"; then
                    sed -i '/^\s*\$DefaultNetstreamDriver\s\+/d' "${conf_file}"
                fi
            fi
        fi
    done
}

# Main configuration logic
echo "Configuring rsyslog to use GnuTLS for default network stream transport..."

# Remove any conflicting settings first
remove_conflicting_settings

# Check if already properly configured
if check_gtls_configured; then
    echo "GnuTLS network stream driver is already configured."
else
    # Create or update the configuration file
    echo "Creating/updating ${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}..."
    
    # Write the configuration
    cat > "${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}" << 'EOF'
# Configure rsyslog to use GnuTLS for encrypted transport of logs
# This ensures sensitive audit and log records are protected in transit

# Load the GnuTLS stream driver module
$ModLoad imtcp
module(load="imtcp" StreamDriver.Name="gtls" StreamDriver.Mode="1" StreamDriver.AuthMode="x509/name")

# Set the default network stream driver to GnuTLS
$DefaultNetstreamDriver gtls
EOF
    
    # Set proper permissions
    chmod 644 "${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}"
    chown root:root "${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}"
    
    echo "Configuration file created: ${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}"
fi

# Validate rsyslog configuration
echo "Validating rsyslog configuration..."
if rsyslogd -N 1 2>&1 | grep -qi "error"; then
    echo "Warning: rsyslog configuration validation reported errors."
    echo "Please review the configuration."
    rsyslogd -N 1
else
    echo "rsyslog configuration is valid."
fi

# Ensure rsyslog service is enabled and restart to apply changes
echo "Ensuring rsyslog service is enabled and restarting..."
systemctl enable rsyslog

# Restart rsyslog to apply the new configuration
systemctl restart rsyslog

# Verify the service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet rsyslog; then
    echo "rsyslog service is running with the new configuration."
else
    echo "Error: rsyslog service failed to start. Please check the logs."
    journalctl -u rsyslog --no-pager -n 20
    exit 1
fi

# Final verification
echo ""
echo "=== Configuration Summary ==="
echo "Configuration file: ${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}"
echo "Contents:"
cat "${RSYSLOG_GTLS_CONF}"
echo ""
echo "rsyslog service status:"
systemctl status rsyslog --no-pager -l | head -10
echo ""
echo "GnuTLS driver configuration has been applied successfully."
echo "The setting will persist across reboots."

echo ""

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to configure PAM password-quality to require at least one special character
# on RHEL 8 systems

set -e

PWQUALITY_CONF="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
PARAM_NAME="ocredit"
PARAM_VALUE="-1"

# Check if the configuration file exists
if [[ ! -f "$PWQUALITY_CONF" ]]; then
    echo "Error: $PWQUALITY_CONF does not exist"
    exit 1
fi

# Check if the parameter already exists in the file
if grep -qE "^\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    # Parameter exists, update it to the correct value
    # Use sed to replace the existing line
    sed -i "s/^\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=.*/${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}/" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
elif grep -qE "^#\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    # Parameter exists but is commented out, uncomment and set the value
    sed -i "s/^#\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=.*/${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}/" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
else
    # Parameter doesn't exist, append it to the file
    echo "${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE}" >> "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
fi

# Verify the change was applied
if grep -qE "^\s*${PARAM_NAME}\s*=\s*${PARAM_VALUE}" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    echo "Successfully configured ${PARAM_NAME} = ${PARAM_VALUE} in ${PWQUALITY_CONF}"
    echo "Passwords will now require at least one special (non-alphanumeric) character"
else
    echo "Error: Failed to configure ${PARAM_NAME} in ${PWQUALITY_CONF}"
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

    # Parameter exists but is commented out, uncomment and set correct value

    echo "WARNING: Verification failed. Please check ${PWQUALITY_CONF} manually"

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rules for monitoring login/logout and failed authentication files
# Compatible with RHEL 8 audit subsystem

set -e

# Define the files to monitor and the audit key
AUDIT_KEY="logins"
FILES_TO_MONITOR=(
    "/var/log/faillog"
    "/var/log/lastlog"
    "/var/run/faillock"
    "/var/log/tallylog"
)

# Define the audit rules file location for RHEL 8
# RHEL 8 uses /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory for persistent rules
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/logins.rules"

# Ensure the audit package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit package..."
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
if [ ! -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"
fi

# Function to check if a rule already exists in the current audit rules
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local path="$2"
    
    if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        grep -q "^-w ${path} -p wa -k ${AUDIT_KEY}$" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if a rule exists in any rules.d file
rule_exists_anywhere() {
    local path="$1"
    
    for file in "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"/*.rules; do
        if [ -f "$file" ]; then
            if grep -q "^-w ${path} -p wa -k ${AUDIT_KEY}$" "$file" 2>/dev/null; then
                return 0
            fi
        fi
    done
    return 1
}

# Create or update the logins.rules file
echo "Configuring audit rules for login monitoring..."

# Start with an empty rules file or preserve existing content
TEMP_RULES=$(mktemp)
RULES_CHANGED=false

# Check each file and add rules as needed
for monitored_file in "${FILES_TO_MONITOR[@]}"; do
    # Only add rule if the file/directory exists or might exist
    # Some of these may not exist on all systems, but we add the rule anyway
    # as the audit system handles non-existent paths gracefully
    
    RULE="-w ${monitored_file} -p wa -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"
    
    if ! rule_exists_anywhere "$monitored_file"; then
        echo "$RULE" >> "$TEMP_RULES"
        RULES_CHANGED=true
        echo "Adding audit rule for: $monitored_file"
    else
        echo "Audit rule already exists for: $monitored_file"
    fi
done

# If there are new rules to add, append them to the rules file
if [ "$RULES_CHANGED" = true ]; then
    # If the logins.rules file exists, preserve non-duplicate rules
    if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
        # Append new rules to existing file
        cat "$TEMP_RULES" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    else
        # Create new rules file with header
        {
            echo "## Audit rules for monitoring login/logout and failed authentication files"
            echo "## Key: ${AUDIT_KEY}"
            echo ""
            cat "$TEMP_RULES"
        } > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Set proper permissions on the rules file
    chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    chown root:root "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    
    echo "Audit rules file updated: $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
else
    echo "All required audit rules already exist. No changes needed."
fi

rm -f "$TEMP_RULES"

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
if ! systemctl is-enabled auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Enabling auditd service..."
    systemctl enable auditd
fi

if ! systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Starting auditd service..."
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Load the new rules into the running audit system
# Use augenrules to merge rules from rules.d and load them
if [ "$RULES_CHANGED" = true ]; then
    echo "Loading audit rules..."
    
    # augenrules merges all *.rules files and loads them
    if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
        augenrules --load
    else
        # Fallback: use auditctl to load rules directly
        for monitored_file in "${FILES_TO_MONITOR[@]}"; do
            # Check if rule is already active in the kernel
            if ! auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "^-w ${monitored_file} -p wa -k ${AUDIT_KEY}$"; then
                auditctl -w "$monitored_file" -p wa -k "$AUDIT_KEY" 2>/dev/null || true
            fi
        done
    fi
fi

# Verify the rules are loaded
echo ""
echo "Verifying loaded audit rules for key '${AUDIT_KEY}':"
auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -E "(-w.+-k ${AUDIT_KEY})" || echo "No rules found with key '${AUDIT_KEY}' (this may be normal if files don't exist)"

echo ""
echo "Audit rules configuration complete."
echo "Rules will persist across reboots via: $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to configure audit rules for kernel module load events
# This script is idempotent and ensures rules persist across reboots

set -e

# Define the audit rules file for persistent rules
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/modules.rules"

# Determine the architecture(s) of the system
ARCHS=()
if [ "$(uname -m)" = "x86_64" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b32" "b64")
elif [ "$(uname -m)" = "i686" ] || [ "$(uname -m)" = "i386" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b32")
elif [ "$(uname -m)" = "aarch64" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b64")
elif [[ "$(uname -m)" =~ ^arm ]]; then
    ARCHS=("b32")
elif [ "$(uname -m)" = "ppc64le" ] || [ "$(uname -m)" = "ppc64" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b64")
elif [ "$(uname -m)" = "s390x" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b64")
else
    # Default to both if architecture detection fails on x86
    ARCHS=("b32" "b64")
fi

# Define the syscalls to monitor for kernel module loading
# init_module - load a kernel module
# finit_module - load a kernel module (file descriptor based)
# delete_module - unload a kernel module (also important to track)
# create_module - deprecated but should be monitored for completeness

# Build the rules content
RULES_CONTENT="## Audit rules for kernel module loading
## Generated by remediation script
## These rules monitor attempts by non-system users (UID >= 1000) to load/unload kernel modules

"

for arch in "${ARCHS[@]}"; do
    RULES_CONTENT+="# Rules for ${arch} architecture
-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S init_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules
-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S finit_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules
-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules
"
    # create_module syscall was removed in kernel 2.6, but include for b32 for older compatibility
    if [ "${arch}" = "b32" ]; then
        RULES_CONTENT+="-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S create_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules
"
    fi
done

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
if [ ! -d "/etc/audit/rules.d" ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d
    chmod 750 /etc/audit/rules.d
fi

# Function to check if rules already exist and are correct
check_existing_rules() {
    if [ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]; then
        # Check if all required rules are present
        local all_rules_present=true
        for arch in "${ARCHS[@]}"; do
            for syscall in init_module finit_module delete_module; do
                if ! grep -q "^-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S ${syscall} -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules$" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
                    all_rules_present=false
                    break 2
                fi
            done
        done
        if [ "${all_rules_present}" = true ]; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Check if rules need to be updated
if check_existing_rules; then
    echo "Audit rules for kernel module loading are already configured correctly."
else
    echo "Configuring audit rules for kernel module loading..."
    
    # Write the rules to the persistent rules file
    echo "${RULES_CONTENT}" > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"

# Check if auditd is running before attempting to reload
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    echo "Reloading audit rules..."
    
    # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
    if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
        augenrules --load
    else
        # Fallback to auditctl if augenrules is not available
        auditctl -R "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
    
    echo "Audit rules reloaded successfully."
else
    echo "Audit daemon is not running. Rules will be loaded on next auditd start."
    # Ensure auditd is enabled to start on boot
    systemctl enable auditd
fi

# Verify the rules are active
echo ""
echo "Verifying active audit rules for kernel modules:"
auditctl -l | grep -E "modules" || echo "Warning: No active rules found with 'modules' key. Auditd may need to be restarted."

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. Kernel module load events will be audited for non-system users (UID >= 1000)."

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to configure audit rules for kernel module load events
# This script is idempotent and ensures rules persist across reboots

set -e

# Define the audit rules file for persistent rules
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/modules.rules"

# Determine the architecture(s) of the system
ARCHS=()
if [ "$(uname -m)" = "x86_64" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b32" "b64")
elif [ "$(uname -m)" = "i686" ] || [ "$(uname -m)" = "i386" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b32")
elif [ "$(uname -m)" = "aarch64" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b64")
elif [[ "$(uname -m)" =~ ^arm ]]; then
    ARCHS=("b32")
elif [ "$(uname -m)" = "ppc64le" ] || [ "$(uname -m)" = "ppc64" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b64")
elif [ "$(uname -m)" = "s390x" ]; then
    ARCHS=("b64")
else
    # Default to both if architecture detection fails on x86
    ARCHS=("b32" "b64")
fi

# Define the syscalls to monitor for kernel module loading
# init_module - load a kernel module
# finit_module - load a kernel module (file descriptor based)
# delete_module - unload a kernel module (also important to track)
# create_module - deprecated but should be monitored for completeness

# Build the rules content
RULES_CONTENT="## Audit rules for kernel module loading
## Generated by remediation script
## These rules monitor attempts by non-system users (UID >= 1000) to load/unload kernel modules

"

for arch in "${ARCHS[@]}"; do
    RULES_CONTENT+="# Rules for ${arch} architecture
-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S init_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules
-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S finit_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules
-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules
"
    # create_module syscall was removed in kernel 2.6, but include for b32 for older compatibility
    if [ "${arch}" = "b32" ]; then
        RULES_CONTENT+="-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S create_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules
"
    fi
done

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
if [ ! -d "/etc/audit/rules.d" ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d
    chmod 750 /etc/audit/rules.d
fi

# Function to check if rules already exist and are correct
check_existing_rules() {
    if [ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]; then
        # Check if all required rules are present
        local all_rules_present=true
        for arch in "${ARCHS[@]}"; do
            for syscall in init_module finit_module delete_module; do
                if ! grep -q "^-a always,exit -F arch=${arch} -S ${syscall} -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k modules$" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
                    all_rules_present=false
                    break 2
                fi
            done
        done
        if [ "${all_rules_present}" = true ]; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Check if rules need to be updated
if check_existing_rules; then
    echo "Audit rules for kernel module loading are already configured correctly."
else
    echo "Configuring audit rules for kernel module loading..."
    
    # Write the rules to the persistent rules file
    echo "${RULES_CONTENT}" > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    
    echo "Audit rules written to ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Load the rules into the running audit system
# Check if auditd is running before attempting to reload
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    echo "Reloading audit rules..."
    
    # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
    if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
        augenrules --load
    else
        # Fallback to auditctl if augenrules is not available
        auditctl -R "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
    
    echo "Audit rules reloaded successfully."
else
    echo "Audit daemon is not running. Rules will be loaded on next auditd start."
    # Ensure auditd is enabled to start on boot
    systemctl enable auditd
fi

# Verify the rules are active
echo ""
echo "Verifying active audit rules for kernel modules:"
auditctl -l | grep -E "modules" || echo "Warning: No active rules found with 'modules' key. Auditd may need to be restarted."

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. Kernel module load events will be audited for non-system users (UID >= 1000)."

# by setting a hard limit of zero on core dump size

LIMITS_FILE="${LIMITS_CONF_DIR}/99-disable-coredumps.conf"
LIMITS_ENTRY="*    hard    core    0"

# Ensure the limits.d directory exists
if [[ ! -d "${LIMITS_CONF_DIR}" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "${LIMITS_CONF_DIR}"
fi

# Check if the file exists and contains the correct setting
if [[ -f "${LIMITS_FILE}" ]]; then
    # Check if the exact setting already exists

        echo "${LIMITS_ENTRY}" > "${LIMITS_FILE}"
        echo "Updated ${LIMITS_FILE} with core dump limit"
    fi
else
    # Create the file with the setting
    echo "${LIMITS_ENTRY}" > "${LIMITS_FILE}"
    echo "Created ${LIMITS_FILE} with core dump limit"
fi

# Set proper permissions on the file
chmod 644 "${LIMITS_FILE}"

# Also check /etc/security/limits.conf for any conflicting settings
# and comment them out if they allow core dumps
MAIN_LIMITS_CONF="/etc/security/limits.conf"
if [[ -f "${MAIN_LIMITS_CONF}" ]]; then
    # Comment out any existing core settings that might override our restriction
    # Look for lines that set core limits to non-zero values
    if grep -qE '^[^#]*\s+hard\s+core\s+[1-9]' "${MAIN_LIMITS_CONF}" || \

        sed -i 's/^\([^#]*[[:space:]]\+hard[[:space:]]\+core[[:space:]]\+\(unlimited\|[1-9][0-9]*\)\)/#\1 # Disabled by security hardening/' "${MAIN_LIMITS_CONF}"
        echo "Commented out conflicting core dump settings in ${MAIN_LIMITS_CONF}"
    fi
fi

echo "Core dump restriction has been configured successfully."

DCONF_DB_DIR="/etc/dconf/db"
GDM_PROFILE="/etc/dconf/profile/gdm"
GDM_DB_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d"
BANNER_CONFIG_FILE="${GDM_DB_DIR}/00-security-settings"

LOCKS_FILE="${LOCKS_DIR}/00-security-settings-lock"

By using this IS (which includes any device attached to this IS), you consent to the following conditions:

-The USG routinely intercepts and monitors communications on this IS for purposes including, but not limited to, penetration testing, COMSEC monitoring, network operations and defense, personnel misconduct (PM), law enforcement (LE), and counterintelligence (CI) investigations.

-At any time, the USG may inspect and seize data stored on this IS.

-Communications using, or data stored on, this IS are not private, are subject to routine monitoring, interception, and search, and may be disclosed or used for any USG-authorized purpose.

-This IS includes security measures (e.g., authentication and access controls) to protect USG interests--not for your personal benefit or privacy.

-Notwithstanding the above, using this IS does not constitute consent to PM, LE or CI investigative searching or monitoring of the content of privileged communications, or work product, related to personal representation or services by attorneys, psychotherapists, or clergy, and their assistants. mkdir -p "${DCONF_PROFILE_DIR}"
mkdir -p "${GDM_DB_DIR}"
mkdir -p "${LOCKS_DIR}"

# The profile tells dconf where to look for settings
cat > "${GDM_PROFILE}" << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:gdm
file-db:/usr/share/gdm/greeter-dconf-defaults
EOF

# Create or update the banner configuration file
# This enables the banner and sets the banner text
cat > "${BANNER_CONFIG_FILE}" << EOF
[org/gnome/login-screen]
banner-message-enable=true
banner-message-text='${BANNER_MESSAGE}'
EOF

# Create or update the locks file to prevent users from changing these settings
cat > "${LOCKS_FILE}" << 'EOF'
/org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-enable
/org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-text
EOF

# Set proper permissions on configuration files
chmod 644 "${GDM_PROFILE}"
chmod 644 "${BANNER_CONFIG_FILE}"
chmod 644 "${LOCKS_FILE}"

# Update the dconf database to apply the changes
# This compiles the keyfiles into a binary database
dconf update

# Verify the configuration was applied
if [ -f "/etc/dconf/db/gdm" ]; then
    echo "GDM login banner configuration successfully applied."
    echo "Banner message enabled and locked."
    echo "Configuration will persist across reboots."
else
    echo "Warning: dconf database may not have been created properly."
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

# Idempotent script to enforce minimum password length of 15 characters on RHEL 8
# This configures the pwquality module used by PAM

PWQUALITY_CONF="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
MIN_LENGTH=15

# Ensure the pwquality.conf file exists
if [[ ! -f "$PWQUALITY_CONF" ]]; then
    echo "Error: $PWQUALITY_CONF does not exist. Ensure libpwquality is installed."
    exit 1
fi

# Check if minlen is already set correctly
if grep -qE "^\s*minlen\s*=\s*${MIN_LENGTH}\s*$" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    echo "Password minimum length is already set to ${MIN_LENGTH}. No changes needed."

if grep -qE "^\s*#?\s*minlen\s*=" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    # Replace existing minlen line (whether commented or not)
    sed -i "s/^\s*#\?\s*minlen\s*=.*/minlen = ${MIN_LENGTH}/" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
    echo "Updated minlen to ${MIN_LENGTH} in $PWQUALITY_CONF"
else
    # Add minlen if it doesn't exist
    echo "minlen = ${MIN_LENGTH}" >> "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
    echo "Added minlen = ${MIN_LENGTH} to $PWQUALITY_CONF"
fi

# Verify the change was applied
if grep -qE "^\s*minlen\s*=\s*${MIN_LENGTH}\s*$" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    echo "Successfully configured minimum password length to ${MIN_LENGTH} characters."
    exit 0
else
    echo "Error: Failed to configure minimum password length."
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rules for detecting privilege escalation
# via setuid/setgid execution on RHEL 8 systems

set -e

# Define the audit rules file path
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/privileged-execve.rules"

# Define the audit rules for detecting setuid/setgid execution
# These rules trigger when effective UID/GID differs from real UID/GID

# Rules for 32-bit architecture (b32)
RULE_32BIT_EUID="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -C euid!=uid -F euid=0 -k setuid"
RULE_32BIT_EGID="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -C egid!=gid -F egid=0 -k setgid"

# Rules for 64-bit architecture (b64)
RULE_64BIT_EUID="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C euid!=uid -F euid=0 -k setuid"
RULE_64BIT_EGID="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C egid!=gid -F egid=0 -k setgid"

# Additional rules to catch non-root privilege escalation as well
RULE_32BIT_EUID_NONROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -C euid!=uid -F euid>0 -k setuid_nonroot"
RULE_32BIT_EGID_NONROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -C egid!=gid -F egid>0 -k setgid_nonroot"
RULE_64BIT_EUID_NONROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C euid!=uid -F euid>0 -k setuid_nonroot"
RULE_64BIT_EGID_NONROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C egid!=gid -F egid>0 -k setgid_nonroot"

# Function to check if a rule exists in the file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -qF -- "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add a rule if it doesn't exist
add_rule_if_missing() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if ! rule_exists "$rule" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        echo "Added rule: $rule"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Rule already exists: $rule"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Ensure the audit package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit package..."
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d

# Create or update the rules file
echo "Configuring audit rules for privilege escalation detection..."

# Track if any changes were made
CHANGES_MADE=0

# Create the file if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "# Audit rules for detecting privilege escalation via setuid/setgid execution" > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "# Generated by privilege escalation audit configuration script" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    CHANGES_MADE=1
fi

# Add rules for root privilege escalation (euid=0 or egid=0)
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT_EUID" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT_EGID" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT_EUID" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT_EGID" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1

# Add rules for non-root privilege escalation
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT_EUID_NONROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT_EGID_NONROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT_EUID_NONROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT_EGID_NONROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
if ! systemctl is-enabled auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Enabling auditd service..."
    systemctl enable auditd
fi

if ! systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Starting auditd service..."
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Reload audit rules if changes were made
if [[ $CHANGES_MADE -eq 1 ]]; then
    echo "Reloading audit rules..."
    # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
    augenrules --load
    echo "Audit rules have been updated and loaded."
else
    echo "No changes needed - audit rules are already configured."
fi

# Verify the rules are loaded
echo ""
echo "Verifying loaded audit rules for privilege escalation detection:"
auditctl -l | grep -E "(setuid|setgid)" || echo "Warning: Rules may not be loaded yet. A reboot may be required."

echo ""
echo "Audit rules configuration complete."
echo "Rules file: $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
echo "These rules will persist across reboots."

RULE_32BIT_EUID_NONROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -C euid!=uid -F euid>0 -k setuid_nonroot"
RULE_32BIT_EGID_NONROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -C egid!=gid -F egid>0 -k setgid_nonroot"
RULE_64BIT_EUID_NONROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C euid!=uid -F euid>0 -k setuid_nonroot"
RULE_64BIT_EGID_NONROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C egid!=gid -F egid>0 -k setgid_nonroot"

# Function to check if a rule exists in the file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -qF -- "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add a rule if it doesn't exist
add_rule_if_missing() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if ! rule_exists "$rule" "$file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        echo "Added rule: $rule"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Rule already exists: $rule"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Ensure the audit package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit package..."
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d

# Create or update the rules file
echo "Configuring audit rules for privilege escalation detection..."

# Track if any changes were made
CHANGES_MADE=0

# Create the file if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "# Audit rules for detecting privilege escalation via setuid/setgid execution" > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "# Generated by privilege escalation audit configuration script" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    CHANGES_MADE=1
fi

# Add rules for root privilege escalation (euid=0 or egid=0)
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT_EUID" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT_EGID" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT_EUID" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT_EGID" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1

# Add rules for non-root privilege escalation
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT_EUID_NONROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_32BIT_EGID_NONROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT_EUID_NONROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1
add_rule_if_missing "$RULE_64BIT_EGID_NONROOT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" && CHANGES_MADE=1

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
if ! systemctl is-enabled auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Enabling auditd service..."
    systemctl enable auditd
fi

if ! systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Starting auditd service..."
    systemctl start auditd
fi

# Reload audit rules if changes were made
if [[ $CHANGES_MADE -eq 1 ]]; then
    echo "Reloading audit rules..."
    # Use augenrules to merge and load rules
    augenrules --load
    echo "Audit rules have been updated and loaded."
else
    echo "No changes needed - audit rules are already configured."
fi

# Verify the rules are loaded
echo ""
echo "Verifying loaded audit rules for privilege escalation detection:"
auditctl -l | grep -E "(setuid|setgid)" || echo "Warning: Rules may not be loaded yet. A reboot may be required."

echo ""
echo "Audit rules configuration complete."
echo "Rules file: $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
echo "These rules will persist across reboots."
```

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure GPG signature verification is enabled for local package installations
# This script configures dnf/yum to require GPG signature verification

DNF_CONF="/etc/dnf/dnf.conf"

# Function to set a configuration option in dnf.conf
set_dnf_option() {
    local option="$1"
    local value="$2"
    local conf_file="$3"
    
    # Check if the [main] section exists
    if ! grep -q '^\[main\]' "$conf_file" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Create the file with [main] section if it doesn't exist
        echo "[main]" > "$conf_file"
    fi
    
    # Check if the option already exists in the file
    if grep -q "^${option}[[:space:]]*=" "$conf_file"; then
        # Option exists, update it
        sed -i "s/^${option}[[:space:]]*=.*/${option}=${value}/" "$conf_file"
    elif grep -q "^#[[:space:]]*${option}[[:space:]]*=" "$conf_file"; then
        # Option is commented out, uncomment and set it
        sed -i "s/^#[[:space:]]*${option}[[:space:]]*=.*/${option}=${value}/" "$conf_file"
    else
        # Option doesn't exist, add it after [main]
        sed -i "/^\[main\]/a ${option}=${value}" "$conf_file"
    fi
}

# Ensure the dnf.conf file exists
if [ ! -f "$DNF_CONF" ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/dnf
    echo "[main]" > "$DNF_CONF"
fi

# Set localpkg_gpgcheck to 1 to enable GPG signature verification for local packages
set_dnf_option "localpkg_gpgcheck" "1" "$DNF_CONF"

# Also ensure gpgcheck is enabled globally for consistency
set_dnf_option "gpgcheck" "1" "$DNF_CONF"

# Verify the settings were applied correctly
echo "Verifying configuration in $DNF_CONF:"
grep -E "^(localpkg_gpgcheck|gpgcheck)" "$DNF_CONF"

# Ensure proper permissions on the configuration file
chmod 644 "$DNF_CONF"
chown root:root "$DNF_CONF"

echo "GPG signature verification for local packages has been enabled."

# Idempotent bash remediation script to audit setfacl command execution
# for users with UID >= 1000

set -e

# Define the audit rule components
SETFACL_PATH=$(which setfacl 2>/dev/null || echo "/usr/bin/setfacl")
AUDIT_KEY="perm_mod"
AUDIT_RULE="-a always,exit -F path=${SETFACL_PATH} -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Function to check if a rule exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local rule_pattern="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -qE "$rule_pattern" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule to a file if not present
add_rule_to_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local rule="$2"
    local dir=$(dirname "$file")
    
    # Create directory if it doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -d "$dir" ]]; then
        mkdir -p "$dir"
    fi
    
    # Create file if it doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
        touch "$file"
        chmod 640 "$file"
    fi
    
    echo "$rule" >> "$file"
}

# Pattern to match the setfacl audit rule (escaped for grep)
RULE_PATTERN="^[[:space:]]*-a[[:space:]]+(always,exit|exit,always)[[:space:]].*-F[[:space:]]+path=${SETFACL_PATH}.*-F[[:space:]]+perm=x.*-F[[:space:]]+auid>=1000.*-F[[:space:]]+auid!=(unset|4294967295|-1).*-k[[:space:]]+${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Check for auditd rules directory (RHEL 8 standard location)
AUDIT_RULES_D="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_D}/30-setfacl.rules"

# Also check the legacy audit.rules file
LEGACY_AUDIT_RULES="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Determine if rules.d directory exists and is being used
USE_RULES_D=false
if [[ -d "$AUDIT_RULES_D" ]]; then
    USE_RULES_D=true
fi

# Check if rule already exists in any rules.d file
RULE_EXISTS_IN_RULES_D=false
if [[ "$USE_RULES_D" == "true" ]]; then
    for rules_file in "$AUDIT_RULES_D"/*.rules; do
        if [[ -f "$rules_file" ]]; then
            if grep -qE "$RULE_PATTERN" "$rules_file" 2>/dev/null; then
                RULE_EXISTS_IN_RULES_D=true
                break
            fi
        fi
    done
fi

# Check if rule exists in legacy audit.rules
RULE_EXISTS_IN_LEGACY=false
if [[ -f "$LEGACY_AUDIT_RULES" ]]; then
    if grep -qE "$RULE_PATTERN" "$LEGACY_AUDIT_RULES" 2>/dev/null; then
        RULE_EXISTS_IN_LEGACY=true
    fi
fi

# Add rule to rules.d if using that mechanism and rule doesn't exist
if [[ "$USE_RULES_D" == "true" ]] && [[ "$RULE_EXISTS_IN_RULES_D" == "false" ]]; then
    add_rule_to_file "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" "$AUDIT_RULE"
    echo "Added audit rule to $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# For systems that might use the legacy file directly, ensure rule is there too
# This handles cases where augenrules is not used
if [[ -f "$LEGACY_AUDIT_RULES" ]] && [[ "$RULE_EXISTS_IN_LEGACY" == "false" ]]; then
    # Check if the file is auto-generated by augenrules
    if grep -q "## This file is automatically generated from" "$LEGACY_AUDIT_RULES" 2>/dev/null; then
        # File is managed by augenrules, rules.d is the source of truth
        # Run augenrules to regenerate
        if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
            augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    else
        # Legacy file is manually managed, add rule directly
        add_rule_to_file "$LEGACY_AUDIT_RULES" "$AUDIT_RULE"
        echo "Added audit rule to $LEGACY_AUDIT_RULES"
    fi
fi

# Load the rules into the running kernel
# Try augenrules first (preferred on RHEL 8), then fall back to auditctl
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null && [[ "$USE_RULES_D" == "true" ]]; then
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Also load directly with auditctl to ensure immediate effect
if command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    # Check if rule is already loaded in running kernel
    if ! auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -qE "path=${SETFACL_PATH}.*perm=x.*auid>=1000.*key=${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        auditctl $AUDIT_RULE 2>/dev/null || true
        echo "Loaded audit rule into running kernel"
    else
        echo "Audit rule already active in running kernel"
    fi
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and running
if systemctl is-enabled auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo "auditd service is enabled"
else
    systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null || true
    echo "Enabled auditd service"
fi

if systemctl is-active auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo "auditd service is running"
else
    systemctl start auditd 2>/dev/null || true
    echo "Started auditd service"
fi

echo "Audit configuration for setfacl command completed successfully"
exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# AIDE configuration file path
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide.conf"

# Check if AIDE is installed
if ! rpm -q aide &>/dev/null; then
    echo "AIDE is not installed. Installing..."
    dnf install -y aide
fi

# Check if AIDE config exists
if [[ ! -f "$AIDE_CONF" ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: AIDE configuration file not found at $AIDE_CONF"
    exit 1
fi

# Backup the original configuration
cp -p "$AIDE_CONF" "${AIDE_CONF}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"

# Create a temporary file for modifications
TEMP_CONF=$(mktemp)

# Process the AIDE configuration file
# We need to find all ruleset definitions and ensure they include 'acl'
# Rulesets in AIDE are defined as: RULESET_NAME = attribute+attribute+...

# Read the file and process line by line
while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n "$line" ]]; do
    # Check if this is a ruleset definition line
    # Ruleset definitions typically look like: NAME = attr1+attr2+attr3
    # They start with a capital letter or are known ruleset names
    # and contain '=' followed by attributes separated by '+'
    
    # Skip comments and empty lines for processing, but keep them
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] || [[ -z "${line// }" ]]; then
        echo "$line" >> "$TEMP_CONF"
        continue
    fi
    
    # Check if this is a ruleset definition (contains = and attributes with +)
    # Rulesets are typically uppercase or known names like NORMAL, PERMS, etc.
    # The pattern: WORD = something with + signs (attributes)
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*(.+)$ ]]; then
        ruleset_name="${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"
        ruleset_value="${BASH_REMATCH[2]}"
        
        # Check if this looks like an attribute list (contains + and typical AIDE attributes)
        # AIDE attributes include: p, i, n, u, g, s, b, m, c, S, acl, selinux, xattrs, sha256, etc.
        # Skip if it's a path definition (starts with / or !)
        if [[ ! "$ruleset_value" =~ ^[[:space:]]*[/!] ]] && [[ "$ruleset_value" =~ \+ ]]; then
            # This appears to be a ruleset definition with attributes
            # Check if 'acl' is already present (case-insensitive check)
            if ! echo "$ruleset_value" | grep -qi '\bacl\b'; then
                # Add 'acl' to the ruleset
                # Append +acl to the end of the ruleset value
                new_line="${ruleset_name} = ${ruleset_value}+acl"
                echo "$new_line" >> "$TEMP_CONF"
                echo "Updated ruleset '$ruleset_name' to include 'acl'"
                continue
            fi
        fi
    fi
    
    # If no modification needed, keep the original line
    echo "$line" >> "$TEMP_CONF"
done < "$AIDE_CONF"

# Check if any changes were made
if diff -q "$AIDE_CONF" "$TEMP_CONF" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "No changes needed - all rulesets already include ACL verification"
    rm -f "$TEMP_CONF"
else
    # Apply the changes
    cat "$TEMP_CONF" > "$AIDE_CONF"
    echo "AIDE configuration updated successfully"
    
    # Verify the configuration syntax
    if aide --config-check &>/dev/null; then
        echo "AIDE configuration syntax verified successfully"
    else
        echo "WARNING: AIDE configuration may have syntax issues. Please review."
    fi
fi

rm -f "$TEMP_CONF"

# Display current rulesets with their ACL status
echo ""
echo "Current AIDE rulesets status:"
echo "=============================="
grep -E '^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*[[:space:]]*=' "$AIDE_CONF" | grep -v '^#' | while read -r line; do
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*(.+)$ ]]; then
        name="${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"
        value="${BASH_REMATCH[2]}"
        if [[ "$value" =~ \+ ]] && [[ ! "$value" =~ ^[[:space:]]*[/!] ]]; then
            if echo "$value" | grep -qi '\bacl\b'; then
                echo "  $name: ACL monitoring ENABLED"
            else
                echo "  $name: ACL monitoring NOT FOUND"
            fi
        fi
    fi
done

exit 0

            fi

    fi

    echo "$line" >> "$TEMP_CONF"
done < "$AIDE_CONF"

# Check if any changes were made
if diff -q "$AIDE_CONF" "$TEMP_CONF" &>/dev/null; then
    echo "No changes needed - all rulesets already include ACL verification"
    rm -f "$TEMP_CONF"
else
    # Apply the changes
    cat "$TEMP_CONF" > "$AIDE_CONF"
    echo "AIDE configuration updated successfully"
    
    # Verify the configuration syntax
    if aide --config-check &>/dev/null; then
        echo "AIDE configuration syntax verified successfully"
    else
        echo "WARNING: AIDE configuration may have syntax issues. Please review."
    fi

rm -f "$TEMP_CONF"

# Display current rulesets with their ACL status
echo ""
echo "Current AIDE rulesets status:"
echo "=============================="
grep -E '^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*[[:space:]]*=' "$AIDE_CONF" | grep -v '^#' | while read -r line; do
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*([A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*(.+)$ ]]; then
        name="${BASH_REMATCH[1]}"
        value="${BASH_REMATCH[2]}"
        if [[ "$value" =~ \+ ]] && [[ ! "$value" =~ ^[[:space:]]*[/!] ]]; then
            if echo "$value" | grep -qi '\bacl\b'; then
                echo "  $name: ACL monitoring ENABLED"
            else
                echo "  $name: ACL monitoring NOT FOUND"
            fi
        fi
    fi
done

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to add nodev mount option to all non-root local filesystems
# This prevents device files from being interpreted on these partitions

set -euo pipefail

FSTAB="/etc/fstab"
BACKUP_FSTAB="/etc/fstab.backup.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"

# Create backup of fstab
cp "$FSTAB" "$BACKUP_FSTAB"

# Function to check if a filesystem is local (not network-based)
is_local_fs() {
    local fstype="$1"
    case "$fstype" in
        ext2|ext3|ext4|xfs|btrfs|vfat|fat|msdos|ntfs|hfs|hfsplus|ufs|jfs|reiserfs|f2fs)
            return 0
            ;;
        *)
            return 1
            ;;
    esac
}

# Function to check if mount point is root
is_root_mount() {
    local mountpoint="$1"
    [[ "$mountpoint" == "/" ]]
}

# Function to add nodev option to mount options string
add_nodev_option() {
    local options="$1"
    if echo "$options" | grep -qw "nodev"; then
        echo "$options"
    else
        if [[ "$options" == "defaults" ]]; then
            echo "defaults,nodev"
        else
            echo "${options},nodev"
        fi
    fi
}

# Process fstab entries
temp_fstab=$(mktemp)
changed=0

while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n "$line" ]]; do
    # Skip comments and empty lines
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] || [[ -z "${line// /}" ]]; then
        echo "$line" >> "$temp_fstab"
        continue
    fi
    
    # Parse fstab fields
    read -r device mountpoint fstype options dump pass <<< "$line"
    
    # Skip if we couldn't parse properly
    if [[ -z "$device" ]] || [[ -z "$mountpoint" ]] || [[ -z "$fstype" ]]; then
        echo "$line" >> "$temp_fstab"
        continue
    fi
    
    # Skip root filesystem
    if is_root_mount "$mountpoint"; then
        echo "$line" >> "$temp_fstab"
        continue
    fi
    
    # Skip non-local filesystems
    if ! is_local_fs "$fstype"; then
        echo "$line" >> "$temp_fstab"
        continue
    fi
    
    # Add nodev option if not already present
    new_options=$(add_nodev_option "$options")
    
    if [[ "$new_options" != "$options" ]]; then
        printf "%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\n" "$device" "$mountpoint" "$fstype" "$new_options" "$dump" "$pass" >> "$temp_fstab"
        changed=1
        echo "Updated $mountpoint: added nodev option"
    else
        echo "$line" >> "$temp_fstab"
    fi
done < "$FSTAB"

# Only update fstab if changes were made
if [[ $changed -eq 1 ]]; then
    mv "$temp_fstab" "$FSTAB"
    chmod 644 "$FSTAB"
    echo "Updated $FSTAB with nodev options"
else
    rm -f "$temp_fstab"
    echo "No changes needed in $FSTAB"
fi

# Remount currently mounted local filesystems with nodev option
echo "Remounting filesystems with nodev option..."

while read -r device mountpoint fstype options rest; do
    # Skip header line if present
    [[ "$device" == "Filesystem" ]] && continue
    
    # Skip root
    [[ "$mountpoint" == "/" ]] && continue
    
    # Skip non-local filesystems
    if ! is_local_fs "$fstype"; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Check if nodev is already in current mount options
    current_options=$(findmnt -n -o OPTIONS "$mountpoint" 2>/dev/null || echo "")
    
    if [[ -n "$current_options" ]] && ! echo "$current_options" | grep -qw "nodev"; then
        echo "Remounting $mountpoint with nodev option"
        mount -o remount,nodev "$mountpoint" 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not remount $mountpoint"
    else
        echo "$mountpoint already has nodev option or is not mounted"
    fi
done < <(df -PT 2>/dev/null | tail -n +2)

# Also check findmnt for any mounts not shown by df
while read -r mountpoint fstype options; do
    # Skip root
    [[ "$mountpoint" == "/" ]] && continue
    
    # Skip non-local filesystems
    if ! is_local_fs "$fstype"; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Check if nodev is already set
    if ! echo "$options" | grep -qw "nodev"; then
        echo "Remounting $mountpoint with nodev option (from findmnt)"
        mount -o remount,nodev "$mountpoint" 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not remount $mountpoint"
    fi
done < <(findmnt -n -l -o TARGET,FSTYPE,OPTIONS 2>/dev/null)

echo "Remediation complete. All non-root local filesystems are configured with nodev option."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure auditd low-space email notification on RHEL 8
# This script sets space_left_action to email so administrators are notified
# when disk space falls below the low-space warning threshold

set -e

AUDITD_CONF="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"
BACKUP_FILE="/etc/audit/auditd.conf.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Check if auditd.conf exists
if [[ ! -f "$AUDITD_CONF" ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: $AUDITD_CONF does not exist" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Function to update or add a configuration parameter
update_config() {
    local param="$1"
    local value="$2"
    local file="$3"
    
    if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*${param}[[:space:]]*=" "$file"; then
        # Parameter exists, update it
        sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*${param}[[:space:]]*=.*/${param} = ${value}/" "$file"
    else
        # Parameter doesn't exist, add it
        echo "${param} = ${value}" >> "$file"
    fi
}

# Check current value of space_left_action
current_value=$(grep -E "^[[:space:]]*space_left_action[[:space:]]*=" "$AUDITD_CONF" 2>/dev/null | sed 's/.*=[[:space:]]*//' | tr -d '[:space:]' || echo "")

if [[ "$current_value" == "email" ]]; then
    echo "space_left_action is already set to 'email'. No changes needed."
else
    # Create backup before making changes
    cp "$AUDITD_CONF" "$BACKUP_FILE"
    echo "Backup created at $BACKUP_FILE"
    
    # Set space_left_action to email
    update_config "space_left_action" "email" "$AUDITD_CONF"
    echo "Set space_left_action = email"
    
    # Ensure action_mail_acct is set (default to root if not set)
    if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*action_mail_acct[[:space:]]*=" "$AUDITD_CONF"; then
        update_config "action_mail_acct" "root" "$AUDITD_CONF"
        echo "Set action_mail_acct = root"
    else
        current_mail=$(grep -E "^[[:space:]]*action_mail_acct[[:space:]]*=" "$AUDITD_CONF" | sed 's/.*=[[:space:]]*//' | tr -d '[:space:]')
        echo "action_mail_acct is already set to: $current_mail"
    fi
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled to persist across reboots
if ! systemctl is-enabled auditd &>/dev/null; then
    systemctl enable auditd
    echo "Enabled auditd service"
else
    echo "auditd service is already enabled"
fi

# Restart auditd to apply changes (using service command as recommended for auditd)
# auditd requires special handling - use 'service' command or send SIGHUP
if [[ "$current_value" != "email" ]]; then
    # Try to reload configuration without full restart if possible
    if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
        augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    # Send SIGHUP to auditd to reload configuration
    pkill -HUP auditd 2>/dev/null || service auditd restart 2>/dev/null || systemctl restart auditd 2>/dev/null || true
    echo "Reloaded auditd configuration"
fi

# Verify the configuration
echo ""
echo "Current auditd low-space configuration:"
grep -E "^[[:space:]]*(space_left|space_left_action|action_mail_acct)[[:space:]]*=" "$AUDITD_CONF" || echo "Configuration parameters not found"

echo ""
echo "Configuration complete. Administrators will receive email alerts when disk space falls below the low-space warning threshold."

# Idempotent remediation script for RHEL 8 to configure pam_faillock with silent option
# This prevents username enumeration by suppressing informational feedback during lockout

set -euo pipefail

# Define the PAM configuration files to modify
PAM_FILES=(
    "/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
    "/etc/pam.d/password-auth"

check_root() {

        exit 1
    fi
}

# Function to backup a file if backup doesn't exist
backup_file() {

    local backup="${file}.bak.pre-silent-remediation"

}

# Function to add silent option to pam_faillock lines
add_silent_to_faillock() {
    local file="$1"
    local modified=0
    local temp_file
    temp_file=$(mktemp)
    
    while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n "$line" ]]; do
        # Check if this line contains pam_faillock.so
        if echo "$line" | grep -q "pam_faillock\.so"; then
            # Check if silent is already present
            if ! echo "$line" | grep -qw "silent"; then
                # Add silent option before the first option or at the end
                # Handle lines with pam_faillock.so
                line=$(echo "$line" | sed 's/pam_faillock\.so/pam_faillock.so silent/')
                modified=1
            fi
        fi
        echo "$line" >> "$temp_file"
    done < "$file"
    
    if [[ $modified -eq 1 ]]; then
        # Preserve original permissions and ownership
        chmod --reference="$file" "$temp_file"
        chown --reference="$file" "$temp_file"
        mv "$temp_file" "$file"
        echo "Modified: $file - added silent option to pam_faillock.so"
    else
        rm -f "$temp_file"
        echo "No changes needed: $file - silent option already present or no pam_faillock.so found"
    fi
}

# Function to configure faillock.conf (RHEL 8 preferred method)
configure_faillock_conf() {
    local faillock_conf="/etc/security/faillock.conf"
    
    if [[ -f "$faillock_conf" ]]; then
        backup_file "$faillock_conf"
        
        # Check if silent is already configured and uncommented
        if grep -q "^silent" "$faillock_conf"; then
            echo "No changes needed: $faillock_conf - silent already enabled"
        elif grep -q "^#.*silent" "$faillock_conf"; then
            # Uncomment the silent line
            sed -i 's/^#.*silent/silent/' "$faillock_conf"
            echo "Modified: $faillock_conf - uncommented silent option"
        else
            # Add silent option
            echo "" >> "$faillock_conf"
            echo "# Added by security remediation script" >> "$faillock_conf"
            echo "silent" >> "$faillock_conf"
            echo "Modified: $faillock_conf - added silent option"
        fi
    else
        echo "Warning: $faillock_conf not found, creating it"
        cat > "$faillock_conf" << 'EOF'
# Configuration for pam_faillock
# Added by security remediation script

# Suppress informational messages during authentication failures
# This prevents username enumeration attacks
silent
EOF
        chmod 644 "$faillock_conf"
        echo "Created: $faillock_conf with silent option"
    fi
}

# Function to ensure pam_faillock is properly configured in PAM files
ensure_faillock_in_pam() {
    local file="$1"
    
    if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
        echo "Warning: $file not found, skipping"
        return
    fi
    
    backup_file "$file"
    
    # Check if pam_faillock.so exists in the file
    if grep -q "pam_faillock\.so" "$file"; then
        add_silent_to_faillock "$file"
    else
        echo "Warning: pam_faillock.so not found in $file"
        echo "Consider running authselect to enable faillock feature"
    fi
}

# Function to handle authselect-managed configurations
handle_authselect() {
    # Check if authselect is managing PAM configuration
    if command -v authselect &> /dev/null; then
        local current_profile
        current_profile=$(authselect current -r 2>/dev/null || echo "")
        
        if [[ -n "$current_profile" ]]; then
            echo "Detected authselect profile: $current_profile"
            
            # Check if with-faillock feature is enabled
            if authselect current 2>/dev/null | grep -q "with-faillock"; then
                echo "Faillock feature is enabled via authselect"
                # For authselect-managed systems, configure via faillock.conf
                configure_faillock_conf
                return 0
            else
                echo "Note: with-faillock feature not enabled in authselect"
                echo "Consider enabling it with: authselect enable-feature with-faillock"
            fi
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Starting PAM faillock silent option remediation for RHEL 8"
    echo "==========================================================="
    
    check_root
    
    # First, configure faillock.conf (preferred method for RHEL 8)
    configure_faillock_conf
    
    # Check if system uses authselect
    if handle_authselect; then
        echo ""
        echo "System uses authselect - configuration applied via faillock.conf"
    fi
    
    # Also ensure PAM files have silent option (belt and suspenders approach)
    # This handles cases where faillock.conf might not be read
    echo ""
    echo "Checking PAM configuration files..."
    for pam_file in "${PAM_FILES[@]}"; do
        ensure_faillock_in_pam "$pam_file"
    done
    
    # Verify the configuration
    echo ""
    echo "Verification:"
    echo "============="
    
    if [[ -f "/etc/security/faillock.conf" ]]; then
        echo "faillock.conf silent setting:"
        grep -E "^silent|^#.*silent" /etc/security/faillock.conf 2>/dev/null || echo "  (not found)"
    fi
    
    for pam_file in "${PAM_FILES[@]}"; do
        if [[ -f "$pam_file" ]]; then
            echo ""
            echo "pam_faillock.so lines in $pam_file:"
            grep "pam_faillock\.so" "$pam_file" 2>/dev/null || echo "  (not found)"
        fi
    done
    
    echo ""
    echo "Remediation complete."
    echo "The silent option will suppress informational feedback during failed authentication attempts."
    echo "Changes will persist across reboots."
}

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure GNOME to lock screen on smartcard removal
# This sets a mandatory system-wide policy that users cannot override

set -euo pipefail

# Define the dconf database directory and file paths
DCONF_DB_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d"
DCONF_LOCKS_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d/locks"
DCONF_PROFILE_DIR="/etc/dconf/profile"

# Create necessary directories if they don't exist
mkdir -p "$DCONF_DB_DIR"
mkdir -p "$DCONF_LOCKS_DIR"
mkdir -p "$DCONF_PROFILE_DIR"

# Define the configuration file for smartcard removal settings
CONFIG_FILE="$DCONF_DB_DIR/00-smartcard"
LOCKS_FILE="$DCONF_LOCKS_DIR/00-smartcard"
PROFILE_FILE="$DCONF_PROFILE_DIR/user"

# Create or update the dconf profile to include local database
# The profile ensures system settings are read
if [[ -f "$PROFILE_FILE" ]]; then
    # Check if the local database entries already exist
    if ! grep -q "^system-db:local$" "$PROFILE_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Backup existing file
        cp "$PROFILE_FILE" "${PROFILE_FILE}.bak"
        # Ensure user-db:user is at top, followed by system-db:local
        if grep -q "^user-db:user$" "$PROFILE_FILE"; then
            # Add system-db:local after user-db:user if not present
            sed -i '/^user-db:user$/a system-db:local' "$PROFILE_FILE"
        else
            # Create proper profile structure
            cat > "$PROFILE_FILE" << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:local
EOF
        fi
    fi
else
    # Create new profile file
    cat > "$PROFILE_FILE" << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:local
EOF
fi

# Remove duplicate lines from profile if any
if [[ -f "$PROFILE_FILE" ]]; then
    awk '!seen[$0]++' "$PROFILE_FILE" > "${PROFILE_FILE}.tmp" && mv "${PROFILE_FILE}.tmp" "$PROFILE_FILE"
fi

# Create or update the smartcard configuration
# Setting removal-action to 'lock-screen' ensures screen locks when card is removed
cat > "$CONFIG_FILE" << 'EOF'
[org/gnome/settings-daemon/peripherals/smartcard]
removal-action='lock-screen'
EOF

# Create or update the locks file to prevent user override
# This makes the setting mandatory and non-overridable
cat > "$LOCKS_FILE" << 'EOF'
/org/gnome/settings-daemon/peripherals/smartcard/removal-action
EOF

# Set appropriate permissions on configuration files
chmod 644 "$CONFIG_FILE"
chmod 644 "$LOCKS_FILE"
chmod 644 "$PROFILE_FILE"

# Update the dconf database to apply changes
dconf update

# Verify the configuration was applied correctly
echo "Smartcard removal screen lock configuration applied successfully."
echo "Configuration file: $CONFIG_FILE"
echo "Locks file: $LOCKS_FILE"
echo "Profile file: $PROFILE_FILE"

# Display the current configuration for verification
echo ""
echo "Current configuration:"
cat "$CONFIG_FILE"
echo ""
echo "Locked settings:"
cat "$LOCKS_FILE"

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure persistent audit rules for monitoring
# the old password store file (/etc/security/opasswd) on RHEL 8

set -e

# Define the file to monitor and the audit key
AUDIT_FILE="/etc/security/opasswd"
AUDIT_KEY="identity"

# Define the audit rules
AUDIT_RULE_64="-a always,exit -F path=${AUDIT_FILE} -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"
AUDIT_RULE_32="-a always,exit -F path=${AUDIT_FILE} -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}"

# Directories for audit rules
AUDIT_RULES_D="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_CONF="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_D}"

# Function to check if a rule already exists in a file
rule_exists_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local path="$2"
    local perm="$3"
    local key="$4"
    
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        # Check for the rule pattern - looking for path, perm=wa, and key
        if grep -q "path=${path}.*perm=wa.*-k ${key}" "$file" 2>/dev/null || \
           grep -q "path=${path}.*perm=wa.*key=${key}" "$file" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to add rule to a file if it doesn't exist
add_rule_to_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local rule="$2"
    local arch="$3"
    
    # Create file if it doesn't exist
    if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
        touch "$file"
        chmod 640 "$file"
    fi
    
    # Check if rule already exists (check for the essential parts)
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$file" "${AUDIT_FILE}" "wa" "${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$file"
        echo "Added audit rule to $file"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Audit rule already exists in $file"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Determine the rule file name for rules.d
RULE_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_D}/75-opasswd.rules"

# Track if changes were made
CHANGES_MADE=0

# Add rules to the persistent rules.d directory
# This works with both augenrules (rule compilation) and auditctl (direct loading)

# Check system architecture and add appropriate rules
ARCH=$(uname -m)

if [[ "$ARCH" == "x86_64" ]]; then
    # For 64-bit systems, we need both 32-bit and 64-bit rules
    # Add 64-bit rule
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$RULE_FILE" "${AUDIT_FILE}" "wa" "${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        # Create or update the rule file with both architectures
        cat > "$RULE_FILE" << EOF
## Audit rules for monitoring ${AUDIT_FILE}
## These rules monitor write and attribute changes to the old password store

# 64-bit architecture
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -F path=${AUDIT_FILE} -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}

# 32-bit architecture (for 32-bit processes on 64-bit system)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -F path=${AUDIT_FILE} -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}
EOF
        chmod 640 "$RULE_FILE"
        CHANGES_MADE=1
        echo "Created audit rules file: $RULE_FILE"
    else
        echo "Audit rules already exist in $RULE_FILE"
    fi
else
    # For 32-bit systems
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$RULE_FILE" "${AUDIT_FILE}" "wa" "${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        cat > "$RULE_FILE" << EOF
## Audit rules for monitoring ${AUDIT_FILE}
## These rules monitor write and attribute changes to the old password store

# 32-bit architecture
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -F path=${AUDIT_FILE} -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}
EOF
        chmod 640 "$RULE_FILE"
        CHANGES_MADE=1
        echo "Created audit rules file: $RULE_FILE"
    else
        echo "Audit rules already exist in $RULE_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Also ensure the file being monitored exists (opasswd might not exist by default)
if [[ ! -f "${AUDIT_FILE}" ]]; then
    touch "${AUDIT_FILE}"
    chmod 600 "${AUDIT_FILE}"
    chown root:root "${AUDIT_FILE}"
    echo "Created ${AUDIT_FILE} with secure permissions"
fi

# Function to load rules into the running audit system
load_audit_rules() {
    # Check if augenrules is available and configured
    if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Loading rules using augenrules..."
        augenrules --load
    elif command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Loading rules using auditctl..."
        # Load rules directly from the rules file
        if [[ -f "$RULE_FILE" ]]; then
            while IFS= read -r line; do
                # Skip comments and empty lines
                [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] && continue
                [[ -z "${line// }" ]] && continue
                auditctl $line 2>/dev/null || true
            done < "$RULE_FILE"
        fi
    fi
}

# Load the rules if changes were made
if [[ $CHANGES_MADE -eq 1 ]]; then
    echo "Loading new audit rules into running system..."
    load_audit_rules
    echo "Audit rules loaded successfully"
else
    echo "No changes needed - rules already configured"
fi

# Verify the rules are in place
echo ""
echo "Verifying audit rules for ${AUDIT_FILE}:"
if command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    auditctl -l | grep -E "opasswd|${AUDIT_KEY}" || echo "Note: Rules may require audit daemon restart to appear"
fi

echo ""
echo "Persistent rule file contents:"
if [[ -f "$RULE_FILE" ]]; then
    cat "$RULE_FILE"
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled for persistence across reboots
if systemctl is-enabled auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo ""
    echo "auditd service is enabled - rules will persist across reboots"
else
    echo ""
    echo "Enabling auditd service for persistence..."
    systemctl enable auditd
fi

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. The audit system will now monitor ${AUDIT_FILE} for write and attribute changes."

        return 0
    else
        echo "Audit rule already exists in $file"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Determine the rule file name for rules.d
RULE_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_D}/75-opasswd.rules"

# Track if changes were made
CHANGES_MADE=0

# Add rules to the persistent rules.d directory
# This works with both augenrules (rule compilation) and auditctl (direct loading)

# Check system architecture and add appropriate rules
ARCH=$(uname -m)

if [[ "$ARCH" == "x86_64" ]]; then
    # For 64-bit systems, we need both 32-bit and 64-bit rules
    # Add 64-bit rule
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$RULE_FILE" "${AUDIT_FILE}" "wa" "${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        # Create or update the rule file with both architectures
        cat > "$RULE_FILE" << EOF
## Audit rules for monitoring ${AUDIT_FILE}
## These rules monitor write and attribute changes to the old password store

# 64-bit architecture
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -F path=${AUDIT_FILE} -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}

# 32-bit architecture (for 32-bit processes on 64-bit system)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -F path=${AUDIT_FILE} -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}
EOF
        chmod 640 "$RULE_FILE"
        CHANGES_MADE=1
        echo "Created audit rules file: $RULE_FILE"
    else
        echo "Audit rules already exist in $RULE_FILE"
    fi
else
    # For 32-bit systems
    if ! rule_exists_in_file "$RULE_FILE" "${AUDIT_FILE}" "wa" "${AUDIT_KEY}"; then
        cat > "$RULE_FILE" << EOF
## Audit rules for monitoring ${AUDIT_FILE}
## These rules monitor write and attribute changes to the old password store

# 32-bit architecture
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -F path=${AUDIT_FILE} -F perm=wa -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k ${AUDIT_KEY}
EOF
        chmod 640 "$RULE_FILE"
        CHANGES_MADE=1
        echo "Created audit rules file: $RULE_FILE"
    else
        echo "Audit rules already exist in $RULE_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Also ensure the file being monitored exists (opasswd might not exist by default)
if [[ ! -f "${AUDIT_FILE}" ]]; then
    touch "${AUDIT_FILE}"
    chmod 600 "${AUDIT_FILE}"
    chown root:root "${AUDIT_FILE}"
    echo "Created ${AUDIT_FILE} with secure permissions"
fi

# Function to load rules into the running audit system
load_audit_rules() {
    # Check if augenrules is available and configured
    if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Loading rules using augenrules..."
        augenrules --load
    elif command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Loading rules using auditctl..."
        # Load rules directly from the rules file
        if [[ -f "$RULE_FILE" ]]; then
            while IFS= read -r line; do
                # Skip comments and empty lines
                [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] && continue
                [[ -z "${line// }" ]] && continue
                auditctl $line 2>/dev/null || true
            done < "$RULE_FILE"
        fi
    fi
}

# Load the rules if changes were made
if [[ $CHANGES_MADE -eq 1 ]]; then
    echo "Loading new audit rules into running system..."
    load_audit_rules
    echo "Audit rules loaded successfully"
else
    echo "No changes needed - rules already configured"
fi

# Verify the rules are in place
echo ""
echo "Verifying audit rules for ${AUDIT_FILE}:"
if command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    auditctl -l | grep -E "opasswd|${AUDIT_KEY}" || echo "Note: Rules may require audit daemon restart to appear"
fi

echo ""
echo "Persistent rule file contents:"
if [[ -f "$RULE_FILE" ]]; then
    cat "$RULE_FILE"
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled for persistence across reboots
if systemctl is-enabled auditd &>/dev/null; then
    echo ""
    echo "auditd service is enabled - rules will persist across reboots"
else
    echo ""
    echo "Enabling auditd service for persistence..."
    systemctl enable auditd
fi

echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. The audit system will now monitor ${AUDIT_FILE} for write and attribute changes."
```

    sysctl -n "$SYSCTL_PARAM" 2>/dev/null
}

}

# Function to ensure persistent configuration
ensure_persistent_config() {
    local config_line="${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}"

    echo "$config_line" >> "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
    
    # Set proper permissions
    chmod 644 "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
}

    local conf_files

        if grep -qE "^[[:space:]]*${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=" /etc/sysctl.conf; then

#!/bin/bash

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
PARAM="X11Forwarding"
VALUE="no"

# Function to check if a line is commented
is_commented() {
    local line="$1"
    [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]]
}

# Function to get the current effective value of X11Forwarding
get_current_value() {
    # Check drop-in files first (they take precedence in RHEL 8)
    if [ -d "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
        for f in "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"/*.conf; do
            if [ -f "$f" ]; then
                local val=$(grep -i "^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]]" "$f" 2>/dev/null | tail -1 | awk '{print $2}')
                if [ -n "$val" ]; then
                    echo "$val"
                    return
                fi
            fi
        done
    fi
    
    # Check main config file
    local val=$(grep -i "^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]]" "$SSHD_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null | tail -1 | awk '{print $2}')
    echo "$val"
}

# Backup the original config if not already backed up
if [ ! -f "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak" ]; then
    cp "$SSHD_CONFIG" "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak"
fi

# Check if the parameter exists (commented or uncommented) in the main config
if grep -qi "^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*${PARAM}" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
    # Parameter exists, update it
    # First, comment out any existing X11Forwarding lines
    sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]].*/#&/" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    
    # Check if there's already a properly set line
    if grep -qi "^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]]*${VALUE}[[:space:]]*$" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
        # Already correctly set, ensure it's not commented
        sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*\(${PARAM}[[:space:]]*${VALUE}\)[[:space:]]*$/\1/" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    else
        # Replace the first commented occurrence or add new line
        if grep -qi "^#.*${PARAM}" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
            # Replace the first commented line with the correct setting
            sed -i "0,/^#.*${PARAM}.*/s//${PARAM} ${VALUE}/" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
        else
            # Add the setting
            echo "${PARAM} ${VALUE}" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
        fi
    fi
else
    # Parameter doesn't exist, add it
    echo "${PARAM} ${VALUE}" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
fi

# Ensure there's only one uncommented X11Forwarding line and it's set to 'no'
# Remove any duplicate uncommented lines, keeping only one correct setting
awk -v param="$PARAM" -v value="$VALUE" '
BEGIN { IGNORECASE=1; found=0 }
{
    if ($0 ~ "^[[:space:]]*" param "[[:space:]]") {
        if (!found && tolower($2) == tolower(value)) {
            print param " " value
            found=1
        }
        # Skip other uncommented X11Forwarding lines
    } else {
        print
    }
}
END {
    if (!found) {
        print param " " value
    }
}
' "$SSHD_CONFIG" > "${SSHD_CONFIG}.tmp" && mv "${SSHD_CONFIG}.tmp" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Set proper permissions on the config file
chmod 600 "$SSHD_CONFIG"
chown root:root "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Also check and update any drop-in config files that might override this setting
if [ -d "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
    for conf_file in "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"/*.conf; do
        if [ -f "$conf_file" ]; then
            if grep -qi "^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]]" "$conf_file"; then
                sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]].*/${PARAM} ${VALUE}/i" "$conf_file"
            fi
        fi
    done
fi

# Validate the configuration before restarting
if sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
    # Configuration is valid, restart sshd
    systemctl restart sshd
else
    echo "ERROR: SSH configuration is invalid. Please check ${SSHD_CONFIG}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Verify the setting is applied
current_value=$(get_current_value)
if [ "${current_value,,}" = "${VALUE,,}" ]; then
    echo "SUCCESS: X11Forwarding has been set to '${VALUE}'"
    exit 0
else

```bash
#!/bin/bash

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
PARAM="X11Forwarding"
VALUE="no"

# Function to check if a line is commented
is_commented() {
    local line="$1"
    [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]]
}

# Function to get the current effective value of X11Forwarding
get_current_value() {
    # Check drop-in files first (they take precedence in RHEL 8)
    if [ -d "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
        for f in "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"/*.conf; do
            if [ -f "$f" ]; then
                local val=$(grep -i "^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]]" "$f" 2>/dev/null | tail -1 | awk '{print $2}')
                if [ -n "$val" ]; then
                    echo "$val"
                    return
                fi
            fi
        done
    fi
    
    # Check main config file
    local val=$(grep -i "^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]]" "$SSHD_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null | tail -1 | awk '{print $2}')
    echo "$val"
}

# Backup the original config if not already backed up
if [ ! -f "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak" ]; then
    cp "$SSHD_CONFIG" "${SSHD_CONFIG}.bak"
fi

# Check if the parameter exists (commented or uncommented) in the main config
if grep -qi "^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*${PARAM}" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
    # Parameter exists, update it
    # First, comment out any existing X11Forwarding lines
    sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]].*/#&/" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    
    # Check if there's already a properly set line
    if grep -qi "^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]]*${VALUE}[[:space:]]*$" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
        # Already correctly set, ensure it's not commented
        sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*\(${PARAM}[[:space:]]*${VALUE}\)[[:space:]]*$/\1/" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    else
        # Replace the first commented occurrence or add new line
        if grep -qi "^#.*${PARAM}" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
            # Replace the first commented line with the correct setting
            sed -i "0,/^#.*${PARAM}.*/s//${PARAM} ${VALUE}/" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
        else
            # Add the setting
            echo "${PARAM} ${VALUE}" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
        fi
    fi
else
    # Parameter doesn't exist, add it
    echo "${PARAM} ${VALUE}" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
fi

# Ensure there's only one uncommented X11Forwarding line and it's set to 'no'
# Remove any duplicate uncommented lines, keeping only one correct setting
awk -v param="$PARAM" -v value="$VALUE" '
BEGIN { IGNORECASE=1; found=0 }
{
    if ($0 ~ "^[[:space:]]*" param "[[:space:]]") {
        if (!found && tolower($2) == tolower(value)) {
            print param " " value
            found=1
        }
        # Skip other uncommented X11Forwarding lines
    } else {
        print
    }
}
END {
    if (!found) {
        print param " " value
    }
}
' "$SSHD_CONFIG" > "${SSHD_CONFIG}.tmp" && mv "${SSHD_CONFIG}.tmp" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Set proper permissions on the config file
chmod 600 "$SSHD_CONFIG"
chown root:root "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Also check and update any drop-in config files that might override this setting
if [ -d "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
    for conf_file in "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"/*.conf; do
        if [ -f "$conf_file" ]; then
            if grep -qi "^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]]" "$conf_file"; then
                sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*${PARAM}[[:space:]].*/${PARAM} ${VALUE}/i" "$conf_file"
            fi
        fi
    done
fi

# Validate the configuration before restarting
if sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
    # Configuration is valid, restart sshd
    systemctl restart sshd
else
    echo "ERROR: SSH configuration is invalid. Please check ${SSHD_CONFIG}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Verify the setting is applied
current_value=$(get_current_value)
if [ "${current_value,,}" = "${VALUE,,}" ]; then
    echo "SUCCESS: X11Forwarding has been set to '${VALUE}'"
    exit 0
else
    echo "WARNING: X11Forwarding may not be correctly set. Current value: ${current_value}" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Configures 15-minute (900 seconds) idle timeout system-wide and locks the setting

set -euo pipefail

# Define variables

DCONF_PROFILE_DIR="/etc/dconf/profile"
DCONF_DB_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d"
DCONF_LOCKS_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d/locks"

DCONF_LOCKS_FILE="${DCONF_LOCKS_DIR}/screensaver"

mkdir -p "${DCONF_DB_DIR}"
mkdir -p "${DCONF_LOCKS_DIR}"

# Create or update dconf profile to include local database
# The profile defines the order of database lookups
if [[ -f "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}" ]]; then
    # Check if user-db and system-db entries exist
    if ! grep -q "^user-db:user$" "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then

system-db:local
EOF
    elif ! grep -q "^system-db:local$" "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Add system-db:local if missing
        echo "system-db:local" >> "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}"
    fi
else
    # Create new profile file
    cat > "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}" << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:local
EOF
fi

# This sets the idle-delay for GNOME session
cat > "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}" << EOF
[org/gnome/desktop/session]
idle-delay=uint32 ${IDLE_DELAY}

[org/gnome/desktop/screensaver]
lock-enabled=true
lock-delay=uint32 0
EOF

# Create or update the locks file to prevent user override
cat > "${DCONF_LOCKS_FILE}" << 'EOF'
/org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-delay
EOF

chmod 644 "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"
chmod 644 "${DCONF_LOCKS_FILE}"
