#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 Audit Configuration Script
# Configures audit rules to monitor chmod syscalls for permission changes
# by regular users (UID >= 1000, excluding unset login UIDs)

# Define variables
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/10-chmod-perm-mod.rules"
MIN_UID=1000
KEY="perm_mod"

# Ensure audit rules directory exists
if [ ! -d "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}" ]; then
    mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"
fi

# Get the minimum UID for regular users from login.defs if available
if [ -f /etc/login.defs ]; then
    EXTRACTED_UID=$(grep -E "^UID_MIN" /etc/login.defs | awk '{print $2}')
    if [ -n "${EXTRACTED_UID}" ]; then
        MIN_UID="${EXTRACTED_UID}"
    fi
fi

# Define the audit rules for chmod syscall
# -a always,exit : Add rule to the exit list, always generate record
# -F arch=b32/b64 : Filter by architecture (32-bit or 64-bit)
# -S chmod : Monitor the chmod syscall
# -F auid>=MIN_UID : Only apply to login UIDs at or above the threshold
# -F auid!=unset : Exclude processes with unset login UIDs (e.g., system services)
# -k KEY : Tag the audit records with the specified key

RULE_32BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S chmod -F auid>=${MIN_UID} -F auid!=unset -k ${KEY}"
RULE_64BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S chmod -F auid>=${MIN_UID} -F auid!=unset -k ${KEY}"

# Also include fchmod and fchmodat syscalls for complete coverage
RULE_32BIT_FCHMOD="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmod -F auid>=${MIN_UID} -F auid!=unset -k ${KEY}"
RULE_64BIT_FCHMOD="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmod -F auid>=${MIN_UID} -F auid!=unset -k ${KEY}"
RULE_32BIT_FCHMODAT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=${MIN_UID} -F auid!=unset -k ${KEY}"
RULE_64BIT_FCHMODAT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=${MIN_UID} -F auid!=unset -k ${KEY}"

# Create or update the audit rules file (idempotent)
cat > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" << EOF
## Audit rules to monitor permission changes (chmod, fchmod, fchmodat)
## by regular users (auid >= ${MIN_UID})
## Generated by remediation script

# 64-bit rules (must come before 32-bit for proper ordering on x86_64)
${RULE_64BIT}
${RULE_64BIT_FCHMOD}
${RULE_64BIT_FCHMODAT}

# 32-bit rules (for 32-bit binaries on 64-bit systems)
${RULE_32BIT}
${RULE_32BIT_FCHMOD}
${RULE_32BIT_FCHMODAT}
EOF

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
chown root:root "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"

# Ensure auditd service is enabled for persistence across reboots
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    systemctl enable auditd.service 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Load the new rules into the running audit system
# Use augenrules to merge and load rules from rules.d
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
elif command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    # Fallback: load rules directly with auditctl
    auditctl -R "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Verify the rules were loaded (optional check)
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    LOADED_RULES=$(auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -c "${KEY}" || echo "0")
    if [ "${LOADED_RULES}" -gt 0 ]; then
        echo "Successfully configured ${LOADED_RULES} audit rules for permission modification monitoring."
    else
        echo "Warning: Rules may not be active until auditd is restarted."
        echo "Rules have been persisted to ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 FIPS 140-2 Compliance Remediation Script
# This script enables FIPS mode for the system in an idempotent manner

set -e

# Function to check if FIPS mode is already enabled
check_fips_enabled() {
    if [ -f /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled ]; then
        fips_status=$(cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled)
        if [ "$fips_status" == "1" ]; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if FIPS mode is configured in kernel command line
check_fips_kernel_param() {
    if grep -q "fips=1" /proc/cmdline 2>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if crypto-policies is set to FIPS
check_crypto_policy() {
    current_policy=$(update-crypto-policies --show 2>/dev/null || echo "")
    if [ "$current_policy" == "FIPS" ]; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if dracut-fips is installed
check_dracut_fips() {
    if rpm -q dracut-fips &>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Main remediation logic
main() {
    echo "Starting FIPS 140-2 compliance remediation..."
    
    # Check if already fully compliant
    if check_fips_enabled && check_crypto_policy; then
        echo "System is already operating in FIPS mode. No changes needed."
        exit 0
    fi
    
    # Ensure required packages are installed
    echo "Ensuring dracut-fips package is installed..."
    if ! check_dracut_fips; then
        dnf install -y dracut-fips
    else
        echo "dracut-fips is already installed."
    fi
    
    # Check if dracut-fips-aesni is needed (for systems with AES-NI support)
    if grep -q aes /proc/cpuinfo 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "AES-NI support detected, ensuring dracut-fips-aesni is installed..."
        if ! rpm -q dracut-fips-aesni &>/dev/null; then
            dnf install -y dracut-fips-aesni || true
        fi
    fi
    
    # Set the system-wide cryptographic policy to FIPS
    echo "Setting system-wide cryptographic policy to FIPS..."
    if ! check_crypto_policy; then
        update-crypto-policies --set FIPS
        echo "Crypto policy set to FIPS."
    else
        echo "Crypto policy is already set to FIPS."
    fi
    
    # Enable FIPS mode using fips-mode-setup (RHEL 8 preferred method)
    echo "Enabling FIPS mode..."
    if command -v fips-mode-setup &>/dev/null; then
        # Check current fips-mode-setup status
        fips_setup_status=$(fips-mode-setup --check 2>&1 || true)
        if echo "$fips_setup_status" | grep -q "FIPS mode is enabled"; then
            echo "FIPS mode is already enabled via fips-mode-setup."
        else
            echo "Running fips-mode-setup --enable..."
            fips-mode-setup --enable
        fi
    else
        # Fallback: Manual FIPS configuration if fips-mode-setup is not available
        echo "fips-mode-setup not found, configuring FIPS manually..."
        
        # Add fips=1 to kernel command line via grubby
        if ! check_fips_kernel_param; then
            # Get the boot device for /boot
            boot_device=$(findmnt -no UUID /boot 2>/dev/null || findmnt -no UUID / 2>/dev/null)
            
            if [ -n "$boot_device" ]; then
                grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args="fips=1 boot=UUID=${boot_device}"
            else
                grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args="fips=1"
            fi
            echo "Added fips=1 to kernel command line."
        fi
        
        # Regenerate initramfs with FIPS module
        echo "Regenerating initramfs..."
        dracut -f
    fi
    
    # Ensure /etc/system-fips exists (marker file for FIPS mode)
    if [ ! -f /etc/system-fips ]; then
        touch /etc/system-fips
        echo "Created /etc/system-fips marker file."
    fi
    
    # Verify FIPS packages are properly installed
    echo "Verifying FIPS package integrity..."
    rpm -q dracut-fips
    
    # Check if prelink is installed and disable it (prelink breaks FIPS)
    if rpm -q prelink &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Disabling prelink (incompatible with FIPS)..."
        if [ -f /etc/sysconfig/prelink ]; then
            sed -i 's/PRELINKING=yes/PRELINKING=no/g' /etc/sysconfig/prelink
        fi
        prelink -ua 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    # Ensure openssh is configured for FIPS
    if [ -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then

#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 FIPS 140-2 Compliance Remediation Script
# This script enables FIPS mode for the system in an idempotent manner

set -e

# Function to check if FIPS mode is already enabled
check_fips_enabled() {
    if [ -f /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled ]; then
        fips_status=$(cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled)
        if [ "$fips_status" == "1" ]; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if FIPS mode is configured in kernel command line
check_fips_kernel_param() {
    if grep -q "fips=1" /proc/cmdline 2>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if crypto-policies is set to FIPS
check_crypto_policy() {
    current_policy=$(update-crypto-policies --show 2>/dev/null || echo "")
    if [ "$current_policy" == "FIPS" ]; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if dracut-fips is installed
check_dracut_fips() {
    if rpm -q dracut-fips &>/dev/null; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Main remediation logic
main() {
    echo "Starting FIPS 140-2 compliance remediation..."
    
    # Check if already fully compliant
    if check_fips_enabled && check_crypto_policy; then
        echo "System is already operating in FIPS mode. No changes needed."
        exit 0
    fi
    
    # Ensure required packages are installed
    echo "Ensuring dracut-fips package is installed..."
    if ! check_dracut_fips; then
        dnf install -y dracut-fips
    else
        echo "dracut-fips is already installed."
    fi
    
    # Check if dracut-fips-aesni is needed (for systems with AES-NI support)
    if grep -q aes /proc/cpuinfo 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "AES-NI support detected, ensuring dracut-fips-aesni is installed..."
        if ! rpm -q dracut-fips-aesni &>/dev/null; then
            dnf install -y dracut-fips-aesni || true
        fi
    fi
    
    # Set the system-wide cryptographic policy to FIPS
    echo "Setting system-wide cryptographic policy to FIPS..."
    if ! check_crypto_policy; then
        update-crypto-policies --set FIPS
        echo "Crypto policy set to FIPS."
    else
        echo "Crypto policy is already set to FIPS."
    fi
    
    # Enable FIPS mode using fips-mode-setup (RHEL 8 preferred method)
    echo "Enabling FIPS mode..."
    if command -v fips-mode-setup &>/dev/null; then
        # Check current fips-mode-setup status
        fips_setup_status=$(fips-mode-setup --check 2>&1 || true)
        if echo "$fips_setup_status" | grep -q "FIPS mode is enabled"; then
            echo "FIPS mode is already enabled via fips-mode-setup."
        else
            echo "Running fips-mode-setup --enable..."
            fips-mode-setup --enable
        fi
    else
        # Fallback: Manual FIPS configuration if fips-mode-setup is not available
        echo "fips-mode-setup not found, configuring FIPS manually..."
        
        # Add fips=1 to kernel command line via grubby
        if ! check_fips_kernel_param; then
            # Get the boot device for /boot
            boot_device=$(findmnt -no UUID /boot 2>/dev/null || findmnt -no UUID / 2>/dev/null)
            
            if [ -n "$boot_device" ]; then
                grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args="fips=1 boot=UUID=${boot_device}"
            else
                grubby --update-kernel=ALL --args="fips=1"
            fi
            echo "Added fips=1 to kernel command line."
        fi
        
        # Regenerate initramfs with FIPS module
        echo "Regenerating initramfs..."
        dracut -f
    fi
    
    # Ensure /etc/system-fips exists (marker file for FIPS mode)
    if [ ! -f /etc/system-fips ]; then
        touch /etc/system-fips
        echo "Created /etc/system-fips marker file."
    fi
    
    # Verify FIPS packages are properly installed
    echo "Verifying FIPS package integrity..."
    rpm -q dracut-fips
    
    # Check if prelink is installed and disable it (prelink breaks FIPS)
    if rpm -q prelink &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Disabling prelink (incompatible with FIPS)..."
        if [ -f /etc/sysconfig/prelink ]; then
            sed -i 's/PRELINKING=yes/PRELINKING=no/g' /etc/sysconfig/prelink
        fi
        prelink -ua 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    # Ensure openssh is configured for FIPS
    if [ -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then
        # Remove any explicit weak cipher/MAC configurations that might override FIPS
        # The crypto-policies will handle the approved algorithms
        echo "SSH will use FIPS-approved algorithms via system crypto-policies."
    fi
    
    echo ""
    echo "=============================================="
    echo "FIPS 140-2 compliance remediation complete."
    echo "=============================================="
    echo ""
    
    # Final status check
    echo "Current crypto policy: $(update-crypto-policies --show)"
    
    if check_fips_enabled; then
        echo "FIPS mode is currently ACTIVE in the running kernel."
    else
        echo "IMPORTANT: A system REBOOT is required to activate FIPS mode."
        echo "After reboot, verify with: cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled"
        echo "Expected output: 1"
    fi
    
    echo ""
    echo "To verify FIPS mode after reboot, run:"
    echo "  fips-mode-setup --check"
    echo "  cat /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled"
    echo "  update-crypto-policies --show"
}

# Run main function
main

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to set maximum password age to 60 days for new accounts on RHEL 8

LOGIN_DEFS="/etc/login.defs"
PASS_MAX_DAYS=60

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Check if the file exists
if [[ ! -f "$LOGIN_DEFS" ]]; then
    echo "Error: $LOGIN_DEFS does not exist" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Check current PASS_MAX_DAYS setting
current_value=$(grep -E "^PASS_MAX_DAYS" "$LOGIN_DEFS" | awk '{print $2}')

if [[ "$current_value" == "$PASS_MAX_DAYS" ]]; then
    echo "PASS_MAX_DAYS is already set to $PASS_MAX_DAYS. No changes needed."
    exit 0
fi

# Check if PASS_MAX_DAYS is set (uncommented)
if grep -qE "^PASS_MAX_DAYS" "$LOGIN_DEFS"; then
    # Update existing uncommented setting
    sed -i "s/^PASS_MAX_DAYS.*/PASS_MAX_DAYS\t$PASS_MAX_DAYS/" "$LOGIN_DEFS"
    echo "Updated PASS_MAX_DAYS from $current_value to $PASS_MAX_DAYS"
elif grep -qE "^#\s*PASS_MAX_DAYS" "$LOGIN_DEFS"; then
    # Uncomment and set the value
    sed -i "s/^#\s*PASS_MAX_DAYS.*/PASS_MAX_DAYS\t$PASS_MAX_DAYS/" "$LOGIN_DEFS"
    echo "Uncommented and set PASS_MAX_DAYS to $PASS_MAX_DAYS"
else
    # Add the setting if it doesn't exist at all
    echo -e "PASS_MAX_DAYS\t$PASS_MAX_DAYS" >> "$LOGIN_DEFS"
    echo "Added PASS_MAX_DAYS $PASS_MAX_DAYS to $LOGIN_DEFS"
fi

# Verify the change
new_value=$(grep -E "^PASS_MAX_DAYS" "$LOGIN_DEFS" | awk '{print $2}')
if [[ "$new_value" == "$PASS_MAX_DAYS" ]]; then
    echo "Successfully configured PASS_MAX_DAYS to $PASS_MAX_DAYS days"
    exit 0
else
    echo "Error: Failed to set PASS_MAX_DAYS correctly" >&2
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! rpm -q aide &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y aide
fi

# Define AIDE database paths
AIDE_DB="/var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz"
AIDE_DB_NEW="/var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz"

# Check if a valid baseline database exists
if [[ -f "$AIDE_DB" ]]; then
    # Verify the database is valid by checking if AIDE can read it
    if aide --check --config=/etc/aide.conf &>/dev/null || [[ $? -eq 0 ]] || [[ $? -eq 1 ]] || [[ $? -eq 2 ]] || [[ $? -eq 3 ]] || [[ $? -eq 4 ]] || [[ $? -eq 5 ]] || [[ $? -eq 6 ]] || [[ $? -eq 7 ]]; then
        # Database exists and AIDE can process it (exit codes 0-7 indicate AIDE ran successfully, even if changes detected)
        # A corrupted database would cause a different error
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Generate new AIDE database
aide --init --config=/etc/aide.conf

# Move the newly generated database to the active location
if [[ -f "$AIDE_DB_NEW" ]]; then
    mv -f "$AIDE_DB_NEW" "$AIDE_DB"
fi

# Set proper permissions on the database
chmod 600 "$AIDE_DB"
chown root:root "$AIDE_DB"

# Verify the database was created successfully
if [[ -f "$AIDE_DB" ]]; then
    exit 0
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to create AIDE database" >&2
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash remediation script for RHEL 8 system logging configuration
# Ensures authentication, privileged-authentication, and daemon messages are logged properly

set -e

RSYSLOG_CONF="/etc/rsyslog.conf"
RSYSLOG_D_DIR="/etc/rsyslog.d"
CUSTOM_CONF="${RSYSLOG_D_DIR}/10-auth-daemon-logging.conf"

# Ensure rsyslog is installed
if ! rpm -q rsyslog &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing rsyslog..."
    dnf install -y rsyslog
fi

# Ensure rsyslog service is enabled and running
systemctl enable rsyslog
systemctl start rsyslog

# Ensure /etc/rsyslog.d directory exists
if [ ! -d "$RSYSLOG_D_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$RSYSLOG_D_DIR"
fi

# Check if rsyslog.conf includes files from rsyslog.d
if ! grep -q '^\$IncludeConfig /etc/rsyslog.d/\*\.conf' "$RSYSLOG_CONF" && \
   ! grep -q '^include(file="/etc/rsyslog.d/\*\.conf")' "$RSYSLOG_CONF"; then
    # Add include directive if not present
    echo '$IncludeConfig /etc/rsyslog.d/*.conf' >> "$RSYSLOG_CONF"
fi

# Define the logging rules we need
AUTH_RULE='auth,authpriv.*                                              /var/log/secure'
DAEMON_RULE='daemon.*                                                    /var/log/messages'

# Function to check if a rule exists in rsyslog configuration
rule_exists() {
    local facility_selector="$1"
    local log_file="$2"
    
    # Check in main config and all .conf files in rsyslog.d
    # Look for the facility selector routing to the specified log file
    grep -r "^[[:space:]]*${facility_selector}" "$RSYSLOG_CONF" "$RSYSLOG_D_DIR"/*.conf 2>/dev/null | \
        grep -q "${log_file}" && return 0
    
    return 1
}

# Function to check for auth/authpriv to /var/log/secure
check_auth_logging() {
    # Check for various forms of auth logging to secure
    grep -r '^[[:space:]]*auth\.\*' "$RSYSLOG_CONF" "$RSYSLOG_D_DIR"/*.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -q '/var/log/secure' && \
    grep -r '^[[:space:]]*authpriv\.\*' "$RSYSLOG_CONF" "$RSYSLOG_D_DIR"/*.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -q '/var/log/secure' && return 0
    
    grep -r '^[[:space:]]*auth,authpriv\.\*' "$RSYSLOG_CONF" "$RSYSLOG_D_DIR"/*.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -q '/var/log/secure' && return 0
    
    return 1
}

# Function to check for daemon to /var/log/messages
check_daemon_logging() {
    grep -r '^[[:space:]]*daemon\.\*' "$RSYSLOG_CONF" "$RSYSLOG_D_DIR"/*.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -q '/var/log/messages' && return 0
    
    # Also check if daemon is included in a combined rule going to messages
    grep -r '^\*\.\*' "$RSYSLOG_CONF" "$RSYSLOG_D_DIR"/*.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -q '/var/log/messages' && return 0
    
    return 1
}

# Create or update custom configuration file
NEEDS_AUTH=false
NEEDS_DAEMON=false

if ! check_auth_logging; then
    NEEDS_AUTH=true
fi

if ! check_daemon_logging; then
    NEEDS_DAEMON=true
fi

# If either rule is missing, create/update the custom config file
if [ "$NEEDS_AUTH" = true ] || [ "$NEEDS_DAEMON" = true ]; then
    echo "Configuring rsyslog rules..."
    
    # Create the custom configuration file
    cat > "$CUSTOM_CONF" << 'EOF'
# Custom logging configuration for security compliance
# Authentication and privileged authentication messages to /var/log/secure
# Daemon messages to /var/log/messages

# Route all authentication and privileged authentication messages to secure log
auth,authpriv.*                                              /var/log/secure

# Route all daemon messages to the general system message log
daemon.*                                                     /var/log/messages
EOF
    
    chmod 644 "$CUSTOM_CONF"
    echo "Created/updated $CUSTOM_CONF"
fi

# Ensure log files exist with proper permissions
LOG_FILES="/var/log/secure /var/log/messages"

for log_file in $LOG_FILES; do
    if [ ! -f "$log_file" ]; then
        touch "$log_file"
        echo "Created $log_file"
    fi
    
    # Set appropriate permissions (readable only by root)
    chmod 600 "$log_file"
    chown root:root "$log_file"
done

# Validate rsyslog configuration
if rsyslogd -N1 &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Rsyslog configuration is valid."
else
    echo "Warning: Rsyslog configuration validation failed. Checking syntax..."
    rsyslogd -N1
fi

# Restart rsyslog to apply changes
systemctl restart rsyslog

# Verify rsyslog is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet rsyslog; then
    echo "Rsyslog service is running."
else
    echo "Error: Rsyslog service failed to start."
    exit 1
fi

# Ensure rsyslog is enabled to start on boot
if systemctl is-enabled --quiet rsyslog; then
    echo "Rsyslog service is enabled to start on boot."
else
    systemctl enable rsyslog
    echo "Enabled rsyslog service to start on boot."
fi

# Test logging functionality
logger -p auth.info "Test authentication log entry - configuration verification"
logger -p authpriv.info "Test privileged authentication log entry - configuration verification"
logger -p daemon.info "Test daemon log entry - configuration verification"

# Brief pause to allow log entries to be written
sleep 1

# Verify test entries were logged
echo ""
echo "Verification of logging configuration:"

if grep -q "Test authentication log entry" /var/log/secure 2>/dev/null || \
   grep -q "Test privileged authentication log entry" /var/log/secure 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "✓ Authentication messages are being logged to /var/log/secure"
else
    echo "⚠ Warning: Authentication test message not found in /var/log/secure"
fi

if grep -q "Test daemon log entry" /var/log/messages 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "✓ Daemon messages are being logged to /var/log/messages"
else
    echo "⚠ Warning: Daemon test message not found in /var/log/messages"
fi

echo ""
echo "System logging configuration completed successfully."
echo "Authentication and privileged-authentication messages -> /var/log/secure"
echo "Daemon messages -> /var/log/messages"
echo "Configuration will persist across reboots."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to disable user list on GNOME login screen (GDM)
# This creates a system-wide dconf policy that cannot be overridden by users

set -e

# Define paths
DCONF_PROFILE_DIR="/etc/dconf/profile"
DCONF_DB_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d"
DCONF_PROFILE_FILE="${DCONF_PROFILE_DIR}/gdm"
DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE="${DCONF_DB_DIR}/00-login-screen"
DCONF_LOCKS_DIR="${DCONF_DB_DIR}/locks"
DCONF_LOCKS_FILE="${DCONF_LOCKS_DIR}/00-login-screen"

# Create directories if they don't exist
mkdir -p "${DCONF_PROFILE_DIR}"
mkdir -p "${DCONF_DB_DIR}"
mkdir -p "${DCONF_LOCKS_DIR}"

# Create or update the GDM dconf profile
# This profile tells GDM to use the gdm database
PROFILE_CONTENT="user-db:user
system-db:gdm
file-db:/usr/share/gdm/greeter-dconf-defaults"

if [[ -f "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}" ]]; then
    # Check if the profile already has the correct content
    if ! grep -q "^system-db:gdm$" "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}"; then
        echo "${PROFILE_CONTENT}" > "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}"
    fi
else
    echo "${PROFILE_CONTENT}" > "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}"
fi

# Create or update the settings file to disable user list
SETTINGS_CONTENT="[org/gnome/login-screen]
disable-user-list=true"

if [[ -f "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}" ]]; then
    # Check if the setting already exists with correct value
    if grep -q "^\[org/gnome/login-screen\]" "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"; then
        # Section exists, check for the setting
        if grep -q "^disable-user-list=true$" "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"; then
            : # Setting already correct, do nothing
        elif grep -q "^disable-user-list=" "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"; then
            # Setting exists but with wrong value, update it
            sed -i 's/^disable-user-list=.*/disable-user-list=true/' "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"
        else
            # Section exists but setting is missing, add it
            sed -i '/^\[org\/gnome\/login-screen\]/a disable-user-list=true' "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"
        fi
    else
        # Section doesn't exist, append it
        echo "" >> "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"
        echo "${SETTINGS_CONTENT}" >> "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"
    fi
else
    echo "${SETTINGS_CONTENT}" > "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"
fi

# Create or update the locks file to prevent user override
LOCKS_CONTENT="/org/gnome/login-screen/disable-user-list"

if [[ -f "${DCONF_LOCKS_FILE}" ]]; then
    if ! grep -q "^/org/gnome/login-screen/disable-user-list$" "${DCONF_LOCKS_FILE}"; then
        echo "${LOCKS_CONTENT}" >> "${DCONF_LOCKS_FILE}"
    fi
else
    echo "${LOCKS_CONTENT}" > "${DCONF_LOCKS_FILE}"
fi

# Set proper permissions
chmod 644 "${DCONF_PROFILE_FILE}"
chmod 644 "${DCONF_SETTINGS_FILE}"
chmod 644 "${DCONF_LOCKS_FILE}"

# Update the dconf database to apply changes
dconf update

echo "GDM user list has been disabled. The setting is locked and will persist across reboots."
echo "A reboot or GDM restart may be required for changes to take effect."

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure auditd is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Ensure auditd is enabled and started
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Define the audit rules directory and file
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/50-perm_mod.rules"

# Define the rules for fchmod syscall (changes DAC permissions on an open file)
# For 64-bit systems, we need both 32-bit (b32) and 64-bit (b64) rules
RULE_64BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmod -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"
RULE_32BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmod -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k perm_mod"

# Create the rules directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"

# Function to check if a rule exists in the file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        grep -qFx "$rule" "$file" 2>/dev/null
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Check if the rules file exists and contains our rules
RULES_ADDED=0

# Check and add 64-bit rule
if ! rule_exists "$RULE_64BIT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    echo "$RULE_64BIT" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    RULES_ADDED=1
fi

# Check and add 32-bit rule
if ! rule_exists "$RULE_32BIT" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    echo "$RULE_32BIT" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    RULES_ADDED=1
fi

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# If rules were added, reload the audit rules
if [[ $RULES_ADDED -eq 1 ]]; then
    # Load the rules into the running audit daemon
    augenrules --load
fi

# Verify the rules are loaded
echo "Verifying audit rules are loaded:"
auditctl -l | grep -E "fchmod.*perm_mod"

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Define the directory containing audit rule files
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"

# Check if the audit rules directory exists
if [[ ! -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ]]; then
    echo "Audit rules directory $AUDIT_RULES_DIR does not exist."
    exit 0
fi

# Find all audit rule definition files and set permissions to 0600
# Audit rule files typically have .rules extension
find "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" -type f -name "*.rules" | while read -r rule_file; do
    # Get current permissions
    current_perms=$(stat -c "%a" "$rule_file" 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [[ "$current_perms" != "600" ]]; then
        echo "Setting permissions on $rule_file from $current_perms to 0600"
        chmod 0600 "$rule_file"
    fi
done

# Also check the main audit.rules file if it exists
MAIN_AUDIT_RULES="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
if [[ -f "$MAIN_AUDIT_RULES" ]]; then
    current_perms=$(stat -c "%a" "$MAIN_AUDIT_RULES" 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [[ "$current_perms" != "600" ]]; then
        echo "Setting permissions on $MAIN_AUDIT_RULES from $current_perms to 0600"
        chmod 0600 "$MAIN_AUDIT_RULES"
    fi
fi

echo "Audit rule file permissions remediation complete."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to configure audit rules for failed file truncation attempts
# due to permission denied (EACCES) or operation not permitted (EPERM) errors
# for regular user accounts (UID >= 1000)

set -e

AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/30-access-truncate.rules"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"

# Define the audit rules for truncate syscalls
# -a always,exit: Add rule to exit list, always trigger
# -F arch=b32/b64: Architecture filter
# -S truncate,ftruncate: System calls to monitor
# -F exit=-EACCES/-EPERM: Only when exit code is permission denied or operation not permitted
# -F auid>=1000: Only for login UIDs >= 1000 (regular users)
# -F auid!=unset (or auid!=-1): Exclude processes with unset audit UID
# -k access: Tag with key "access"

# Create the rules file with all required rules
cat > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" << 'EOF'
## Audit rules for failed file truncation attempts (permission denied/operation not permitted)
## For regular user accounts (UID >= 1000, excluding unset UIDs)
## Tagged with key: access

# 32-bit syscalls - truncate with EACCES
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S truncate -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

# 32-bit syscalls - truncate with EPERM
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S truncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

# 32-bit syscalls - ftruncate with EACCES
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S ftruncate -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

# 32-bit syscalls - ftruncate with EPERM
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S ftruncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

# 64-bit syscalls - truncate with EACCES
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S truncate -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

# 64-bit syscalls - truncate with EPERM
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S truncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

# 64-bit syscalls - ftruncate with EACCES
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S ftruncate -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access

# 64-bit syscalls - ftruncate with EPERM
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S ftruncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k access
EOF

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
chown root:root "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"

# Ensure auditd service is enabled to persist across reboots
systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null || true

# Load the new rules into the running audit system
# Use augenrules to merge and load rules from /etc/audit/rules.d/
if command -v augenrules &>/dev/null; then
    augenrules --load
else
    # Fallback: directly load the rules file
    auditctl -R "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

echo "Audit rules for failed truncate attempts have been configured successfully."
echo "Rules file: ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
echo "Rules will persist across reboots and are now active."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to configure chrony in client-only mode on RHEL 8
# This prevents chrony from listening on any network ports for serving time

set -e

CHRONY_CONF="/etc/chrony.conf"
CHRONY_CONF_DIR="/etc/chrony.d"

# Function to check if a directive exists in chrony configuration
directive_exists() {
    local directive="$1"
    grep -qE "^\s*${directive}\s*$" "$CHRONY_CONF" 2>/dev/null || \
    ([ -d "$CHRONY_CONF_DIR" ] && grep -rqE "^\s*${directive}\s*$" "$CHRONY_CONF_DIR" 2>/dev/null)
}

# Function to check if a directive with value exists
directive_with_value_exists() {
    local directive="$1"
    local value="$2"
    grep -qE "^\s*${directive}\s+${value}" "$CHRONY_CONF" 2>/dev/null || \
    ([ -d "$CHRONY_CONF_DIR" ] && grep -rqE "^\s*${directive}\s+${value}" "$CHRONY_CONF_DIR" 2>/dev/null)
}

# Backup original configuration if not already backed up
if [ ! -f "${CHRONY_CONF}.orig" ]; then
    cp "$CHRONY_CONF" "${CHRONY_CONF}.orig"
fi

# Remove or comment out 'allow' directives that permit NTP clients
# These directives allow other hosts to use this system as an NTP server
if grep -qE "^\s*allow" "$CHRONY_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    sed -i 's/^\s*allow/#&/' "$CHRONY_CONF"
fi

# Remove or comment out 'cmdallow' directives that permit remote chronyc access
if grep -qE "^\s*cmdallow" "$CHRONY_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    sed -i 's/^\s*cmdallow/#&/' "$CHRONY_CONF"
fi

# Comment out any 'bindaddress' for NTP (port 123) if it's set to listen on interfaces
# Note: bindcmdaddress for localhost is fine for local chronyc commands
if grep -qE "^\s*bindaddress\s" "$CHRONY_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    sed -i 's/^\s*bindaddress\s/#&/' "$CHRONY_CONF"
fi

# Ensure 'port 0' is set to disable NTP server functionality
# This prevents chrony from listening on UDP port 123
if ! directive_with_value_exists "port" "0"; then
    # Remove any existing port directive first
    sed -i '/^\s*port\s/d' "$CHRONY_CONF"
    # Add port 0 to disable NTP server listening
    echo "port 0" >> "$CHRONY_CONF"
fi

# Ensure 'cmdport 0' is set to disable command port listening on network
# This prevents remote chronyc connections (local socket still works)
if ! directive_with_value_exists "cmdport" "0"; then
    # Remove any existing cmdport directive first
    sed -i '/^\s*cmdport\s/d' "$CHRONY_CONF"
    # Add cmdport 0 to disable command port listening
    echo "cmdport 0" >> "$CHRONY_CONF"
fi

# Process any drop-in configuration files in /etc/chrony.d/
if [ -d "$CHRONY_CONF_DIR" ]; then
    for conf_file in "$CHRONY_CONF_DIR"/*.conf; do
        if [ -f "$conf_file" ]; then
            # Comment out allow directives
            if grep -qE "^\s*allow" "$conf_file" 2>/dev/null; then
                sed -i 's/^\s*allow/#&/' "$conf_file"
            fi
            # Comment out cmdallow directives
            if grep -qE "^\s*cmdallow" "$conf_file" 2>/dev/null; then
                sed -i 's/^\s*cmdallow/#&/' "$conf_file"
            fi
            # Comment out bindaddress directives
            if grep -qE "^\s*bindaddress\s" "$conf_file" 2>/dev/null; then
                sed -i 's/^\s*bindaddress\s/#&/' "$conf_file"
            fi
        fi
    done
fi

# Validate chrony configuration
if ! chronyd -p -f "$CHRONY_CONF" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "Warning: chrony configuration validation failed, checking syntax..."
    chronyd -p -f "$CHRONY_CONF" || true
fi

# Restart chronyd to apply changes
systemctl restart chronyd

# Ensure chronyd is enabled to start on boot
systemctl enable chronyd

# Verify the configuration is applied
echo "Chrony client-only configuration applied successfully."
echo "Verifying no listening ports for NTP service..."

# Give chronyd a moment to fully start
sleep 2

# Check that chronyd is not listening on port 123 (NTP) or 323 (chronyc command port)
if ss -ulnp | grep -E ':123\s|:323\s' | grep -q chronyd; then
    echo "WARNING: chronyd may still be listening on network ports"
    ss -ulnp | grep chronyd
    exit 1
else
    echo "SUCCESS: chronyd is configured in client-only mode (no listening ports)"
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

set -e

CHRONY_CONF="/etc/chrony.conf"
CHRONY_CONF_DIR="/etc/chrony.d"

# Function to check if a directive exists in chrony configuration
directive_exists() {
    local directive="$1"
    grep -qE "^\s*${directive}\s*$" "$CHRONY_CONF" 2>/dev/null || \
    ([ -d "$CHRONY_CONF_DIR" ] && grep -rqE "^\s*${directive}\s*$" "$CHRONY_CONF_DIR" 2>/dev/null)
}

# Function to check if a directive with value exists
directive_with_value_exists() {
    local directive="$1"
    local value="$2"

if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then

    exit 1

RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
RULES_FILE="${RULES_DIR}/30-access.rules"

mkdir -p "${RULES_DIR}"

# These rules monitor truncate and ftruncate syscalls for permission denied (EACCES=-13) 
# and operation not permitted (EPERM=-1) errors
# Only for users with auid >= 1000 and auid != unset (4294967295)

    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S truncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S truncate -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S truncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S ftruncate -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S ftruncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S ftruncate -F exit=-EACCES -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=access"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S ftruncate -F exit=-EPERM -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=access"
)

# Create or update the rules file
# First, read existing content if file exists
if [[ -f "${RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
    EXISTING_CONTENT=$(cat "${RULES_FILE}")
else
    EXISTING_CONTENT=""
fi

# Function to check if a rule exists in content
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local content="$2"
    # Normalize whitespace for comparison
    local normalized_rule=$(echo "$rule" | tr -s ' ')
    echo "$content" | while IFS= read -r line; do
        local normalized_line=$(echo "$line" | tr -s ' ')
        if [[ "$normalized_line" == "$normalized_rule" ]]; then
            return 0
        fi
    done
    grep -qF "$rule" <<< "$content"
}

# Build the new content
NEW_CONTENT="${EXISTING_CONTENT}"

for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
    # Check if rule already exists (idempotent check)
    if ! grep -qxF "$rule" "${RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
        if [[ -z "${NEW_CONTENT}" ]]; then
            NEW_CONTENT="${rule}"
        else
            NEW_CONTENT="${NEW_CONTENT}"$'\n'"${rule}"
        fi
    fi
done

# Write the rules file
echo "${NEW_CONTENT}" > "${RULES_FILE}"

# Set proper permissions
chmod 640 "${RULES_FILE}"
chown root:root "${RULES_FILE}"

# Ensure auditd service is enabled
systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null

# Load the rules into the running audit system
# Check if augenrules is being used (default on RHEL 8)
if grep -q "^USE_AUGENRULES" /etc/sysconfig/auditd 2>/dev/null; then
    USE_AUGENRULES=$(grep "^USE_AUGENRULES" /etc/sysconfig/auditd | cut -d'=' -f2 | tr -d '"' | tr -d ' ')
else
    # Default on RHEL 8 is to use augenrules
    USE_AUGENRULES="yes"
fi

# Load the rules
if [[ "${USE_AUGENRULES,,}" == "yes" ]]; then
    # Use augenrules to load
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null
else
    # Use auditctl to load directly
    auditctl -R "${RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null
fi

# Verify the rules are loaded
echo "Verifying audit rules are loaded..."
for rule in "${RULES[@]}"; do
    # Extract key components to verify
    if auditctl -l | grep -q "truncate.*exit=-13.*auid>=1000.*key=access" || \
       auditctl -l | grep -q "truncate.*exit=-1.*auid>=1000.*key=access" || \
       auditctl -l | grep -q "ftruncate.*exit=-13.*auid>=1000.*key=access" || \
       auditctl -l | grep -q "ftruncate.*exit=-1.*auid>=1000.*key=access"; then
        continue
    fi
done

echo "Audit rules for file truncation access monitoring have been configured."
echo "Rules file: ${RULES_FILE}"
echo ""
echo "Current loaded rules matching 'access' key:"
auditctl -l | grep "key=access" | grep -E "(truncate|ftruncate)"

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

set -e

SYSCTL_PARAM="kernel.unprivileged_bpf_disabled"
DESIRED_VALUE="1"
SYSCTL_CONF_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-unprivileged-bpf-disabled.conf"

# Apply the setting at runtime if not already set
current_value=$(sysctl -n "$SYSCTL_PARAM" 2>/dev/null || echo "0")
if [ "$current_value" != "$DESIRED_VALUE" ]; then
    sysctl -w "${SYSCTL_PARAM}=${DESIRED_VALUE}"
fi

# Ensure the setting persists across reboots
config_line="${SYSCTL_PARAM} = ${DESIRED_VALUE}"

if [ -f "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE" ]; then
    # Check if the correct setting already exists
    if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"; then
        # Update existing entry if different
        if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*${DESIRED_VALUE}[[:space:]]*$" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"; then
            sed -i "s/^[[:space:]]*${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=.*/${config_line}/" "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
        fi
    else
        # Append the setting
        echo "$config_line" >> "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
    fi
else
    # Create the file with the setting
    echo "$config_line" > "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
fi

# Set proper permissions on the config file
chmod 644 "$SYSCTL_CONF_FILE"
